title: Tab key double-fires in search mode causing mode switch conflict
description:
## Description
Tab key is handled by BOTH the active mode controller AND the shell in search mode, causing unpredictable behavior.

## Evidence
When tab is pressed in search mode:
1. The key is forwarded to search controller → calls cycleFocus(1) at internal/mode/search/model.go:132
2. The shell's tab handler at internal/app/model.go:258-270 also fires because CapturesShellKey does not capture tab
3. Result: single tab press both mutates search pane focus AND triggers mode switch

In board mode, tab is captured by the board controller (board/model.go:160) so the shell correctly short-circuits. Search mode does not have equivalent capture logic.

## Instructions
1. Have search mode's CapturesShellKey return true for tab key, matching board mode's behavior
2. Verify tab correctly cycles query→results→preview without switching modes
3. Add test coverage for this interaction

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Tab in search mode cycles pane focus without switching modes
- [ ] Tab in board mode continues to work as before (column navigation)
- [ ] Tab behavior is consistent: captured by active mode when mode uses it, falls through to shell only when mode doesn't
close_reason: Fixed search Tab interaction by making search mode capture Tab for shell routing; verified Tab cycles search panes without shell mode-switch conflicts and added controller test coverage.

title: Fix broken search mode UX and interaction model
description:
## Description
User feedback confirms search mode is currently not acceptable. The mode looks visually poor, does not feel like the Perles search workflow, and is effectively broken as an operator experience. Specific problems reported: there is an unwanted search-box style screen, the overall layout looks bad, switching into search with `2` is awkward compared to the expected Perles-style quick toggle, and returning cleanly to the dashboard is not obvious/reliable enough in practice.

## Evidence
- User feedback after direct runtime use
- Current app requires `2` for mode switch instead of expected quick search toggle flow
- Search screen visual design does not meet product quality expectations
- User reports search mode is “completely broken” and not working well at all

## Instructions
1. Rework search mode to feel like a professional operator workflow rather than a rough form screen.
2. Use Perles as a UX reference for how entering/searching/exiting search should feel, without copying architecture.
3. Fix the mode-switch interaction so entering and leaving search is smooth and predictable.
4. Ensure search results, filters, focus, and keyboard flow are all usable in real runtime.
5. Add/update automated coverage plus a full-app verification path for the search workflow.

## Files to Inspect
- `internal/mode/search/`
- `internal/ui/search/`
- `internal/app/model.go`
- related search/app tests and goldens

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Search mode is visually coherent and no longer presents as a broken search-box workflow
- [ ] Entering search and returning to board are smooth and discoverable in real use
- [ ] Search interaction works in runtime: query/filtering/focus/results/navigation
- [ ] Search mode feels materially closer to Perles UX expectations
- [ ] Automated tests and full-app verification cover the intended flow
close_reason: Search-mode UX fixes were implemented and validated: ctrl+space/esc workflow, stable no-flicker query/results layout, caret-like query affordance, and updated automated search/app coverage; full go test ./... passes.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Scope clarification from discussion: use Perles only as a UX reference donor for the full-text search workflow first. Priority is not to reproduce all of Perles search capabilities; priority is to make entering search, seeing results, and returning to board feel right and work reliably without the current ugly/broken mode-switch and form-heavy UX.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Implemented first-pass search-mode redesign around a Perles-inspired full-text workflow: ctrl+space mode toggle, esc back to board, query/results/preview split layout, simplified text-first controller, and updated renderer/controller/app tests. Focused search and app tests are being used as the immediate validation loop before broader quality gates.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Search redesign and broader UX polish are holding up under focused and broad validation. One remaining test failure during the full suite is in internal/mode/details/model_test.go because it asserted the old detail text prefix format; this is just a test expectation update caused by the deliberate detail presentation cleanup.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Additional direct runtime feedback: search still has three unacceptable UX problems after the first redesign pass. (1) The query field does not feel like a real text field because there is no visible cursor/caret. (2) The leading '/' in the query area feels wrong/confusing in this product and should be removed unless it serves a clearly justified purpose. (3) Typing causes visible flicker, which makes the interaction feel broken. Next step is to make the search box feel like a real focused input and keep the search layout stable while queries are running instead of flashing a loading screen.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Second search UX pass addresses the remaining obvious problems: removed the confusing leading slash from the visible query field, added a caret-like focused input affordance, and stopped swapping the whole screen to a loading view while typing so the layout stays stable and avoids flicker. Focused search/app tests are validating this pass.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Current code state is committed-ready: Perles-style ctrl+space toggle, stable text-first search layout, visible caret-like focused input, and reduced typing flicker via stable in-place loading behavior. Full go test suite passes, but the issue remains open for any further runtime polish discovered in direct use.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Session landed in git with commits c0352f6 and 9fe6b30 after search/detail/dashboard UX work. Note: session-close Pushing to Dolt remote... could not run because this repo currently has no configured Dolt remote; git push to origin/main is being completed separately.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## UX Review: Help text describes phantom search features

**What**: `shellKeyHelp()` in `internal/app/model.go` (lines 674-701) describes search filters that do not exist: `f` to cycle filter focus, status/type/priority cycling, Ready/Blocked toggles, and assignee filters. A user pressing `?` in search mode gets instructions for features that are not implemented. This is actively misleading — the only search capability is free-text query.

**Why**: This is a trust-killer. A user who reads the help and tries to use filters will think the app is broken. Help text that lies is worse than no help text. It also means the acceptance criteria "search mode is visually coherent and no longer presents as a broken search-box workflow" cannot be checked off — a core operator surface (help) is incoherent.

**Suggested action**: Strip the phantom filter descriptions from `shellKeyHelp()`. Only document what actually works: free-text query, tab to cycle panes, enter to select, esc to return. If filters are planned future work, that's a separate issue — help text must match current reality.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## UX Review: Tab key double-fires in search mode

**What**: When `tab` is pressed in search mode, the key message is forwarded to the search controller (which calls `cycleFocus(1)` at `internal/mode/search/model.go:132`), AND THEN the shell's own tab handler (`internal/app/model.go:258-270`) also fires because `CapturesShellKey` does not capture `tab`. This means a single tab press both mutates search pane focus AND triggers a mode switch. The user gets unpredictable behavior.

**Why**: This is a real interaction bug. The search mode was redesigned around 3-pane focus cycling with tab, but the shell still interprets tab as a mode-switch key. These two behaviors conflict. A user pressing tab in search to cycle from query → results → preview will instead get yanked out of search mode. This directly contradicts the "entering search and returning to board are smooth and discoverable" acceptance criterion.

**Suggested action**: Either (a) have search mode's `CapturesShellKey` return true for `tab` so the shell doesn't also handle it, or (b) change the pane cycling key in search to something that doesn't conflict with shell-level mode switching. Option (a) is simpler and more consistent — search mode owns tab when active.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## UX Review: Search mode has zero golden test coverage

**What**: Board mode has 8+ golden files across terminal widths (80, 120, 200 cols). Detail mode has 5 golden files (full and compact). Search mode — the most recently redesigned and most complex rendering surface (3-pane layout with query, results, and preview) — has zero golden files. Its tests use only `strings.Contains` assertions.

**Why**: Golden tests are the primary visual regression safety net in this project. Without them, any change to search rendering could silently break the layout and nobody would know until manual inspection. Given that search mode was just redesigned to fix visual problems, shipping it without golden coverage means the fix itself is unprotected. The acceptance criterion "search mode is visually coherent" is not verifiable by the test suite.

**Suggested action**: Add golden files for search mode at minimum: (1) results with a selected item showing preview, (2) empty results state, (3) narrow terminal width to test truncation. Follow the same pattern used by `internal/ui/board/board_test.go` and `internal/ui/details/details_test.go`.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## UX Review: Bug 925 is NOT done — three open issues remain

**What**: After full UX review of search mode in its current state, three distinct problems remain that prevent closing this bug: (1) help text describes non-existent filter features, (2) tab key double-fires causing unpredictable mode switching, (3) no golden test coverage for the search renderer. See individual comments on each.

**Why**: The acceptance criteria state "search mode is visually coherent and no longer presents as a broken search-box workflow" and "entering search and returning to board are smooth and discoverable." The tab double-fire directly breaks smooth entry/exit. The phantom help text breaks coherence. The missing goldens mean coherence can't be verified going forward. The visual layout itself IS improved — credit where due — but these three issues keep the bug open.

**Suggested action**: Address the three findings in this order: (1) fix tab double-handling first — it's an interaction bug users will hit immediately, (2) strip phantom help text — quick fix, high trust impact, (3) add golden tests — protects the investment already made in the redesign.

title: Make dashboard lanes full-height and full-screen like Perles
description:
## Description
Real runtime review against `pearles.png` and `our-tool.png` shows the current Beads Workbench dashboard still fails a core product requirement: the board lanes do not use the full terminal height like Perles. Instead of full-height, equal-height vertical lanes anchored to the bottom of the board area, the current UI renders short floating boxes near the top with large unused vertical space below. This makes the screen look unfinished and unprofessional.

## Evidence
- User-provided screenshots `pearles.png` and `our-tool.png`
- Real PTY runtime capture from current app shows 4 columns at the top and large unused vertical space below
- User explicitly requires a Perles-like full-height dashboard and does not accept the current look

## Instructions
1. Rework the board renderer and shell layout so the 4 dashboard lanes use the available board height, extending visually to the bottom of the board area.
2. Ensure the visible columns share the same rendered height in the main dashboard screen.
3. Remove the floating-card look; the dashboard should read as one coherent full-height board grid.
4. Keep the current 4-column grouping, compact rows, and no-large-sidebar layout unless a small adjustment is strictly required.
5. Add/update golden tests at representative heights and widths so lane height and bottom alignment are test-covered.
6. Re-check the real PTY/runtime against the user's Perles-style expectation.

## Files to Modify
- `internal/ui/board/board.go`
- `internal/ui/board/board_test.go`
- `internal/app/model.go`
- `internal/app/model_test.go`
- related board/app golden files

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] On representative terminal sizes, visible dashboard columns render with equal visual height
- [ ] The dashboard lanes extend to the bottom of the available board area instead of floating near the top
- [ ] The board reads as a coherent full-screen dashboard grid closer to Perles
- [ ] Updated golden/runtime tests verify lane height and bottom alignment behavior
- [ ] Real PTY/runtime review confirms the dashboard no longer has the floating-box look
close_reason: Accepted after automated verification and direct user visual confirmation of the runtime result

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Focused board/app snapshot reconciliation is now passing. Remaining focused failure is the board-mode density heuristic in internal/mode/board/model_test.go: the old maxChromeOverhead thresholds assume short floating-card lanes and now fail intentionally with the new full-height Perles-style columns. Next step is to recalibrate or replace that assertion so it validates dense rows without rejecting full-height lanes.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Updated internal/mode/board density coverage to validate compact one-line issue rows without penalizing intentional full-height lane padding. This keeps UX guardrails aligned with the Perles-style full-height dashboard requirement.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
Verification review performed for beads-workbench-12h.
- [PASS] Equal visual height on representative terminal sizes — tested via 'go test ./internal/ui/board', 'go test ./internal/mode/board -run TestBoardModeDashboardLayoutGoldensAcrossWidths -v', and reviewed board/app goldens at 80/120/180 cols. Observed equal-height bordered columns with shared bottom borders.
- [PASS] Lanes extend to the bottom of the available board area — reviewed internal/ui/board/board.go (fixed section height via FormSection Height) plus app/board goldens. Observed columns padded to full height instead of short top-floating cards.
- [PASS] Board reads as a coherent full-screen dashboard grid closer to Perles — reviewed embedded-fixture full-board and board→detail→board goldens/tests. Observed no sidebar chrome and consistent full-height multi-column grid.
- [PASS] Updated golden/runtime tests verify lane height and bottom alignment behavior — observed passing commands: 'go test ./internal/ui/styles ./internal/ui/board ./internal/mode/board ./internal/app', 'go build ./cmd/bwb', 'go vet ./...', 'go test ./...'.
- [UNVERIFIED] Real PTY/runtime review confirms the floating-box look is gone — available script/PTy evidence does not show the rendered alt-screen board, only terminal probes. This acceptance criterion remains unverified.
Result: BLOCKED pending better runtime evidence. Created follow-up bug beads-workbench-w4z to track the verification gap.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: User visually confirmed the dashboard now really looks better and the full-height lane change meets the intended improvement. This satisfies the remaining runtime-review acceptance need that automated PTY capture could not directly preserve.

title: Build multi-width dashboard UX test harness and runtime checks
description:
## Description
Before implementing the dashboard redesign, add the testing infrastructure needed to force real UX validation. The redesign tasks must not be able to pass on model-state tests alone; they must prove layout quality at representative terminal sizes and real runtime behavior.

## Instructions
1. Add parameterized dashboard rendering tests for representative terminal widths (at minimum 80, 120, and 180 columns).
2. Add helpers/goldens for full-board captures using realistic repo data and the embedded fixture when practical.
3. Add a board→detail→board round-trip test that verifies layout and focus behavior, not just state transitions.
4. Add simple density/layout assertions where appropriate (for example visible rows per column / max chrome overhead).
5. Document the expected UX verification workflow in tests or docs if needed.

## Files to Modify
- `internal/testing/ui/`
- `internal/app/*_test.go`
- `internal/mode/board/*_test.go`
- relevant golden files under `testdata/`
- `docs/TESTING.md` if test workflow needs documenting

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Dashboard layout is golden-tested at 80, 120, and 180 columns
- [ ] There is at least one realistic full-board rendering capture test
- [ ] There is a board→detail→board round-trip layout test
- [ ] Tests enforce actual render/layout behavior, not only model state
close_reason: Verified dashboard UX test harness and runtime checks

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Verifier PASS: dashboard layout is now golden-tested at 80/120/180 columns; there is a realistic embedded-fixture full-board rendering capture test; there is a board→detail→board round-trip layout test; and the harness enforces actual runtime render/layout behavior rather than model-state-only assertions.

title: Define triage-oriented dashboard grouping and startup focus behavior
description:
## Description
Define the default triage dashboard grouping and startup focus behavior so opening the app immediately shows a useful 4-column overview similar in usefulness to Perles. This task owns both the exact lane strategy and the deterministic startup focus needed for a stable initial dashboard.

## Explicit 4-Column Target
Implement this default 4-column dashboard in order:
1. **Not Ready** — work that is blocked or otherwise not ready to act on yet
2. **Ready** — actionable work ready to start now
3. **In Progress** — currently active work
4. **Done** — recently completed / recent finished work suitable for review context

These are the intended startup columns for the redesign effort. Do not substitute the old `Ready / In Progress / Blocked / Recent Work` grouping.

## Instructions
1. Implement the explicit 4-column default dashboard above in `internal/dashboard/`.
2. Use Perles as a UX benchmark only when choosing how to map those lanes to supported gateway query types.
3. Keep all grouping/query definitions backed by supported gateway query types only.
4. Fold startup focus stability work from `beads-workbench-3tm` into this task.
5. Ensure startup focus is deterministic and stable in dashboard order during async loads.
6. Add/update tests for dashboard definitions, query mapping, stable startup focus behavior, and rendered startup dashboard behavior using the new multi-width/runtime test infrastructure.
7. Include at least one runtime/render assertion for the startup dashboard shape, not only model-state assertions.

## Files to Modify
- `internal/dashboard/`
- `internal/mode/board/model.go`
- `internal/mode/board/model_test.go`
- `internal/app/model_test.go` if startup integration coverage changes
- related dashboard/board golden files if needed

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The default dashboard explicitly implements these 4 columns in order: Not Ready, Ready, In Progress, Done
- [ ] Grouping remains backed only by supported gateway query types
- [ ] Startup focus is deterministic and stable during async loads
- [ ] Automated tests cover grouping/query mapping and startup focus behavior
- [ ] Render/runtime-level checks verify the intended startup dashboard shape
close_reason: Verified 4-column triage grouping and startup focus behavior

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## "Triage-meaningful groups" is undefined — instructions say what to avoid but not what to build

**What**: Instruction #2 says "prefer triage-meaningful groups (for example readiness/state-based groupings) over mechanically exposing every existing queue." This gives an example category but doesn't specify the target columns. The user explicitly wants 4 Perles-style columns. The current code has Ready/In Progress/Blocked/Recent Work (+ optional Assigned to Me). Is that the right set? Should "Recent Work" survive? Should "Done" appear (as in Perles)?

**Why**: The implementer is told to "revisit" the grouping but has no product spec to aim for. They'll either keep the status quo (wasting the task) or pick something creative that the user rejects. This is the highest-risk ambiguity in the entire epic.

**Suggested action**: The task description must specify the target columns or explicitly state: "Confirm or change the default 4 columns, document the choice in a comment before implementing." At minimum, the acceptance criteria should require the implementer to justify their column choice in writing.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Startup focus acceptance criteria lack a concrete test shape

**What**: Criteria say "Startup focus is deterministic and stable during async loads" and "Automated tests cover grouping/query mapping and startup focus behavior." But the existing test in mode/board/model_test.go (TestBoardModeSelectsEarliestNonEmptyColumnAsSectionsLoad) only tests the model-state side — it simulates sectionLoadedMsg delivery order and checks focusedColumn. There's no test that validates the rendered output shows a stable selection indicator during the async load sequence, and no PTY/runtime test for startup focus.

**Why**: The bug beads-workbench-3tm is about visible selection jumps in a real terminal. A model-state test can pass while the rendered output still jumps — for example if the View() method or the app shell mishandles the transition. The criteria need to require a render-level or PTY-level check, not just "automated tests."

**Suggested action**: Add a criterion: "Golden test captures the board View() output at each sectionLoadedMsg delivery and confirms the selection indicator lands on the intended column." Ideally, also require a teatest-based startup sequence test that validates final output stability.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Task overlaps heavily with bug y3x — scope boundary is unclear

**What**: Bug y3x says "Revisit the default lane/group strategy; do not assume the current grouping is correct." Task 7qz says "Revisit the default lane/group strategy in internal/dashboard/ using Perles as a UX benchmark only." These are the same work. The task also covers startup focus, which overlaps with bug 3tm. Both the bug and this task touch internal/dashboard/ and internal/mode/board/model.go.

**Why**: An implementer claiming 7qz doesn't know whether y3x is separately assigned, already addressed by this task, or a prerequisite. Two people could work on conflicting changes to the same files. Or one person does the work and the other task/bug sits orphaned.

**Suggested action**: Either (a) explicitly close y3x as "folded into 7qz" before execution starts, with a comment on y3x pointing to 7qz, or (b) split the scope clearly: 7qz owns grouping/columns, y3x owns something else entirely. The current overlap is a coordination hazard.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Cannot execute yet: ticket is stale/ambiguous based on unresolved comments. (1) Target 4-column triage layout is not explicitly specified (unclear whether to keep Ready/In Progress/Blocked/Recent Work, include Done, etc.). (2) Startup focus acceptance criteria do not define required render/runtime test shape for async-load stability, so implementation could pass model-state tests while still flickering visually. (3) Scope overlap with y3x (and folded 3tm) is unresolved, so ownership/boundary is unclear and risks conflicting changes. Please update the task description/AC to codify the exact 4 columns, required startup-focus verification level, and relationship to overlapping work.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Verifier PASS: the default dashboard now explicitly implements the 4 columns in order Not Ready, Ready, In Progress, Done; all lanes are backed only by supported query types; startup focus is stable during async loads and settles by dashboard order; and render/runtime checks confirm the intended startup dashboard shape.

title: Acceptance Review: Dashboard UX redesign
description:
## Description
Verify that the dashboard redesign achieves a triage-quality browsing experience before feature work resumes.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All dashboard redesign tasks are closed
- [ ] The default board immediately presents a proper 4-column triage dashboard on app start
- [ ] The board uses the full terminal effectively and is materially closer to Perles-level triage usability
- [ ] The main board no longer uses the current large selected-issue sidebar
- [ ] Any selected summary on the main board is at most a short 2-3 line triage summary
- [ ] The board shows significantly more actionable work at once than the previous layout
- [ ] Grouping/lanes are useful for triage on realistic repo data
- [ ] Row metadata is compact and glanceable
- [ ] `go build ./cmd/bwb` passes
- [ ] `go vet ./...` passes
- [ ] `go test ./...` passes
- [ ] Real PTY/runtime review confirms the UI is good enough to resume feature work
close_reason: Acceptance review passed

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Acceptance review task lacks a layout validation methodology — "real PTY/runtime review" is not a test

**What**: Criterion: "Real PTY/runtime review confirms the UI is reviewable enough to resume feature work." This is the most important criterion in the entire epic, and it has no methodology. What does the reviewer run? What terminal size? What data? What do they compare against? The project's TESTING.md explicitly says "We do not rely on manual human terminal inspection to prove correctness."

**Why**: If the acceptance reviewer just eyeballs the output in their terminal, they're violating the project's own testing policy. If they run the existing teatest goldens, those only cover fixed fake data — not "realistic repo data." There's a gap between the project's stated testing philosophy and what this criterion actually asks for.

**Suggested action**: The acceptance review should require:
1. Running the embedded-fixture e2e smoke test and verifying the golden output matches at 80, 120, and 180+ column widths.
2. A new acceptance-specific golden test using the embedded fixture that captures the full startup board at representative sizes.
3. If manual PTY review is still needed, document it as an explicit exception with specific steps: "run bwb against the embedded fixture repo, verify at 120x34 that N issues are visible."

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Acceptance review depends on y3x but y3x scope is entirely covered by the three implementation tasks

**What**: Task 9oo depends on bug y3x. But y3x's instructions are: redesign grouping (→ covered by 7qz), rework row presentation (→ covered by u5s), rebalance detail pane (→ covered by j34), and validate with golden tests + PTY review (→ covered by all three tasks). Bug y3x is an umbrella bug whose every instruction is duplicated in a specific task.

**Why**: If y3x remains open as a dependency, the acceptance reviewer must verify it separately from the tasks — but there's nothing separate to verify. This creates confusion: does y3x need its own implementation pass? If not, it should be closed as "addressed by 7qz + u5s + j34" before the acceptance review.

**Suggested action**: Close y3x as "folded into epic child tasks" with a comment listing which task addresses which y3x finding. Then remove y3x as a dependency of 9oo (since its child tasks are already dependencies). This eliminates redundant verification work.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Acceptance criteria don't verify the board→detail→board round-trip UX

**What**: The user's core flow is: open app → see dashboard → select issue → press enter for detail → press esc to return to dashboard. The acceptance criteria check board density, grouping, row metadata, and balance — but no criterion verifies that entering and exiting detail mode works correctly and returns to the correct board position.

**Why**: The layout redesign (removing/reducing the sidebar per j34) fundamentally changes how detail mode is entered and exited. If the round-trip is broken or loses board position, the triage workflow is broken regardless of how good the dashboard looks.

**Suggested action**: Add an acceptance criterion: "Navigating from board to detail mode (enter) and back (esc) preserves the previously focused column and row. Verified by automated test."

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Verifier PASS: the dashboard redesign now delivers a proper 4-column startup dashboard, full-width triage-first browsing, no large main-screen selected sidebar, compact/glanceable row metadata, dense actionable board rendering, and passing build/vet/test/runtime checks. UI quality is now sufficient to resume feature work.

title: Rebalance board and detail pane for triage-first browsing
description:
## Description
Rebalance the browse screen so the main board remains the primary high-value surface. The current selected-issue sidebar is wrong for the main screen. Full issue detail belongs behind enter/detail mode; any selected summary shown on the main dashboard must be very short (2-3 lines max) or omitted entirely.

## Instructions
1. Rework the main board layout in `internal/app/model.go` so the board uses the full terminal effectively.
2. Remove the current large selected-issue sidebar from the main dashboard screen.
3. If selected-summary context remains on the main screen, keep it to a short triage-oriented 2-3 line summary only.
4. Preserve dedicated detail mode for full issue inspection.
5. Reduce chrome/header/footer overhead if it materially harms board density.
6. Add/update app-level golden tests and board→detail→board round-trip tests.
7. Re-check the browsing surface in a real PTY/runtime.

## Files to Modify
- `internal/app/model.go`
- `internal/app/model_test.go`
- `internal/ui/details/` if summary rendering needs adjustment
- related app/detail golden files

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The main board screen no longer shows the current large selected-issue sidebar
- [ ] The board uses the full terminal effectively as the primary browsing surface
- [ ] Any main-screen selected summary is at most a short 2-3 line triage summary
- [ ] Dedicated detail mode still supports fuller issue inspection
- [ ] Golden tests cover the new board/detail behavior and board→detail→board round-trip
- [ ] PTY/runtime review shows improved overall triage usefulness
close_reason: Verified triage-first board/detail balance

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Task j34 doesn't commit to removing the persistent sidebar — but the user's intent is clear

**What**: The current code in model.go shows a persistent detail sidebar consuming ~32% of width on terminals ≥110 cols (dualPaneMinWidth=110, browseBoardRatio=0.68). The user said: "The current selected-issue panel in the main board screen is wrong" and "Full detail belongs behind an explicit enter/detail action; any selected summary on the main screen should be very small (2-3 lines max) if it exists at all." Task j34 says "Reduce or simplify the persistent detail pane IF it harms board density" (emphasis mine — conditional language).

**Why**: The user is not asking for a conditional reduction. They're saying the sidebar is wrong. An implementer reading "if it harms board density" might conclude that a smaller sidebar is fine. That will fail the user's review.

**Suggested action**: Change the instruction from conditional ("if it harms board density") to directive: "Remove the persistent detail sidebar from browse mode. The board should occupy the full terminal width. If any selected-issue summary is shown, it must be at most 2-3 lines and must not reduce the board's column width." This aligns with the user's stated requirements.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Missing test for the "open detail from dashboard" user flow

**What**: The user said: "Full detail belongs behind an explicit enter/detail action." Task j34 must preserve the enter→detail flow after removing/reducing the sidebar. But j34's acceptance criteria say nothing about testing the board→detail transition. The existing test (TestModelBoardNavigationUpdatesShellSelectionAndDetailState) tests the current sidebar-based flow. If j34 removes the sidebar, that test needs to be updated, and a new golden test should verify the transition from full-width board to dedicated detail mode.

**Why**: The board→detail transition is the primary navigation pattern in the redesigned UX. If it breaks or looks wrong after the layout change, the whole redesign fails. There's no criterion that requires testing it.

**Suggested action**: Add an acceptance criterion: "Golden test captures the full board view (no sidebar) and the subsequent detail mode view after pressing enter, verifying both states at representative terminal sizes."

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Task j34 lists internal/ui/details/ in Files to Modify — but the real work is in internal/app/model.go layout logic

**What**: The heavy lifting for this task is in model.go's renderBody() function (lines 431-471), which contains the dual-pane layout logic, the browsePaneWidths() function, and the dualPaneMinWidth constant. The details/ package is a rendering component that probably doesn't change much — the layout decision lives entirely in the app shell. The task should emphasize that the core change is rewriting renderBody(), not tweaking the details renderer.

**Why**: If the implementer focuses on internal/ui/details/, they'll optimize the wrong thing. The sidebar exists because renderBody() always renders it when width ≥ 110. The fix is in renderBody()'s layout logic, not in the detail renderer.

**Suggested action**: Reorder/emphasize Files to Modify: primary changes are in internal/app/model.go (renderBody, browsePaneWidths, dualPaneMinWidth constants). internal/ui/details/ may need a compact-summary mode, but that's secondary.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Verifier PASS: the large selected-issue sidebar is gone from the main board screen; the board now uses the full browse surface; only a compact footer selection summary remains in browse mode; dedicated detail mode still provides full inspection via enter/detail; golden coverage and PTY board→detail→board checks all pass.

title: Redesign dashboard UX for triage-quality browsing
description:
## Description
Redesign the Beads Workbench dashboard and browsing surface so opening the app immediately shows a proper triage dashboard comparable in usefulness to Perles. The board must become the primary high-value surface, use the full terminal effectively, and stop relying on a large selected-issue sidebar in the main screen.

## Goals
- Opening the app shows a proper 4-column triage dashboard immediately
- The dashboard uses the full terminal size effectively
- The main board screen is Perles-like in overview usefulness, density, and scanability
- Any selected summary shown on the main board screen is at most a short 2-3 line summary; full detail lives behind enter/detail mode
- Validation includes golden tests, representative-width layout tests, PTY/runtime checks, and board→detail→board UX verification

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Child tasks complete
- [ ] Existing UI bugs are resolved or folded into task outcomes
- [ ] Acceptance review passes with real PTY/runtime validation
- [ ] The user can open the app and immediately see a proper dashboard without a large detail sidebar consuming the board
close_reason: Dashboard UX redesign completed and accepted

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
Planned execution scope under this epic:
- beads-workbench-7qz — Define triage-oriented dashboard grouping and startup focus behavior
- beads-workbench-u5s — Redesign board rows and metadata for compact scanability
- beads-workbench-j34 — Rebalance board and detail pane for triage-first browsing
- beads-workbench-y3x — Broad dashboard redesign bug folded into this epic
- beads-workbench-3tm — Startup focus stability bug folded into this epic
- beads-workbench-9oo — Acceptance Review: Dashboard UX redesign

Note: bd does not allow this epic to depend directly on tasks/bugs, so the relationship is documented here and enforced through the acceptance review task dependencies.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Epic scope does not address the user's #1 priority: 4-column Perles-style dashboard as the startup screen

**What**: The user explicitly stated that on opening the app they should see a "proper dashboard immediately" with "the 4 columns of the Perles-style dashboard." The epic's goals mention "make the board the primary high-value browsing surface" and "revisit default lane/group strategy" — but nothing in the epic or its children specifies what those 4 columns actually are or commits to a specific information architecture. The current built-in provider has Ready/In Progress/Blocked/Recent Work — is that the right 4? The epic doesn't say.

**Why**: An implementer reading this epic will redesign row density and pane balance without knowing whether they're building the right dashboard in the first place. The column definitions are the product decision — everything else (density, pane balance, row rendering) is styling on top. Getting the styling right on the wrong columns wastes the entire effort.

**Suggested action**: Add an explicit success criterion to the epic: "The default dashboard shows exactly N columns with [specific semantics], matching the user's stated Perles-style layout priorities." If the exact 4 columns aren't decided yet, make that a blocking decision before implementation starts — don't let the implementer guess.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## The detail pane requirement is ambiguously specified — "small summary" vs "no pane" vs "enter-only" needs a decision

**What**: The user said: "Full detail belongs behind an explicit enter/detail action; any selected summary on the main screen should be very small (2-3 lines max) if it exists at all." The epic says "rebalance board vs detail presentation." Task j34 says "reduce or simplify the persistent detail pane if it harms board density." None of these commit to one of the three possible designs: (A) no persistent pane at all on the main screen, (B) a 2-3 line inline summary beneath the board, or (C) a narrow sidebar. The current code allocates 32% width to a persistent detail sidebar (browseBoardRatio = 0.68). The user seems to want something radically different from that.

**Why**: The implementer for j34 will have to pick a design with no explicit product direction. If they pick (C) with a smaller sidebar, the user will likely reject it — the user said "if it exists at all." This ambiguity will cause rework.

**Suggested action**: Before j34 starts, the epic should commit to a concrete answer: Is the persistent detail sidebar eliminated, replaced with a 2-3 line inline snippet, or merely reduced? Encode that decision as a specific acceptance criterion.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Epic success criteria are subjective and un-checkable

**What**: The success criteria are: "Child tasks complete," "Existing UI bugs are resolved or folded," and "Acceptance review passes with real PTY/runtime validation." The first two are mechanical checks. The third defers all quality judgment to the acceptance review task (9oo), which itself has subjective criteria like "feels polished and intentional" and "materially closer to Perles-level triage usability."

**Why**: These criteria don't provide a measurable pass/fail bar. "Materially closer" and "feels polished" are impossible for an automated or independent reviewer to evaluate consistently. The acceptance reviewer will have to invent their own standards.

**Suggested action**: Add at least two concrete measurable criteria to the epic:
1. A specific issues-per-screen density target (e.g., "at 120x34, the default board shows at least N visible issue rows across all columns combined")
2. A specific layout assertion (e.g., "the board occupies at least 90% of terminal width when detail is not open," or "no persistent sidebar in browse mode")

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Recommended task execution order and simplification

**What**: The current plan has 3 implementation tasks + 2 bugs + 1 acceptance review, all with overlapping scope and no explicit ordering among the implementation tasks. Here's the recommended execution order and simplification:

**Execution order:**
1. **7qz first** (grouping + startup focus) — This defines WHAT the dashboard shows. Everything else is how it looks. Also subsumes bug 3tm entirely.
2. **j34 second** (remove sidebar, full-width board) — This defines the LAYOUT. Must happen before row density work because row density depends on knowing the available column width (full-width board vs sidebar-reduced board).
3. **u5s third** (row rendering) — This refines the PRESENTATION within the layout established by j34. Depends on knowing the Row struct needs Type/Status (discovered during j34/7qz work).
4. **9oo last** (acceptance) — Already correctly positioned.

**Simplification:** Close bugs y3x and 3tm as folded into the tasks. This reduces 6 items to 4 (3 tasks + 1 acceptance). Every finding in the bugs is explicitly covered by a task.

**Why**: Without explicit ordering, an implementer might start with u5s (row rendering), optimize for 2/3-width columns, then discover that j34 removes the sidebar and invalidates all their density work. The ordering prevents rework.

**Suggested action**: Document this order in the epic description or as a blocking dependency chain: 7qz → j34 → u5s → 9oo.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Missing: a testing infrastructure task for multi-width golden validation

**What**: Multiple tasks (u5s, j34, 9oo) require golden tests at representative terminal sizes and PTY/runtime validation. But the current test infrastructure doesn't have:
1. A parameterized golden test helper that runs the same board state at multiple widths (80, 120, 180)
2. An embedded-fixture-backed board golden test (the existing e2e test only checks string presence, not layout)
3. A density assertion helper (e.g., "count visible issue rows in rendered output")

The user explicitly said: "The implementation plan must ensure strong testing, including real runtime/render/layout validation — not only model-state tests."

**Why**: Without this infrastructure, each task implementer will either skip the multi-width/PTY tests (because there's no pattern to follow), or each will reinvent their own ad-hoc approach (creating inconsistent test quality). The user specifically called this out because they don't trust model-state-only tests.

**Suggested action**: Either (a) add a prerequisite task before u5s that builds the multi-width golden test harness and embedded-fixture board golden test, or (b) add this infrastructure work as an explicit instruction in u5s (since u5s is where golden tests are most heavily exercised). I recommend (a) — a small focused task that establishes the test pattern, so u5s/j34/9oo can all use it consistently.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## No task addresses the header/footer chrome that steals vertical space from the board

**What**: Looking at the current app model.go View(), the rendered output includes: a title line, a tab line, 1-2 help lines, a blank line, the body, a blank line, a footer with selection text + loading state + optional narrow-view hint. That's ~6-8 lines of chrome before and after the board. At 34 rows terminal height, that leaves only ~26 rows for the board — and the user wants maximum density.

The user said they want to "see a proper dashboard immediately" and use "the full terminal size effectively." None of the tasks address reducing the header/footer overhead.

**Why**: You can optimize row density all you want (u5s), but if 20% of vertical space is consumed by chrome, the dashboard still won't feel like Perles. This is a gap in the plan.

**Suggested action**: Either fold this into j34 (since j34 owns the overall layout balance) with an explicit instruction: "Review and minimize header/footer vertical footprint — the board body should get maximum vertical space," or create a small additional task for it.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
Reviewer-driven execution order:
1. beads-workbench-1ri — build multi-width dashboard UX test harness and runtime checks
2. beads-workbench-7qz — define explicit 4-column triage grouping and stable startup focus (folding beads-workbench-3tm)
3. beads-workbench-j34 — remove large main-screen selected sidebar and rebalance board/detail
4. beads-workbench-u5s — redesign rows/metadata for compact scanability
5. beads-workbench-9oo — acceptance review

User-priority constraints:
- opening the app must immediately show a proper 4-column dashboard
- the board should use the full terminal effectively
- full detail belongs behind enter/detail mode
- any main-screen selected summary must be at most a short 2-3 line summary

title: Redesign board rows and metadata for compact scanability
description:
## Description
Redesign board row rendering so important issue signals are glanceable at high density. The target is a compact triage row style closer to Perles, with substantially more visible work per screen than the previous two-line-row layout.

## Instructions
1. Redesign row rendering in `internal/ui/board/` for materially better scanability and density.
2. Include compact representations for type, priority, state, ID, and title in the rendered row format.
3. Update the board row data shape and mapping as needed so `internal/mode/board/model.go` supplies the required row fields to the UI renderer.
4. Improve issues-per-screen over the old two-line layout while preserving distinguishability on realistic IDs/titles.
5. Target a compact single-row format where practical; if a row expands beyond one line in normal board rendering, it must be justified by readability and still meet density goals.
6. Add/update golden tests at 80, 120, and 180 columns with realistic repo data.
7. Use the dashboard UX test harness from `beads-workbench-1ri`, including runtime/render assertions rather than only model-state assertions.
8. Perform a PTY/runtime verification pass and report what improved in the actual board rendering.

## Files to Modify
- `internal/ui/board/board.go`
- `internal/ui/board/board_test.go`
- `internal/ui/board/testdata/*`
- `internal/mode/board/model.go`
- `internal/mode/board/model_test.go`
- `internal/mode/board/testdata/*` if mode goldens change

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Normal board rows render in a compact single-line form at representative widths unless a justified exception is documented in tests
- [ ] The new layout shows more visible issues per viewport than the previous two-line-row design at the same representative widths
- [ ] Type/priority/state/ID/title signals are compact and glanceable in the rendered row format
- [ ] Long realistic IDs/titles remain distinguishable without destroying density
- [ ] Golden tests cover dense board rendering at 80, 120, and 180 columns
- [ ] Runtime/render-level tests verify the new row presentation, not only model state
- [ ] PTY/runtime review shows improved scanability on realistic repo data
close_reason: Verified dense board row and metadata redesign

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Row struct is missing type and status fields — the user wants type/priority/state/ID/title in a compact row

**What**: The user and bug y3x both say: "Perles rows encode issue type, priority, readiness state, and ID in a compact, scannable prefix." The current Row struct is `{ID, Title, Priority, Selected}`. There is no Type field and no Status field. The renderRowLines function only renders title + ID + priority. The mode/board model.go maps issues to rows and only passes ID/Title/Priority/Selected.

**Why**: An implementer following u5s instruction #2 ("Make type/priority/state/ID/title signals compact") will discover they can't render type or status because the data isn't in the Row struct. They'll need to change the Row struct, update the mode-to-renderer mapping in mode/board/model.go, and update all existing golden files. This isn't mentioned in the task's "Files to Modify" or instructions.

**Suggested action**: Add explicit instructions: "Extend the ui/board.Row struct to include Type and Status fields. Update the board mode model's View() mapping to populate them. This is a prerequisite for rendering the compact metadata prefix." Also add internal/mode/board/model.go to the "Files to Modify" list.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## "Materially denser" is not a testable criterion — needs a measurable density target

**What**: Acceptance criteria #1 says "Row rendering is materially denser than the current two-line-row design." The current design uses exactly 2 lines per row (title line + meta line). The golden files confirm this. "Materially denser" could mean 1 line per row, 1.5 lines, or just slightly shorter padding. There's no target.

**Why**: An implementer could achieve "materially denser" by reducing padding by 1 character and claim success. Or they could achieve a compact single-line row that truncates too aggressively. Without a target, the acceptance reviewer has to make a subjective call.

**Suggested action**: Replace "materially denser" with a concrete target: "At 120 columns wide with 4 visible sections, the board renders at least N issue rows per column in a 30-line viewport." Even a rough target (e.g., "each row occupies at most 1 rendered line") is better than a subjective adjective.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Golden tests need terminal-size-parameterized cases, not just one width

**What**: Criteria #4 says "Golden tests cover representative dense board rendering cases." The current board golden tests only cover two widths: 100 and 120. There are no tests at common narrow widths (80 cols) or very wide widths (160+, 200+). The user cares about "full terminal size" usage, which varies wildly.

**Why**: A density improvement that works at 120 cols might break horribly at 80 (truncation destroys readability) or look wrong at 200 (wasted space). Golden tests at a single width give false confidence about the redesign.

**Suggested action**: Require golden test coverage at minimum 3 representative widths: 80 (narrow), 120 (standard), and 180+ (ultrawide). Each golden should show the full board state including column windowing behavior. The TESTING.md already supports this pattern — it just needs to be exercised.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## PTY/runtime check is listed as a criterion but there's no test pattern for it — implementer will skip it

**What**: Criteria #5 says "PTY/runtime review shows improved scanability on realistic repo data." But there's no existing test or helper that does a PTY-based render check against embedded fixture data for the board specifically. The existing teatest usage in mode/board/model_test.go runs a teatest program but only checks for substring presence, not layout or density. The app-level e2e test (TestModelEmbeddedFixtureBoardToDetailSmokeWorkflow) checks string presence but doesn't validate layout.

**Why**: Without a concrete test pattern or helper, "PTY/runtime review" will be interpreted as "I looked at it in my terminal and it seemed fine" — which is exactly the manual inspection that TESTING.md says the project does not rely on ("Product verification must be automated").

**Suggested action**: Either (a) require a new teatest-based golden test using embedded fixture data that captures the full board output at the target terminal size, or (b) explicitly document that this one criterion is a manual-review exception (and explain why the automated golden tests are insufficient). Option (a) is strongly preferred given TESTING.md policy.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Cannot execute yet: ticket is stale/underspecified relative to its own comments. Missing explicit prerequisite to add Type and Status to ui/board.Row and populate them from mode/board mapping (likely requires internal/mode/board/model.go changes beyond listed files). Acceptance criterion 'materially denser' is not measurable/testable (needs concrete density target, e.g., max 1 rendered line per row and/or rows-per-viewport target at 120 cols). PTY/runtime criterion is not operationalized against project policy preferring automated verification (needs required teatest/golden runtime pattern or explicit documented manual exception). Please fold these decisions into description/instructions/AC before implementation.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Verifier FAIL: compact single-line rows are in place and scanability is improved, but wide app layout still truncates titles too aggressively in runtime board rendering (for example only 'Implement crea...' / 'Implement launc...' at 160 cols). Follow-up required: relax wide-board column packing or allocate more title width so realistic titles remain more readable while preserving density.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Verifier PASS: board rows now render as compact single-line entries across representative widths; density is higher than the old two-line design; type/priority/state/ID/title signals are glanceable; wide-layout title readability was rebalanced without regressing density; golden/runtime tests at 80/120/180 plus app-level render checks all pass.

title: Redesign dashboard information architecture and board density to match usable Perles-style triage
description:
## Description
User-provided screenshots comparing Perles (`pearles.png`) with the current Beads Workbench dashboard (`our-tool.png`) confirm that the current UI is still not close to a proper triage-quality board. The latest screenshots make the remaining problems clearer than earlier terminal captures: Perles achieves much higher information density, clearer lane semantics, and better scanability, while Beads Workbench still feels sparse, over-expanded, and structurally less useful for day-to-day triage.

## Screenshot-Specific Findings
1. **Rows are still too tall and too sparse**
   - Perles fits significantly more actionable items per column.
   - Beads Workbench uses two-line rows everywhere, which dramatically reduces visible issue count.

2. **The detail pane is consuming too much value relative to its cost**
   - In Beads Workbench, the selected-issue pane occupies a large area but mostly shows long wrapped issue body text.
   - In Perles, the board itself remains the star of the screen and supports fast triage.

3. **Queue/group semantics are weaker than Perles**
   - Perles uses triage-meaningful groupings (`Not Ready`, `Ready`, `In Progress`, `Done`) that map well to decision-making.
   - Beads Workbench's current `Ready / In Progress / Blocked / Recent Work` layout is less immediately useful and less balanced visually.

4. **Metadata presentation is less glanceable**
   - Perles rows encode issue type, priority, readiness state, and ID in a compact, scannable prefix.
   - Beads Workbench still requires more visual effort to parse row meaning.

5. **Overall information architecture still feels like a shell around data, not a polished dashboard**
   - The current screen is improved over earlier iterations but still far from the clarity and confidence of the Perles dashboard.

## Instructions
1. Redesign the default board/dashboard so it is optimized for triage density and glanceability, using Perles as a UX benchmark only.
2. Revisit the default lane/group strategy; do not assume the current grouping is correct.
3. Rework row presentation so key status/type/priority/ID/title signals are readable in a compact single-row or otherwise denser form.
4. Rebalance or reduce the persistent detail pane if it harms board density; the board view itself must become the primary high-value surface.
5. Validate changes with updated golden tests plus real PTY/runtime review against realistic repo data and screenshot comparison.

## Files To Modify
- `internal/dashboard/`
- `internal/ui/board/`
- `internal/app/model.go`
- related board/app tests and goldens

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The default dashboard is materially closer to Perles-level triage usability
- [ ] The board shows significantly more actionable work at once than the current two-line-row layout
- [ ] Row metadata is compact and glanceable enough for fast scanning
- [ ] The board/detail balance improves overall triage usefulness rather than consuming space on low-value detail text
- [ ] PTY/runtime review on realistic repo data shows a polished, reviewable dashboard experience
- [ ] Updated golden/render tests lock in the improved dashboard information architecture and presentation
close_reason: Folded into completed dashboard redesign tasks and acceptance review

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## This bug is entirely redundant with the epic's implementation tasks — should be closed as folded

**What**: Every instruction in y3x maps 1:1 to an implementation task:
- "Redesign default board/dashboard for triage density" → task 7qz
- "Revisit default lane/group strategy" → task 7qz
- "Rework row presentation for compact signals" → task u5s
- "Rebalance or reduce persistent detail pane" → task j34
- "Validate with golden tests plus real PTY/runtime review" → all tasks + 9oo

The acceptance criteria are also duplicates of the corresponding task criteria.

**Why**: Leaving this bug open creates confusion about ownership. An implementer might think y3x requires separate work beyond the three tasks. The acceptance reviewer (9oo) depends on y3x and must verify it — but there's nothing to verify that isn't already covered by 7qz + u5s + j34.

**Suggested action**: Close y3x with a comment: "All findings addressed by epic child tasks: 7qz (grouping), u5s (row density), j34 (pane balance). Verification via 9oo acceptance review." Remove y3x as a dependency of 9oo.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: All findings from this broad redesign bug were addressed by the dashboard UX redesign effort: beads-workbench-7qz (4-column grouping + startup focus), beads-workbench-j34 (board-first layout and sidebar removal), beads-workbench-u5s (dense compact row rendering), with runtime/render verification established by beads-workbench-1ri and acceptance review in beads-workbench-9oo.

title: Improve board shell information density and detail visibility
description:
## Description
The current wide-terminal browsing screen is structurally broken, not merely under-polished. The board/dashboard runtime renders all built-in sections side-by-side with no responsive column limiting, which produces unreadable narrow columns and hides useful selected-issue context. The resulting screen feels like a debug skeleton rather than a reviewable product surface.

## Evidence
- User screenshot review (`img.png`)
- Runtime PTY exploration showed severe truncation (`beads-workbench-...` rows), weak selected-issue visibility, and ineffective use of wide-terminal space
- Verifier confirmed that on a wide terminal the expected browse/detail split is not product-acceptable and selected issue context is reduced mostly to footer text

## Root Causes To Address
1. Board column count does not adapt to available width.
2. Board columns can render far too narrow to keep issue IDs/titles readable.
3. The browse/detail split does not sufficiently protect a visible detail preview during board browsing.
4. Board height/layout leaves excessive dead space while failing to surface useful issue context.

## Instructions
1. Make the board layout responsive to terminal width instead of rendering all sections side-by-side unconditionally.
2. Ensure visible board columns remain readable; if necessary, limit simultaneously visible sections and make others navigable.
3. Ensure the wide browse screen shows a clearly visible selected-issue detail/context preview during board browsing.
4. Reduce border/chrome overhead and dead space so more terminal area is devoted to issue content.
5. Update golden/render tests at representative wide-terminal sizes and re-check the real runtime in a PTY after the fix.

## Files to Modify
- `internal/app/model.go`
- `internal/ui/board/`
- `internal/ui/details/`
- related app/board/detail golden tests

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] At a representative wide terminal size, the board does not render all sections as unreadably narrow columns
- [ ] Visible board columns preserve enough width to distinguish issue IDs and titles without near-total truncation
- [ ] The selected issue detail/context is visibly present in the main browsing screen, not only in footer text
- [ ] Dead space/chrome is materially reduced compared with the current browsing screenshot/runtime
- [ ] Updated golden/render tests cover the improved wide-terminal presentation
- [ ] Real PTY/runtime re-check shows the browsing screen is reviewable as a product surface
close_reason: Verified browsing layout and readability fix

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Priority raised by user review: do not continue feature expansion until the broken browsing layout is fixed and verified in the real runtime. Quality first; use real runtime rendering checks in addition to automated tests.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Bug description understates the severity — screenshot shows a fundamentally broken board layout

**What**: The description says "leaves large amounts of empty space" and "does not make the selected issue's detail context sufficiently visible." The actual screenshot (img.png) shows a worse problem: 5 dashboard columns (Ready, In Progress, Blocked, Recent Work, Assigned to Me) are crammed horizontally into a wide terminal with text truncated to near-unreadable widths. The columns are visually undifferentiated — you cannot tell what column is focused because the text is too small to read. The detail pane on the right is invisible in this layout because the board's left pane (at 56% width per model.go line 376) dedicates all its space to 5 tiny columns. Additionally, the board columns have no height constraint — columns with fewer issues leave huge dead space below them while columns with more issues get no scrolling.

**Why**: The current description will lead an implementer to tune padding and spacing. The actual problem is structural: the board renders N columns side-by-side regardless of terminal width, and 5 columns at any reasonable terminal width produces unreadable content. The fix requires layout logic changes (e.g., column limiting, horizontal scrolling, or responsive column count), not just cosmetic spacing adjustments.

**Suggested action**: Rewrite instructions to be specific about the structural layout problems:
1. Board column count must adapt to available width — 5 side-by-side columns is never readable
2. Column width must have a minimum that keeps issue IDs and titles readable (at least ~30 chars inner width)
3. The detail pane split ratio (currently hardcoded 56/44 at line 376) needs to account for the number of board columns
4. Columns need height management — either max-height with scroll or truncation with a count indicator

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Acceptance criteria are too subjective to verify

**What**: All 5 criteria use qualitative language: "uses screen space effectively," "clearly visible," "materially reduced," "reviewable as a product surface." None of these have a measurable threshold. What does "effectively" mean? What does "clearly visible" mean for a detail pane?

**Why**: A tasker will implement something they think looks good; a verifier will reject it based on different subjective standards. This will cause rework cycles. The UX bugs are the hardest to close precisely because "good enough" is undefined.

**Suggested action**: Add concrete verifiable criteria, for example:
- At 120-col terminal width, board columns show at least 25 characters of issue title per row
- At 120-col terminal width, no more than 3 board columns render simultaneously (others are navigable)
- Selected issue detail pane shows at minimum: title, ID, status, priority, type, assignee, description first paragraph
- Golden tests capture the improved layout at 120x34 and 200x50 terminal sizes
- No board column renders narrower than 30 characters inner width

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Missing instruction: the 5-column default dashboard definition is itself a problem

**What**: The built-in dashboard (internal/dashboard/builtin_provider.go) defines 4 columns by default (Ready, In Progress, Blocked, Recent Work) plus a conditional 5th column (Assigned to Me) when BEADS_ACTOR is set. The board renderer (internal/ui/board/board.go) distributes available width evenly across ALL columns with no maximum column count. At the standard 120-col width, 5 columns get ~20 chars each inner width — too narrow for "beads-workbench-yze.4.3 · P1 · Implement launcher framework..." to be readable.

**Why**: The bug instructions say "improve the board shell layout" but don't mention the dashboard definition or column count as root causes. An implementer might try to make 5 narrow columns look better (impossible) instead of addressing the root cause: either limit visible columns, or reduce the default dashboard to 3 columns.

**Suggested action**: Add an instruction to either:
(a) Limit visible board columns to fit terminal width (e.g., max 3 at 120 cols, with h/l scrolling through column groups), OR
(b) Reduce the default dashboard to 3 sections (Ready, In Progress, Blocked) as the v1 default, deferring Recent Work and Assigned to Me to a second dashboard or tab

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Verifier FAIL after first pass: responsive column windowing and visible selected-issue pane are now in place, but the browsing screen is still not product-reviewable. Remaining gaps: (1) board row formatting still lets long issue IDs consume nearly all row width, hiding too much title text on real data, and (2) the header/help text still truncates/hurts presentation around 120 columns. Keep this bug open and fix these runtime readability issues before sign-off.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Verifier PASS after second readability pass: the board now uses a responsive visible-column window, rows prioritize readable title text with compact metadata, the main browsing screen shows a visible selected-issue pane, header help adapts to representative widths, golden/render coverage was updated, and PTY/runtime re-checks at wide sizes now show a reviewable product surface.

title: Launcher action is not wired into app runtime path
description:
## Description
The launcher framework exists under `internal/launcher/`, but the active app runtime path does not invoke it. The shell's launcher entrypoint still routes through `services.Editor.EditIssue()` instead of `services.Launcher.Launch()`, so launcher definitions are unreachable from issue context in the running app. This also causes the launcher-focused app tests to hang because they expect the fake launcher seam to be called but the runtime tries to open a real editor instead.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Pressing the launcher entrypoint in the shell invokes `launcher.Service` with the selected issue context
- [ ] Launcher failures surface clear UI feedback from `launcher.Service`
- [ ] `TestModelLaunchEditorActionUsesLauncherService` passes without timeout
- [ ] `TestModelLaunchEditorActionShowsErrorToastWhenLauncherFails` passes without timeout
- [ ] Launcher-focused app tests do not require a real interactive editor process
acceptance_criteria:
- Pressing the launcher entrypoint in the shell invokes launcher.Service with the selected issue detail or equivalent issue context
- The app shows launcher errors from launcher.Service
- The existing launcher-focused app tests pass without requiring a real interactive editor
close_reason: Verified launcher runtime wiring fix

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Bug description is accurate and verified — e key calls services.Editor not services.Launcher

**What**: Confirmed by code inspection. model.go line 272 calls editIssueCmd which at line 586 calls services.Editor.EditIssue(). The services.Launcher field is constructed in services.go but never called from the runtime path. The test TestModelLaunchEditorActionUsesLauncherService (model_test.go line 237) injects a FakeLauncher and expects it to be called, but the runtime calls services.Editor instead — causing the test to hang for 30s then panic (confirmed: go test ./internal/app/... times out on this test because ProcessOpener.Open spawns a real editor subprocess).

**Why**: This means the entire launcher framework (internal/launcher/service.go with interpolation, definitions, process runner) is dead code from the app runtime's perspective. The "e" key bypasses it entirely.

**Suggested action**: The description and acceptance criteria are adequate for implementation. One addition: the criteria should explicitly state that TestModelLaunchEditorActionUsesLauncherService must pass without timing out, since this test currently hangs (it's the concrete proof the bug exists).

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## This bug blocks all internal/app tests — severity is higher than P1

**What**: TestModelLaunchEditorActionUsesLauncherService hangs for 30s then panics because the code path spawns a real editor process. Since go test runs all tests in a package, this means "go test ./internal/app/..." is currently broken — it will always timeout. This blocks verification of ANY app-level fix including w53 (layout), ybx (detail reload), and any new app tests.

**Why**: A P1 bug that blocks all testing in the core package is effectively P0. You cannot verify fixes to other bugs until this test either passes or is skipped. The bug description does not mention this cascading impact.

**Suggested action**: Either raise to P0, or at minimum add a note that this must be fixed before any other app-level bug can be verified through tests. The test either needs to be fixed (wire to launcher) or temporarily skipped with a clear TODO pointing to this bug.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## 9t8 is also responsible for a second hanging test — TestModelLaunchEditorActionShowsErrorToastWhenLauncherFails

**What**: There are two tests that hang due to this bug, not one: TestModelLaunchEditorActionUsesLauncherService (line 237) and TestModelLaunchEditorActionShowsErrorToastWhenLauncherFails (line 273). Both inject FakeLauncher but the runtime calls services.Editor (backed by a real ProcessOpener), causing both to spawn a real editor process and hang. The error-injection test at line 280 sets FakeLauncher.Err but since the code never calls the launcher, the error is never triggered.

**Why**: The acceptance criteria say "existing launcher-focused app tests pass" — this should explicitly name both tests, since both are broken.

**Suggested action**: Add both test names to the acceptance criteria: TestModelLaunchEditorActionUsesLauncherService AND TestModelLaunchEditorActionShowsErrorToastWhenLauncherFails must pass without timeout.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Verifier PASS: the shell launcher entrypoint now routes through services.Launcher with selected issue context, launcher failures surface toast feedback, and the launcher-focused app tests pass quickly without requiring a real interactive editor process.

title: Dependencies pane navigation is asymmetric across group boundaries
description:
Discovered from live user review of the current detail UI on `beads-workbench-t7o.2`. Expected: dependency selection should traverse grouped rows in a predictable linear order, so moving down from the last row of one non-empty group should enter the first row of the next non-empty group, and moving up should behave symmetrically in reverse. Actual: in the Dependencies pane for `beads-workbench-t7o.2`, when selection is in `Blocks (2)`, pressing Down on the last Blocks row does not advance into the `Structure (5)` list below it. However, pressing Up from the top of the Blocks list can move into the Structure list, so traversal is asymmetric and confusing. Repro: 1) `go build -o /tmp/bwb ./cmd/bwb` 2) run `BD_NON_INTERACTIVE=1 /tmp/bwb` from the repo root 3) navigate to `beads-workbench-t7o.2` and open detail mode 4) focus Dependencies with `LEFT` 5) move selection within `Blocks (2)` to the second row 6) press Down and observe selection does not enter `Structure (5)` 7) from the top Blocks row, press Up and observe selection can move into Structure. Impact: dependency browsing feels broken because list traversal order is not intuitive or consistent across group boundaries.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Dependency selection traverses non-empty grouped dependency rows in a consistent linear order across `Blocked by`, `Blocks`, `Related`, and `Structure`.
- [ ] Pressing Down on the last row of a group moves to the first selectable row of the next non-empty group.
- [ ] Pressing Up on the first row of a group moves to the last selectable row of the previous non-empty group.
- [ ] Real-app verification on `beads-workbench-t7o.2` confirms symmetric traversal between `Blocks` and `Structure`.
close_reason: Fixed dependency navigation list to match rendered group row order (Blocked by -> Blocks -> Related -> Structure), added focused traversal tests, and verified symmetric Blocks/Structure movement on beads-workbench-t7o.2 in runtime UI capture.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Root cause confirmed: moveRelatedSelection (model.go:309-324) navigates a flat BrowserItems list, but the rendering in dependencyGroups (details.go:273-283) includes Structure items via browserItems only when ParentGroupBrowser is present. The flat list is built by browserItemsFromDependencies (model.go:436-465) which concatenates BlockedBy+Blocks+Related but does NOT include Structure/browserItems. So when ParentGroupBrowser exists, browserItemsFromParentGroup builds a completely different list (parent+children), and BrowserItems is that structure list — meaning dependency-group rows (BlockedBy/Blocks/Related) shown in the rendering are NOT in the navigation list at all. The asymmetric behavior comes from the mismatch between what renderRelationshipGroups renders vs what BrowserItems contains. Fix: BrowserItems must be rebuilt as a flat ordered list matching the exact rendered row order across ALL visible non-empty groups (BlockedBy → Blocks → Related → Structure), so moveRelatedSelection traverses them linearly. syncBrowserPanel needs to unify both paths.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Coupling note: 4k8p and 3ymu are independent. 4k8p is purely rendering (metadata.go + details.go renderMetadataPaneLines). 3ymu is purely navigation model (model.go syncBrowserPanel/browserItemsFromDependencies). No shared code paths. Recommended order: fix 3ymu first — it's a functional navigation bug (users cannot reach items). Then 4k8p — it's cosmetic polish. Both are small (< 30 lines changed each).

title: Metadata pane uses redundant editable banner and non-shared selection styling
description:
Discovered from live user review of the current detail UI after the earlier metadata-interaction follow-up was shipped. Expected: when the Metadata pane is focused, the pane border already communicates focus, so metadata field selection should use the same shared selected-row/cursor treatment as Dependencies/dashboard (same alignment and same blue cursor/selection style) without adding a redundant chevron banner. Actual: in detail mode on `beads-workbench-t7o.2`, focusing Metadata shows a top line `› Editable: Status, Priority`, which duplicates the pane-focus signal and introduces an extra chevron that does not match the rest of the UI. The selected metadata field is also rendered with an inline `›` prefix (`› Priority: P1` / `› Status ...`) rather than with the same shared selected-row treatment used in Dependencies/dashboard, so text alignment shifts and the selection affordance feels inconsistent. Repro: 1) `go build -o /tmp/bwb ./cmd/bwb` 2) run `BD_NON_INTERACTIVE=1 /tmp/bwb` from the repo root 3) open `beads-workbench-t7o.2` in detail mode (`3`) 4) focus Metadata with `RIGHT` 5) observe the `› Editable: Status, Priority` banner and inline-chevron selection styling while moving between Status/Priority. Impact: metadata interaction works functionally but feels visually inconsistent and less polished than the rest of the selection system.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The redundant `› Editable: Status, Priority` banner is removed or replaced with a non-chevron affordance that does not compete with pane focus.
- [ ] Metadata field selection uses the same shared selection/cursor component style as Dependencies/dashboard, including consistent color treatment and stable text alignment.
- [ ] Moving between Status and Priority does not shift row alignment with ad-hoc inline prefixes.
- [ ] Real-app review confirms Metadata selection feels visually consistent with the rest of the app.
close_reason: Removed metadata editable banner and switched metadata selection to shared styled row indicator with stable alignment for editable fields; verified via focused tests and runtime UI capture.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Root cause confirmed: renderMetadataPaneLines (details.go:354-357) unconditionally prepends '› Editable: Status, Priority' when focused=true, and renderMetadataRail (metadata.go:63-64) prefixes selected fields with '› ' inline. Both are ad-hoc — no shared selection component is used. Fix: (1) Delete lines 354-357 (the banner). (2) Replace the inline '› ' prefix in metadata.go:63-64 with the same issuerow.RenderReferenceCompact or shared selected-row style used in the dependencies pane — this means adding a padding/indent to ALL metadata rows and using a highlight color on the selected one, so alignment stays stable. Implementation is self-contained in these two files.

title: relates-to dependents still render under Blocks in detail view
description:
Discovered during verification of beads-workbench-x2u in the built binary against the embedded fixture repo. Expected: after running `bd dep relate <issue-a> <issue-b>`, the linked issue appears only under Related in detail view.
