Actual: `bd show --json` includes the relates-to issue in both dependencies and dependents with `dependency_type=relates-to`; the gateway normalization was fixed on the dependency side but the dependents path still maps entries straight into Blocks, so the live app shows the same issue under Blocks and Related.

## Root Cause
Gateway handling for dependents still treats all dependent refs as Blocks. A `relates-to` dependent must be classified as Related, not Blocks.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Gateway/detail normalization classifies `relates-to` dependents as Related instead of Blocks.
- [ ] Unit tests cover the dependent-side `relates-to` case.
- [ ] In the real app, an issue whose dependents include a `relates-to` entry shows that issue only under Related, not under Blocks.
close_reason: Fixed dependent-side normalization: relates-to dependents now map to Related (not Blocks); added gateway + embedded fixture coverage and verified targeted tests.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Root cause confirmed at read_gateway.go:152. What: referencesFromPayload(primary.Dependents) dumps all dependents into Blocks without checking dependency_type. The x2u fix added relates-to filtering only on the dependencies side (line 543), not the dependents side. A bd dep relate A B produces relates-to entries in both A.dependencies and B.dependents — the dependencies side now correctly routes to Related, but the dependents side still falls through to Blocks. Why: this means every relates-to link appears under both Related AND Blocks, which is the exact symptom described. Suggested action: replace referencesFromPayload for dependents with a type-aware function (like dependencyReferencesFromPayload but for the dependent direction) that routes relates-to dependents to Related and parent-child dependents to nowhere (they are handled by parent-group). This is the mirror of the x2u fix.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Missing acceptance criterion. What: no criterion explicitly states that dependents with dependency_type relates-to must NOT appear in Blocks. Why: a tasker could fix the Related rendering without removing the duplicate from Blocks, leaving the bug half-fixed. Suggested action: add criterion: 'An issue whose dependents include a relates-to entry must show that entry only under Related, not under Blocks.'

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Ordering relative to 74r/6eq. What: 6sk is a functional correctness bug in the gateway layer (read_gateway.go). 74r/6eq are cosmetic layout bugs in details.go. Why: 6sk changes what data flows into Blocks vs Related, which affects row counts in the Dependencies pane. If 74r width tuning is done first, the visible result will shift when 6sk removes phantom Blocks entries. No code conflict — 6sk touches read_gateway.go, 74r/6eq touch details.go — but visual verification would need redoing. Suggested action: fix 6sk before 74r/6eq. 6sk and yxjx are independent (gateway vs UI) and can run in parallel.

title: Dependencies pane selection ignores blocked/related rows
description:
Discovered during verification of beads-workbench-59z. Expected: in detail-mode Dependencies, Blocked by / Blocks / Related rows visibly use the shared selected-row treatment, up/down moves that visible selection, and Enter opens the selected dependency issue. Actual: the live app only derives dependency-pane selection from BrowserItems/Structure rows. On an issue with normal dependency refs but no parent-group structure (for example the embedded fixture issue bwf-2), rows render compact metadata but no dependency row is visibly selected, down does not move a visible selection, and Enter does not open a dependency.

## Root Cause
`BrowserItems` is only populated from `ParentGroupBrowser`. When an issue has BlockedBy/Blocks/Related refs but no parent-group structure, the model leaves the selectable dependency list empty, so the render fix from beads-workbench-59z has nothing meaningful to select.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] When an issue has BlockedBy, Blocks, or Related refs but no parent-group structure, the detail-mode selectable dependency list is populated from those refs.
- [ ] The default dependency selection is initialized predictably for those refs.
- [ ] Up/down in Dependencies visibly moves selection across blocked/blocks/related rows in the real app.
- [ ] Enter on the selected dependency opens the selected issue in the real app.
- [ ] The fix preserves existing parent-group/structure browsing behavior when that context is present.
close_reason: Fixed detail-mode dependency browser population for non-parent-group issues; added model/app tests for selection defaults and Enter-open behavior; focused detail/app test suites pass.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review: Root cause confirmed and bug is well-scoped. What: BrowserItems is only populated from ParentGroupBrowser (model.go:312), never from BlockedBy/Blocks/Related refs. When an issue has blockers but no parent-group structure, BrowserItems stays empty, so moveRelatedSelection has nothing to move and BrowserSelectedIndex stays -1. The render side (59z fix) correctly passes selection state now, but there are zero items to select. Why: this is the actual functional gap — 59z fixed render wiring but the model never populates BrowserItems for non-structure dependency rows. Suggested action: populateBrowserFromIssue (or equivalent) must flatten BlockedBy + Blocks + Related into BrowserItems when no parent-group is active, so selection/navigation/enter all work on ordinary dependency refs.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review: Missing acceptance criterion. What: no criterion says 'BrowserItems must be populated from blocked-by/blocks/related refs when parent-group is absent.' The existing criteria inherited from 59z assume render-only fixes. Why: without this criterion, a tasker could fix render wiring again and miss the model population gap — exactly what happened with 59z. Suggested action: add criterion: 'When an issue has BlockedBy, Blocks, or Related refs but no parent-group structure, BrowserItems must contain those refs and BrowserSelectedIndex must default to 0.'

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review: Ordering relative to 74r/6eq. What: 2l4 is a functional bug (dependency browsing is broken); 74r/6eq are cosmetic layout bugs. Why: 2l4 blocks the core use case of navigating dependencies. Layout polish on a non-functional pane is wasted effort if 2l4 changes how BrowserItems are populated (which may affect row count and scroll behavior). Suggested action: implement 2l4 before 74r/6eq. x2u is independent (gateway-only, no UI coupling) and can proceed in parallel with anything.

title: Dependency issue rows do not show ANSI color/styled dashboard rendering in detail pane
description: Discovered from user review after epic beads-workbench-90w was closed. Expected: dependency issue rows in the Dependencies pane should use the same styled/colored rendering as dashboard rows. Actual: dependency rendering currently calls `issuerow.RenderReferenceCompact(... Styled: false)` so ANSI/styled dashboard-like color treatment is absent. Repro: compare dashboard issue rows vs Dependencies pane rows in the running app. Impact: the promised renderer reuse is incomplete visually and dependency rows feel flatter than dashboard rows.
close_reason: Closed

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review: Actionable and trivial — flip Styled:false to Styled:true in renderReferenceRow (details.go:531). However, this is the same code site as beads-workbench-59z (Selected:false at line 529). What: these two bugs touch the same function call with the same root cause — renderReferenceRow doesn't pass through real state. Why: implementing them separately risks merge conflicts and duplicated test updates for no benefit. Suggested action: merge beads-workbench-2a6 into beads-workbench-59z as a single fix that wires both Selected and Styled through renderReferenceRow.

title: Dependencies pane selection and keyboard navigation should match dashboard issue selection behavior
description:
Discovered from user review after epic beads-workbench-90w was closed. Expected: in the Dependencies pane, up/down keys should provide the same visible issue selection behavior as the dashboard, including shared row styling/color treatment for the selected dependency and non-selected dependency rows. Actual: detail-mode state tracks a related selection index, but dependency rendering hardcodes `renderReferenceRow(... Selected:false, Styled:false)`, so the pane does not show dashboard-like selection or ANSI/styled row rendering. Repro: focus Dependencies in detail mode and use up/down keys on a detail item with multiple dependency/structure rows. Impact: dependency browsing is confusing and the pane does not behave like a proper issue list.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Dependency rows render through the shared compact issue-row path with styled output enabled.
- [ ] The currently selected dependency row uses the same visible selected treatment as dashboard rows.
- [ ] Up/down in Dependencies moves the visible selected row predictably.
- [ ] Enter still opens the selected dependency issue.
- [ ] The fix applies to dependency/structure rows rendered in this pane without introducing a separate one-off row renderer.
close_reason: Fixed dependencies-pane row rendering to use shared styled compact rows with selected issue highlighting wired to BrowserSelectedIssueID; verified detail UI and detail mode tests pass.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review: This is partially already implemented. Code at model.go:178-186 shows moveRelatedSelection is called on scroll-up/down when FocusPaneDependencies is active, and BrowserSelectedIndex is tracked. The real bug is in the render side: renderReferenceRow (details.go:526-532) hardcodes Selected:false and Styled:false, so the selection state is never visually reflected. What: the model-side selection logic exists; only the render path is broken. Why: this means the fix is simpler than the description implies — it's a render wiring bug, not a missing navigation model. Suggested action: narrow the description to 'pass BrowserSelectedIndex to renderReferenceRow so Selected reflects actual state'. This overlaps heavily with beads-workbench-2a6 (Styled:false). Consider merging them into one fix.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Set review — this is solid and ready. What: the prior reviewer correctly identified the fix is render-only (renderReferenceRow hardcodes Selected:false, Styled:false at line 526-532). The model-side selection logic already exists. Acceptance criteria are clear and testable. Why: this is the highest-value fix in the set — it makes the dependency pane actually interactive. One coupling note: if 74r (width change) lands first, the styled rows will render with the new width, which is fine. If 6eq (framing) also lands, the styled rows need to fit inside frame borders. Suggested action: implement this first or second. The fix is: pass selection index and Styled:true through renderReferenceRow. Ensure renderRelationshipGroups receives the selected index to compare against flat row position.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Verifier rerun: FAIL. Shared compact rows render, but live Dependencies selection still only follows BrowserItems/Structure context. On fixture issue bwf-2, blocker rows show no selected-row treatment, DOWN leaves no visible selection change, and ENTER does not open a dependency. Follow-up: beads-workbench-2l4.

title: Metadata rail should use a framed panel instead of an unboxed text rail
description:
Discovered from user review after epic beads-workbench-90w was closed. Expected: the metadata side panel should use a frame/border similar to Dependencies and Content. User decision: frame the Metadata pane only; Dependencies and Content are already correctly framed. Actual: metadata is rendered as an unboxed text rail with a vertical separator only, which looks mechanically weaker and less polished than the other panes. Repro: open the detail screen in the real app and compare visual treatment of Dependencies/Content vs Metadata. Impact: detail layout feels visually inconsistent and less polished than intended.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Metadata pane is rendered in a framed/bordered panel.
- [ ] Dependencies and Content keep their existing framed treatment; this follow-up only adds a frame to Metadata.
- [ ] Real-app review confirms improved visual consistency across the three panes.
close_reason: Rendered metadata as framed FormSection pane while preserving existing dependencies/content pane framing and interaction behavior.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review: Well-scoped, straightforward visual change. No hidden complexity. One observation: the current rendering in renderThreePane (details.go:195-198) uses a '│' separator for metadata but no box for any pane — Dependencies and Content are also unboxed columns. What: verify that Dependencies and Content actually use frames/borders today. If they don't, this bug may be requesting framing for metadata alone, which would create visual inconsistency in the opposite direction. Why: the description says 'match the other major panes' but if those panes aren't framed either, the fix scope changes. Suggested action: confirm current frame state of all three panes before implementing; if none are framed, consider whether all three should be, or just metadata.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Set review — frame scope is ambiguous and prior reviewer's concern is unresolved. What: the prior comment correctly identified that Dependencies and Content are ALSO unboxed (renderThreePane joins columns with padToWidth + gap, no borders). The description says 'match the other major panes' but those panes have no frames either. Why: if you frame only metadata, you create a different inconsistency. If you frame all three, scope triples. The owner hasn't responded to clarify intent. Suggested action: owner must decide: (a) frame all three panes consistently, or (b) frame metadata only and accept the asymmetry. Option (a) is the right call but needs to be stated explicitly. This issue is blocked on that decision.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Verifier rerun: PASS. Built /tmp/bwb and reviewed real detail mode against embedded fixture at 120x34 and 160x34. Metadata now renders as a framed panel, three-pane composition looks coherent, and metadata paging/scroll behavior remained intact in runtime review.

title: Dependencies pane is too narrow relative to content and should use a larger share of width
description:
Discovered from user review after epic beads-workbench-90w was closed. Expected: the Dependencies pane should use a somewhat larger share of width so dependency rows and structure data are comfortably readable beside Content, but not as large as one-third. User decision: target roughly 25% of available width (subject to sensible min/max bounds) instead of the current narrow near-fixed 22..28 column pane. Actual: detail layout currently uses a narrow left pane derived from `splitThreePaneWidths` with `left = clamp(available/5, 22..28)`, which makes Dependencies feel too cramped. Repro: open the real app detail view at 120x34 or 160x34 and compare Dependencies width vs Content width; dependency titles truncate aggressively. Impact: relationship browsing/readability is weaker than intended and panel balance feels off.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Dependencies width uses an updated formula targeting roughly 25% of the available pane width rather than the current near-fixed narrow rail.
- [ ] The pane remains bounded with sensible min/max values so narrow terminals still render acceptably.
- [ ] Real-app review at 120x34 and 160x34 shows materially better dependency readability without starving Content.
close_reason: Updated detail pane width split to target ~25% dependencies rail with bounded min/max and verified at 120x34 and 160x34.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review: Well-scoped and actionable. The code path is clear (splitThreePaneWidths, line 212). One concern: the description says 'roughly around double current width' but doesn't specify a target ratio or formula. What: acceptance criteria should specify the desired allocation (e.g., 'left pane gets 1/3 of available minus metadata' or a specific min/max range). Why: without a target, the implementer will guess and may need rework. Suggested action: add a concrete width target or ratio to the description, e.g., 'left = clamp(available/3, 30..50)' or similar.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Set review — width target still missing. What: prior reviewer correctly flagged that no concrete ratio/formula is specified. The description still says 'roughly around double' which is not implementable. Why: the implementer will pick an arbitrary number, get feedback, and rework. Suggested action: owner should specify a target formula before this is ready for implementation, e.g., 'left = clamp(available/3, 30..50)' with metadata staying at 34. This is a 1-line constant change once the target is decided — do not overcomplicate.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Execution ordering note for full set (74r, 6eq, 59z, axe, pxs). Recommended order: (1) 59z — render wiring fix, highest value, no dependencies. (2) 74r — width change, requires owner to specify target first. (3) 6eq — framing, requires owner decision on scope, touches same renderThreePane code as 74r so do after. (4) axe — new feature, lowest risk of rework if done after layout stabilizes. (5) pxs — investigation only, may close as non-bug. Coupling risk: 74r and 6eq both modify renderThreePane/splitThreePaneWidths and the column assembly code. If done in parallel they will conflict. Do them sequentially.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Verifier rerun: PASS. Built /tmp/bwb and reviewed real detail mode against embedded fixture at 120x34 and 160x34. Dependencies rail is materially wider (~29 cols at 120, ~39 at 160), titles are more readable, content remains usable, and no dependency-pane regressions were observed.

title: Detail content pane leaves lower half blank and does not anchor comments low
description:
## Description
Discovered during verification of beads-workbench-90w.2. Expected: the redesigned 3-pane detail screen should use available vertical space well, with comments rendered as a visually secondary lower subsection inside the dominant Content pane. Actual: built-binary runtime captures at 120x34, 140x34, and 160x34 against the embedded fixture show description/notes/comments clustered in the upper half of Content with a large blank region below, so comments are not positioned as a lower subsection and the layout still feels vertically under-filled.

## Likely Root Cause
The Content pane currently renders sections top-down and then pads remaining height with blank lines, so short/medium content stays top-aligned and comments are never intentionally pushed lower inside the pane.

## Fix Direction
Preserve the accepted single Content-pane scroll model. Rebalance vertical allocation inside the Content pane by inserting adaptive spacer lines between upper content (details/notes) and the comments subsection when total content height is shorter than the available inner pane height. When content already fills/exceeds available height, insert no spacer and preserve current scroll behavior.

## Constraints
- Do not split Content into multiple focus panes or multiple independent scroll regions.
- Do not hardcode for a single terminal size such as 120x34.
- Keep comments visually secondary and lower in the Content pane when there is spare height.
- Preserve shared rendering/component reuse already introduced by beads-workbench-90w.1.

## Repro
1. `go build -o /tmp/bwb ./cmd/bwb`
2. `repoPath="$(mktemp -d)" && sh internal/testing/e2e/embeddedfixture/setup.sh "$repoPath" internal/testing/e2e/embeddedfixture/seed.json`
3. `python3 scripts/capture_bwb_screen.py --cwd "$repoPath" --width 120 --height 34 --startup-wait 1.2 --steps '0.2:3,0.2:CTRL+Q' -- -- env BD_NON_INTERACTIVE=1 /tmp/bwb`
4. Repeat at 140x34 and 160x34.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] When total center-pane content is shorter than the available inner height, the comments subsection is pushed lower instead of remaining immediately under notes/body.
- [ ] When total center-pane content fills or exceeds available inner height, no spacer is inserted and scrolling behavior remains unchanged.
- [ ] Spacer calculation is adaptive to available pane height rather than hardcoded to one terminal size.
- [ ] Runtime captures at 120x34 and 160x34 show materially stronger vertical usage in the Content pane.
- [ ] Comments remain visually secondary and read as a lower subsection within Content.
close_reason: Implemented adaptive Content-pane spacer insertion so comments shift lower when content is shorter than pane height while preserving existing single-scroll behavior when content fills/exceeds available height; updated detail renderer goldens and verified via focused tests and 120x34/160x34 runtime captures.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Root cause is clear from code: renderContentPaneLines() builds content top-down (title, description, notes, comments) and returns a flat line slice. sliceWithOffset() then pads remaining lines with empty strings to fill innerHeight (line 371-373 of details.go). The content is top-aligned by construction — there is no mechanism to push comments toward the bottom or distribute vertical space between sections. The bug description correctly identifies the symptom but does not name this root cause. Suggested action: add a one-line root cause note to the bug description referencing the top-aligned padding in sliceWithOffset and the lack of vertical space distribution in renderContentPaneLines.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Fix direction: renderContentPaneLines should insert spacer lines between the notes section and the comments section so comments are pushed toward the bottom of the available height. Specifically: compute the total lines used by title+description+notes and by comments, then fill the gap with empty lines so comments anchor to the lower portion of innerHeight. This keeps rendering as a flat []string, preserves sliceWithOffset scroll semantics unchanged, and requires no new abstractions. Why this over alternatives: (1) bottom-aligning ALL content would look wrong when content is short, (2) a split-viewport approach would add complexity and break the single-scroll model, (3) spacer insertion is ~10 lines of code in one function.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Risk: spacer insertion must be height-aware, meaning renderContentPaneLines needs the available innerHeight as a parameter (it currently only takes detail and width). If the spacer is computed naively without clamping, short-content issues with many comments could produce zero or negative spacer — handle with max(0, ...). Also: when total content exceeds innerHeight (long description + many comments), spacer should be 0 and scroll should work normally. Test both cases.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Bug acceptance criteria are underspecified. The bug says 'distributes content more intentionally' which is not testable. Suggested acceptance language: (1) When total content lines < innerHeight, comments section starts no higher than 60% of innerHeight (i.e. lower portion). (2) When total content lines >= innerHeight, no spacer is inserted and scroll works as before. (3) Runtime captures at 120x34 and 160x34 show no blank region larger than 2 lines between the last notes line and the comments header. These three criteria let a tasker verify mechanically.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Anti-pattern warning: do NOT solve this by adding a second scrollable viewport or splitting Content into two Bubble Tea models. That would break the single-scroll-offset model already working in sliceWithOffset and add disproportionate complexity for a layout spacing fix. The fix belongs entirely in renderContentPaneLines (plus passing innerHeight into it).

title: Preserve board and search context during auto-refresh
description:
## Description
Make board and search safe for automatic reload so freshness checks do not yank the user away from current context.

## Instructions
1. Introduce a non-destructive board auto-refresh path separate from the existing manual `r` full reload behavior.
2. Update `internal/mode/board/model.go` so board auto-refresh can preserve the focused column and previously selected issue when that issue still exists after reload.
3. Define predictable fallback behavior when the prior board selection no longer exists.
4. Update `internal/mode/search/model.go` and shell orchestration so search auto-refresh reruns the current query only when safe and does not interrupt active typing.
5. Preserve existing selection/query state as much as possible across auto-refresh, and do not force mode switches or clear current detail state as a side effect of board/search refresh.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Board auto-refresh restores prior selection and focus when the issue still exists
- [ ] Board uses a deterministic fallback when the prior selection disappears
- [ ] Board auto-refresh is non-destructive relative to the existing manual full reload behavior
- [ ] Search auto-refresh does not fire while the user is actively typing in the query field
- [ ] Search refresh preserves the current query and current result selection when possible
- [ ] Board/search refresh does not force active-mode changes or clear current detail state as a side effect

## Files to Modify
- `internal/mode/board/model.go`
- `internal/mode/board/model_test.go`
- `internal/mode/search/model.go`
- `internal/mode/search/model_test.go`
- `internal/app/model.go`

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Hidden complexity: board reload currently resets all state — loading=true, pendingLoads=0, selectedRow reset to 0 for all columns (line 114-118 of board/model.go). What: preserving selection requires either (a) a new reload path that diffs old/new data and restores cursors, or (b) splitting load into fetch + merge phases. Why: this is the hardest part of the entire epic and the instructions understate it with 'reload can preserve the focused column.' Suggested action: add an instruction step that explicitly says 'introduce a non-destructive reload path separate from the existing full-reset reload triggered by the R key.'

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Missing AC: what happens to detail view during auto-refresh? What: the task says 'board and search' but detail is listed in the epic's success criteria and in t7o.1's routing. Detail view is not mentioned in t7o.2's instructions or AC at all. Why: if board auto-refresh triggers while detail pane is open (split view), the detail pane's issue might disappear from the board. Suggested action: either explicitly scope detail out of t7o.2 with a note, or add an AC covering detail stability during board refresh.

title: Apply dirty and stale refresh policy across active views
description:
## Description
Combine event-driven invalidation with time-based staleness so the active view stays fresh without turning every surface into a polling loop.

## Instructions
1. Track per-surface dirty/stale metadata and last-refresh timestamps in `internal/app/model.go`.
2. Keep immediate detail reload after successful edit/update/close/comment/status flows, and mark board/search dirty when those writes can invalidate browse data.
3. Refresh only the active surface on eligible focus regain, timer ticks, or view activation when that surface is dirty or stale.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Write flows still reload detail immediately after success
- [ ] Board/search can be marked dirty by writes and refresh on the next eligible active-view refresh
- [ ] Auto-refresh is limited to the active surface rather than hidden background polling of all views
- [ ] Default refresh cadence is low-frequency (about 60 seconds) and test-covered

## Files to Modify
- `internal/app/model.go`
- `internal/app/model_test.go`

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Scope concern: instruction 4 says 'update user-facing guidance or docs if auto-refresh changes reload expectations' and files-to-modify lists docs/CODING.md and key-bindings.md. What: docs updates are filler in a technical task and will either be skipped or done perfunctorily. Why: docs changes should follow implementation, not be bundled into the integration task. Suggested action: drop docs from t7o.3's files-to-modify and AC, and handle docs as part of the acceptance review (t7o.4) or a separate micro-task if needed.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Good: AC bullet 3 correctly limits refresh to the active surface only. This prevents the background-polling-all-views antipattern.

title: Acceptance Review: focus-aware and scheduled auto-refresh
description:
## Description
Verify focus-aware and scheduled auto-refresh works end-to-end before closing the epic.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All implementation tasks for the epic are closed
- [ ] Manual verification confirms active-view refresh on focus regain
- [ ] Manual or automated verification confirms low-frequency scheduled refresh behavior
- [ ] Board/search/detail keep user context stable during refresh, or any gaps are tracked as follow-up issues
- [ ] Any user-facing reload/help text changes are documented when implementation changes behavior expectations
- [ ] Required tests run and pass
- [ ] Any discovered defects are captured as new beads issues

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Acceptance task is well structured. AC covers the right gates: all tasks closed, manual focus-regain verification, scheduled refresh verification, context stability, tests pass, and defect capture. No changes needed.

title: Add focus-aware and scheduled auto-refresh
description:
## Description
Implement shell-managed auto-refresh so the visible workbench view stays current without manual reload. Combine focus-regain refresh, scheduled stale refresh, and existing mutation-driven refresh while keeping board/search/detail UX stable.

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Focus regain refreshes the active view so returning users see current data
- [ ] Active-view auto-refresh runs on a low-frequency schedule (target about 60s), not aggressive polling
- [ ] Existing mutation-triggered detail refresh behavior remains intact
- [ ] Writes that invalidate browse data mark board/search dirty for the next eligible active-view refresh
- [ ] Board/search/detail interactions remain stable during auto-refresh; disruptive regressions are tracked or fixed
- [ ] Acceptance review task passes before epic closure

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Plan review: dependency structure is correct. t7o.1 and t7o.2 are independent and parallelizable, t7o.3 integrates both, t7o.4 gates closure. The 3-task + acceptance breakdown is right-sized for this scope — no need to split or merge.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Risk: epic success criteria bullet 3 says 'existing mutation-triggered refresh behavior remains intact and is integrated with dirty/stale tracking.' This conflates a regression guard (keep existing behavior) with new feature work (dirty/stale integration). Why: the tasker may treat 'integrated' as mandatory scope creep vs a nice-to-have. Suggested action: split into two criteria — one for regression safety ('mutation-triggered detail refresh still works'), one for the new dirty-marking behavior ('writes mark board/search dirty').

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Reviewer verdict: good enough with minor changes. Updated epic and child tasks to clarify the named ~60s tick constant, focus-reporting fallback behavior, non-destructive board auto-refresh, detail-state stability expectations, and to move docs verification into acceptance review.

title: Expose parent-group browser context for issue details view
description:
## Description
Expose the normalized parent-group context needed by the issue-details left browser panel so the UI can show the parent issue and sibling child issues without mutating the panel contents while browsing inside the same group.

## Instructions
1. Extend the issue detail read model to carry the parent-group browser context needed by the redesigned issue details view.
2. Use real `bd show --json` parent-child relationship data to derive:
   - the current issue's parent issue when one exists
   - the sibling child issues that share that parent
3. Keep arbitrary related/blocking relationships out of this browser context for v1; the left panel is for parent-group browsing, not general relationship browsing.
4. If the current issue has no parent issue, return an empty browser-group context so the UI can hide or omit the left panel for now.
5. Add regression tests that lock down the observed parent-child mapping behavior and the no-parent fallback behavior.

## Files to Modify
- internal/domain/issues.go
- internal/gateway/beads/read_gateway.go
- internal/gateway/beads/read_gateway_test.go
- gateway fixtures/testdata as needed

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Issue detail exposes parent-group browser context separate from generic related/blocking references
- [ ] Gateway parsing derives parent and sibling issues from real parent-child relationship data
- [ ] No-parent issues produce an empty browser-group context
- [ ] General related/blocking references remain separate from the browser-group model
- [ ] Tests pass: go test ./internal/gateway/beads
close_reason: Added parent-group browser context to IssueDetail, mapped parent/child relationships from bd show JSON with no-parent fallback, and added regression tests for mapping and separation from related/blocking refs.

title: Define the unified panel interaction model across board, search, and detail
description:
## Description
Capture the resolved issue-details panel contract and the remaining implementation guidance before execution.

## Resolved Contract
- The special left browser panel exists only in issue details view.
- Board and search do not adopt the same explicit browser/content/metadata panel structure.
- Board and search still must share the same shell sizing/full-height contract so the app uses space consistently.
- In issue details, the left browser should show a stable parent-group context: the parent issue plus sibling child issues.
- Selecting a left-browser item updates the center/right detail workspace.
- Browsing inside the same parent group should not mutate the left-browser contents for every selection.
- If the current issue has no parent issue, omit or hide the special left browser panel for now.
- Do not rely on inline key hints.
- Ignore ordering concerns for now.

## Remaining Implementation Guidance
- The parent-group browser context needs explicit backend/domain support because the current issue detail model does not expose parent and sibling grouping.
- The right metadata panel still needs one final interaction decision for Enter-driven status editing.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Issue-details panel contract is explicit enough for implementation
- [ ] Board/search sizing expectations are explicit without forcing them into the same panel structure
- [ ] Left-browser data source is explicitly chosen as parent-group context
- [ ] No-parent fallback behavior is explicit
close_reason: Panel contract decisions captured from discussion

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Most open questions are already answered by user decisions. What: The user's stated direction resolves panel roles (left=browser, center=content, right=metadata+actions), navigation model (Enter updates adjacent detail, consistent focus across app), and full-height/width contract (all screens must use full terminal dimensions consistently). Why: This task is blocking .1 and .2 but its open questions are largely resolved — leaving it as-is stalls execution unnecessarily. Suggested action: Convert this from a discussion task into a brief contract document task. The tasker should codify the user's stated decisions into acceptance criteria rather than re-discovering them. The remaining genuinely open question is: what does the panel model look like for board and search modes — do they also get the three-panel layout, or does only dedicated detail have it? That specific question should be explicitly answered before unblocking.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Missing open question: board/search panel model is undefined. What: The epic says 'board, search, and detail share a coherent panel-layout model' but the user's direction focuses on the dedicated detail three-panel layout. Board and search currently render as single full-body surfaces (board = column grid, search = query/results/preview). The .4 contract must explicitly state whether board and search adopt the same three-panel split or retain their current single-surface layouts with only the shared sizing contract applied. Why: If .1 and .2 assume board/search get the three-panel treatment but they don't, the shared layout contract will be over-designed. If they don't get it, the 'coherent panel model' success criterion needs to be scoped to mean 'consistent sizing plus shared primitives' not 'identical panel structure'. Suggested action: Add this as an explicit decision point in .4's instructions.

title: Promote issue browsing to a true left panel that drives adjacent detail
description:
## Description
Replace the nested left related-issues widget model with a true left browser panel whose selection updates the adjacent detail workspace.

## Instructions
1. Implement the left side as a real browser panel in issue details view, not as a focusable sub-widget embedded inside detail content.
2. Build the browser panel on the normalized parent-group context from the backend task; do not use generic related/blocking relationships as the primary panel source in v1.
3. Show a stable group consisting of the parent issue plus sibling child issues.
4. Selecting a browser-panel item updates the center/right issue inspection workspace.
5. Browsing between items in the same parent group must not rebuild the left-panel contents on every selection.
6. If there is no parent-group context, omit or hide the special left browser panel for now.
7. Keep the center and right panes conceptually paired: center for issue text/content, right for metadata and lightweight actions.
8. Add or update rendering and app-integration tests to verify stable browser-panel behavior and browser-to-detail updates.

## Files to Modify
- internal/app/model.go
- internal/app/model_test.go
- internal/mode/details/model.go
- internal/mode/details/model_test.go
- internal/ui/details/details.go
- internal/ui/details/details_test.go
- shared row helpers as needed

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Left issue browser behaves like a real panel rather than a nested widget
- [ ] The left browser uses parent-group context rather than generic relation lists
- [ ] Selecting an issue in the left panel updates the center/right detail workspace
- [ ] Left-panel contents stay stable while browsing within the same parent group
- [ ] No-parent issues omit or hide the special left browser panel for now
- [ ] Tests pass: go test ./internal/app ./internal/mode/details ./internal/ui/details
close_reason: Implemented issue-details parent-group left browser panel, wired browser selection to detail workspace updates, stabilized browser contents within parent group, hid browser when no parent-group context, and updated app/mode/ui tests including wide layout golden.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: The current left rail is a renderer column, not a panel component — the task understates the refactor scope. What: The instructions say 'Redesign dedicated detail presentation so the left side is treated as a browser panel' but the current left rail exists only as a column inside ui/details/details.go's renderThreeColumn(), with rendering logic (renderRelatedIssuesRail) inline in the same file. There is no separate panel component to promote. The current 'left rail' also only shows related issues for the currently viewed issue — it does not show arbitrary browsable issues. Why: A tasker reading 'promote left related-issues widget' may think there is an existing selectable widget to extract. In reality, the left column is a stateless render function and the browsing state (SelectedRelatedIndex, focus pane, Enter-to-navigate) lives in mode/details/model.go. The refactor needs to: (a) create a new panel component, (b) move selection state into it, (c) wire it as a shell-level panel rather than a detail-renderer column. This is a new component, not a promotion. Suggested action: Reframe the instructions to acknowledge this is building a new left browser panel component, not extracting an existing one. Specify whether the left panel content is 'related issues for the selected issue' (current behavior) or 'all issues from the browse mode that led here' (a fundamentally different data source).

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Missing data source decision: what populates the left browser panel? What: The user says 'Enter on an issue in the left browser should update adjacent details, similar to browsing issues in the dashboard.' This implies the left panel shows a list of issues the user can browse through — but from where? Options: (a) related issues of the currently-viewed issue (current behavior), (b) the list from the board/search mode that spawned detail, or (c) a new independent query. These have very different implementation shapes. Why: Option (a) is close to what exists but limits navigation to the issue graph. Option (b) requires the shell to pass the browse-mode's issue list into detail, which is a new data flow. Option (c) requires a gateway query. The task cannot be executed without this decision. Suggested action: This should be resolved as part of .4's contract definition. The user's language ('similar to browsing issues in the dashboard') suggests option (b) — the same issue list from board/search becomes the left panel in detail. Confirm or reject this interpretation before unblocking .2.

title: Refactor shell and mode layout sizing to use a shared full-height workspace contract
description:
## Description
Make board, search, and detail consume the available shell body height through one shared layout contract instead of each surface calculating height differently.

## Instructions
1. Inspect shell body sizing in `internal/app/model.go` and identify the current height/width calculations used by board, search, and detail.
2. Introduce a shared workspace sizing contract for the main application body so all primary screens consume the same available height budget.
3. Remove the current inconsistency where dedicated detail is wrapped differently from board/search and ends up using height differently.
4. Preserve header/footer/help/toast/modal behavior while making the main workspace full-height.
5. Board and search keep their mode-specific layouts, but they must satisfy the same shell height/width contract as detail.
6. Add or update tests that lock down the shared body-height contract and prevent regressions in shell layout sizing.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Board, search, and detail derive body size from one shared shell contract
- [ ] Full available body height is used consistently across the main screens
- [ ] Shell body height is measured explicitly enough in tests to catch regressions in board/detail/search sizing
- [ ] Dedicated detail no longer has a special boxed body wrapper that wastes height relative to board/search
- [ ] Existing overlays/header/footer behavior remains intact
- [ ] Tests pass: go test ./internal/app ./internal/ui/...
close_reason: Implemented shared full-height workspace sizing contract across board/search/detail; removed dedicated detail body wrapper and added regression coverage for shell body sizing.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Detail is not a mode.Controller — structural asymmetry the task should address explicitly. What: Board and search implement the mode.Controller interface (Init/Update/View/SetSize), but detail is a raw detailsmode.Model struct managed directly by the shell with ad-hoc sizing (detailViewportHeight(), width-8 content width, FormSection wrapping in renderBody). The shell's renderBody() applies completely different sizing logic for detail vs board/search. Why: A 'shared full-height workspace contract' that only standardizes the numbers but leaves the structural asymmetry (Controller vs raw struct) in place will be fragile. The shell will still have two different sizing paths. Suggested action: The instructions should explicitly call out that the detail sizing path in renderBody() and detailViewportHeight() must be unified with the board/search SetSize path, even if detail doesn't become a full Controller. At minimum, detail needs to receive the same (width, height) contract board/search get via SetSize rather than deriving dimensions ad-hoc.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Acceptance criteria need a concrete measurable for full-height. What: Criterion says 'Full available body height is used consistently' but does not define what 'full available body height' means numerically. Currently board/search get max(14, height-7) and detail gets max(1, max(14, height-7)-2), which are already different by 2 lines. Why: Without a concrete formula (e.g., 'body height = terminal height - header lines - footer lines, with no additional padding or wrapping'), a tasker may produce a new contract that is still inconsistent but in a different way. The verifier also cannot objectively check this criterion. Suggested action: Add an explicit acceptance criterion like 'All primary screens receive body dimensions as (termWidth - shellHorizontalChrome, termHeight - headerLines - footerLines) with no mode-specific adjustments to the body budget'.

title: Acceptance Review: Unified full-height panel layout
description:
## Description
Verify the unified panel-layout redesign before the epic is closed.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Panel behavior is coherent across board, search, and dedicated detail
- [ ] Full-height and width usage is consistent and no longer boxed in
- [ ] Left browser panel updates adjacent detail instead of behaving like a nested widget
- [ ] Metadata action workflow is usable for the initial supported fields
- [ ] Relevant tests, quality gates, and runtime verification pass

## Owner
verifier
close_reason: Verified unified panel layout via focused tests, full quality gates, and runtime fixture run

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Verifier rerun: PASS. Focused panel-layout tests, full quality gates, and built-binary runtime verification passed. Artifact: /tmp/beads-workbench-9tl-verify (runtime_summary.json, runtime_transcript.bin). Next: close acceptance review.

title: Rework issue browsing into a full-height unified panel layout
description:
## Description
Redesign the issue details experience around a full-height workspace with a dedicated left browser panel and a paired center/right inspection workspace, while also fixing the app-wide shell sizing inconsistencies that currently waste screen height and width.

## Goals
- Make issue details use a dedicated left browser panel for navigating connected issues in a stable grouping context
- Treat center and right as one inspection workspace: center for issue text/content, right for metadata and lightweight actions
- Use the full available terminal height and width consistently across the app shell
- Remove the current inconsistency where board and detail use the available height differently
- Make metadata directly actionable where appropriate, starting with status editing

## Product Direction
- The left browser panel is a special part of the issue details view, not a new universal panel that every mode must copy.
- Board and search keep their own mode-specific layouts, but they must share the same shell sizing contract and stop wasting vertical space differently from detail.
- The issue-details left browser should prefer stable parent-group browsing over general relation browsing.
- Selecting an item in the left browser updates the center/right detail workspace.
- The left browser contents should stay stable while browsing within the same group.

## Problems This Epic Must Address
- Current left related-issues area behaves like a nested detail widget instead of a true side browser
- Board and detail do not use full height consistently
- Layout under-uses screen width and feels boxed in
- Dashboard and detail use height differently, so the app is not even consistently wrong
- Metadata panel is mostly passive instead of being useful for quick actions

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Issue details has a true left browser panel with stable grouping behavior
- [ ] The center/right inspection workspace uses full available screen height and width consistently
- [ ] Board, search, and detail share a consistent shell sizing contract even though their panel content differs by mode
- [ ] Selecting an issue in the left browser updates adjacent detail without mutating the browser contents unnecessarily
- [ ] Metadata panel supports at least one meaningful direct action workflow
- [ ] Acceptance review confirms layout consistency, full-height usage, and UX coherence
close_reason: all steps complete

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Planning corrected after creation failure. Added blocked discussion task beads-workbench-9dn.4 for the cross-mode panel contract, blocked implementation tasks behind it, and attached acceptance review beads-workbench-9tl to the epic. Explicitly carrying forward user feedback that full-height/full-width usage and panel consistency are first-class requirements.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Plan structure is sound but has one gap that will stall execution: the left browser panel data source. What: The dependency graph (.4 → .1, .4 → .2, then .3, then acceptance) is correct. The task decomposition is reasonable — sizing contract, panel promotion, metadata actions, acceptance. However, all tasks downstream of .4 depend on a decision .4 does not explicitly surface: what data populates the left browser panel in detail mode? The user says 'similar to browsing issues in the dashboard' which implies the board/search issue list carries over into detail, but this is not stated in any task. Why: This is the single decision that determines whether .2 is a 'rewire existing related-issues rail' task or a 'build new issue-list passthrough from board/search into detail' task. These are materially different in scope and wiring. Suggested action: Add this as an explicit question to .4 and resolve it before unblocking. Also add .1 and .2 as dependencies for .3 — they are currently missing.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Reviewer verdict: plan is sound but not execution-ready yet. Updated tasks to reflect resolved UX decisions, added missing .3 dependencies on sizing/browser work, and narrowed remaining blocking questions to the dedicated-detail browser data source, board/search alignment with the shared panel model, and the exact Enter-driven status-edit interaction.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Discussion resolved the core panel contract: the special left browser panel exists only in issue details view, not board/search; it should show a stable parent-group context (parent issue plus sibling child issues), and selecting items there updates center/right detail without changing the left panel contents. Added a dedicated backend task for parent-group browser context because current domain/gateway models do not expose parent/child grouping.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Final remaining UX decision resolved: status editing in the right metadata panel uses a dialog when Enter is pressed on the status field. Reuse an existing dialog pattern if suitable; otherwise create a dedicated status dialog. Priority remains out of scope for the first pass.

title: Acceptance Review: Simplify UI core architecture and harden package boundaries
description:
## Description
Verify that the architecture simplification epic achieved its intended boundary cleanup without widening product scope.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All child implementation tasks are closed
- [ ] Architecture guardrail test passes: `go test ./cmd/bwb -run TestArchitectureGuardrails`
- [ ] Build passes: `go build ./cmd/bwb`
- [ ] Vet passes: `go vet ./...`
- [ ] Tests pass: `go test ./...`
- [ ] The final package layout still honors the single `BeadsGateway` boundary and the architecture guardrail intent
- [ ] No new bugs or follow on tasks are hiding inside comments instead of tracked work

## Owner
verifier
close_reason: All criteria verified

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Acts as the epic gate for beads-workbench-wz9. Epic closure waits for this review task to pass after all implementation tasks close.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review finding — acceptance task is solid. The criteria are concrete: all children closed, build/vet/tests pass, architecture guardrails pass, no hidden follow-on work. The 'no new bugs or follow on tasks hiding inside comments' criterion is exactly right — it forces the verifier to read all task comments and file any outstanding work as tracked issues before closing. Why: this is the right gate. Suggested action: none.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review finding — acceptance should explicitly verify architecture guardrail test passes. What: the acceptance criteria say 'build passes, vet passes, tests pass' which implicitly includes the guardrail test in cmd/bwb/architecture_guardrails_test.go. But docs/CODING.md recommends running it explicitly first: 'go test ./cmd/bwb -run TestArchitectureGuardrails'. Why: if the refactor accidentally introduces a forbidden import, the guardrail test catches it — but only if the verifier actually runs it. Relying on 'go test ./...' is correct but making it explicit reduces risk of oversight. Suggested action: add explicit acceptance line: 'Architecture guardrail test passes: go test ./cmd/bwb -run TestArchitectureGuardrails'

title: Unify selection and detail coordination inside internal/app
description:
## Description
Make message-driven selection updates the single source of truth inside `internal/app`. Remove polling-based selection syncing and keep `model.go` readable without widening the architecture.

## Instructions
1. Keep the message-based path — `SelectionChangedMsg` emitted by board and search — as the only selection synchronization mechanism driving detail loading.
2. Remove the polling-based shell selection path, including `syncSelectionFromControllers` and related controller `CurrentSelection()` polling.
3. If extraction materially shrinks `internal/app/model.go`, limit it to focused `internal/app` helpers for selection/detail coordination or shell-owned action dispatch. Do not create new packages, registries, or broad abstractions.
4. If new helper files are needed, keep them local and concrete (for example `internal/app/detail_selection.go` or `internal/app/shell_actions.go`) rather than introducing a new subsystem.
5. Preserve current user-facing behavior for help modal, action modal, detail loading, editor flow, launcher flow, and toast feedback.
6. Update scenario tests and golden files as needed.

## Files to Modify
- `internal/app/model.go` — remove duplicate selection sync and keep root update flow readable
- `internal/app/detail_selection.go` — create only if needed for focused detail/selection helpers
- `internal/app/shell_actions.go` — create only if needed for focused shell action helpers
- `internal/app/model_test.go` — update shell behavior coverage
- `internal/app/scenario_workflow_test.go` — update scenario coverage as needed
- `internal/app/testdata/*` — refresh affected golden files if behavior-preserving layout changes require it
- `internal/mode/board/model.go` — keep emitted selection flow aligned with the message-driven contract
- `internal/mode/search/model.go` — keep emitted selection flow aligned with the message-driven contract

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `SelectionChangedMsg` remains the only selection/detail synchronization mechanism between browse modes and the shell
- [ ] `syncSelectionFromControllers` or equivalent polling-based selection sync is removed
- [ ] `internal/app/model.go` no longer contains both `SelectionChangedMsg` handling and controller polling for the same state transition
- [ ] Any extracted helper files stay inside `internal/app` and do not add new abstractions or packages
- [ ] Existing help, modal, detail reload, editor, launcher, and toast behavior remains covered by tests
- [ ] Scenario and golden tests pass after the refactor
close_reason: Removed polling selection sync from app shell; selection/detail now synchronized solely via SelectionChangedMsg and app tests pass.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review finding — acceptance criterion is testable but the 'which mechanism wins' decision is unspecified. What: the instructions say 'pick a single selection sync mechanism' but do not state which one the tasker should pick. The two mechanisms are: (A) SelectionChangedMsg emitted by board/search after state changes, handled at model.go line 302; (B) syncSelectionFromControllers() polling via type-asserted CurrentSelection() at model.go line 159/483. Why: this is a design decision that materially affects how board/search communicate with the shell. If the tasker picks the wrong one, they either add coupling or break the Bubble Tea message-passing pattern. The message-based approach (A) is clearly better because it follows Bubble Tea conventions and eliminates the type assertion polling code. Suggested action: state in instructions that the message-based path (SelectionChangedMsg) should be kept and the polling path (syncSelectionFromControllers) should be removed. This removes ambiguity without adding scope.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review finding — extraction scope is vague. What: instruction 2 says 'extract focused helpers or files inside internal/app for selection and detail coordination and for shell owned action flows where that meaningfully shrinks model.go'. No concrete split point or target file count is given. Why: a tasker could extract anything from nothing to ten files. This is the kind of instruction that leads to over-engineering or analysis paralysis. The real goal is: keep model.go's Update function readable after removing the dual sync. Suggested action: tighten to something like 'extract detail coordination (ensureDetailForCurrentSelectionCmd and related helpers) into a focused file if it meaningfully reduces model.go line count; do not create new packages or new abstractions.'

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review question — dependency on wz9.1 may be over-constrained. What: wz9.2 depends on wz9.1. The selection unification (removing syncSelectionFromControllers polling) could technically be done before or independently of flattening mode.Controller storage. The polling code type-asserts through mode.Controller, yes, but removing the polling path and keeping only SelectionChangedMsg does not require the Controller interface to be gone first. Why: if the dependency is hard, these two tasks are serial and the critical path is longer. If the dependency could be relaxed, a tasker could work on wz9.2 in parallel with wz9.1 (or even first). Suggested action: consider whether the dependency should be 'soft' (recommended ordering) rather than 'hard' (blocks). If wz9.1 lands first the cleanup is cleaner, but wz9.2 is independently viable.

title: Replace map based bd decoding with typed payloads in internal/gateway/beads
description:
## Description
Stable `bd --json` output justifies typed command payloads. Keep the gateway boundary but make the CLI adapter simpler and safer.

## Instructions
1. Introduce typed payload structs for the `bd` read commands used by the app, including `list`, `ready`, `blocked`, `show`, `search`, `statuses`, `types`, and `label list-all` as needed.
2. Refactor `internal/gateway/beads/read_gateway.go` to decode into those structs instead of `map[string]any` and field by field extraction helpers.
3. Keep gateway level validation, pagination, and error normalization behavior intact.
4. Update tests and fixtures to cover the typed mapping paths.

## Files to Modify
- `internal/gateway/beads/read_gateway.go` — replace generic map decoding with typed payload mapping
- `internal/gateway/beads/*.go` — add typed payload definitions and helpers as needed
- `internal/gateway/beads/read_gateway_test.go` — update gateway read tests
- `internal/gateway/beads/testdata/*` — refresh or extend fixtures as needed
- `internal/domain/issues.go` — update read models only if typed mapping exposes needed fields already present in stable payloads

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Primary gateway read paths no longer decode command output through `map[string]any`
- [ ] `BeadsGateway` interface remains unchanged
- [ ] Existing list, ready, blocked, show, search, and catalog behavior stays covered by tests
- [ ] Error and pagination behavior remains unchanged from the caller perspective
close_reason: Implemented typed bd read payload decoding in gateway; removed map-based extraction helpers; validated with gateway package tests.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review finding — scope is clear but write paths are excluded without justification. What: the task targets read paths only. writes.go also likely uses similar patterns. Why: if writes.go uses map[string]any or untyped patterns, excluding it from this task means the gateway still has mixed decoding strategies after the epic closes. The acceptance criterion says 'primary read paths no longer decode through map[string]any' which is fine as scoping language — but the epic success criteria says 'internal/gateway/beads decodes stable bd --json payloads through typed structs instead of generic maps' without qualifying read-only. Suggested action: either (1) verify writes.go does not decode JSON responses through maps and document that it is already clean, or (2) if write commands return JSON output that gets decoded through maps, include those paths in scope.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Correction to previous comment — writes.go is clean. Verified: CreateIssue reads stdout as plain string (line 52), UpdateIssue/CloseIssue/AddComment ignore stdout. No map[string]any decoding in write paths. The read-only scope is correct and the epic success criteria alignment is fine.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review finding — bd --json stability assumption should be pinned. What: the task description says 'stable bd --json output justifies typed command payloads' but does not specify what version of bd this was validated against or how to detect breaking changes. Why: if bd updates its JSON schema, typed structs will silently drop new fields (fine) or fail on removed/renamed fields (not fine). The current map-based approach is ironically more resilient to schema drift. Typed decoding is still the right call, but the task should acknowledge the tradeoff. Suggested action: add a note in instructions: 'use json tags with omitempty where appropriate so optional/new fields do not cause hard failures. Consider adding a lightweight integration smoke test that runs bd list --json against a real beads project to catch schema drift.'

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review finding — helper function cleanup scope is implicit. What: read_gateway.go lines 552-896 contain ~15 helper functions (stringFromMap, intFromMap, optionalStringFromMap, timestampFromMap, mapArrayFromMap, etc.) that exist solely to extract fields from map[string]any. These should be deleted when typed decoding replaces them. Why: if the helpers survive alongside typed structs, the codebase has dead code and the refactor is incomplete. Suggested action: add explicit acceptance criterion: 'generic map extraction helpers (stringFromMap, intFromMap, etc.) are removed or reduced to the minimum needed for any remaining untyped paths.'

title: Flatten top level mode ownership in internal/app and internal/mode
description:
## Description
Remove the weak generic mode controller boundary. The shell should own concrete board and search controllers plus the shell-owned detail model.

## Instructions
1. Update `internal/app/model.go` to store concrete board and search controllers directly instead of generic `mode.Controller` values.
2. Update `internal/mode/board/model.go` and `internal/mode/search/model.go` so their `Update` methods no longer return `mode.Controller`. Use explicit concrete return types or another package-local non-generic convention that keeps ownership concrete.
3. Refactor shell message forwarding in `internal/app/model.go`, including board/search forwarding helpers, to call concrete update methods and reassign concrete controller state without polymorphic controller storage.
4. Reduce `internal/mode/contracts.go` to the smallest shared IDs, messages, and selection types still needed after the refactor. Remove controller interface machinery if it is no longer needed.
5. Eliminate the current shell-side capability recovery paths that type-assert through `mode.Controller`: selection provider, shell key capture, loading reporter, and result counter.
6. Keep detail state shell-owned in `internal/mode/details` and do not introduce a new top-level mode registry or replacement generic controller abstraction.
7. Update board/search constructors, message flows, and tests to match the explicit ownership model.

## Files to Modify
- `internal/app/model.go` — update shell ownership, forwarding, and remove generic controller usage
- `internal/mode/contracts.go` — remove or shrink generic controller contract
- `internal/mode/board/model.go` — align concrete board ownership and update return convention
- `internal/mode/search/model.go` — align concrete search ownership and update return convention
- `internal/mode/details/model.go` — keep detail state contract explicit
- `internal/app/model_test.go` — update shell tests
- `internal/mode/board/model_test.go` — update board tests as needed
- `internal/mode/search/model_test.go` — update search tests as needed

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `internal/app/model.go` no longer stores board and search as `mode.Controller`
- [ ] Board and search update wiring no longer depends on `mode.Controller` return values
- [ ] Shell code no longer uses type assertions for selection provider, shell key capture, loading reporter, or result counter
- [ ] Board, search, and detail behavior remains unchanged in existing tests or updated equivalent coverage
- [ ] No new top level mode abstraction is introduced
close_reason: Replaced generic mode.Controller shell wiring with concrete board/search ownership; updated mode contracts and tests for concrete update flow.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review finding — Update signature change not addressed. What: board.Model.Update() returns (mode.Controller, tea.Cmd) at line 96 and search.Model.Update() returns (mode.Controller, tea.Cmd) at line 76. If wz9.1 removes mode.Controller, these return types must change. The task instructions say 'update board and search constructors, message flows, and tests' but do not explicitly call out the Update() return type change. Why: the tasker needs to know that removing mode.Controller means choosing a new return convention for board/search Update methods. Returning concrete *board.Model/*search.Model is the obvious answer, but the shell then needs to store and call them differently (no more polymorphic Update). This is the core mechanical challenge of wz9.1 and the instructions should be explicit. Suggested action: add to instructions: 'Update board.Model.Update() and search.Model.Update() to return concrete types instead of mode.Controller. Update shell message forwarding in model.go accordingly.'

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review finding — type assertion inventory is incomplete. What: the task says 'shell code no longer uses type assertions to recover board or search capabilities'. The actual type assertions in model.go are: (1) selectionProvider at line 494-496, (2) searchShellKeyCapture at line 329-331, (3) loadingReporter at lines 674-676 and 690-692, (4) resultCounter at lines 708-710. That is four distinct ad-hoc interfaces being type-asserted through mode.Controller. Why: the tasker needs to know the full inventory to verify the acceptance criterion. If even one is missed, the acceptance review will fail. Suggested action: list these four type assertions explicitly in the instructions or files-to-modify section so the tasker has a checklist.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review finding — forwardBoardMessage/forwardSearchMessage will need structural change. What: lines 726-744 forward messages to m.board and m.search through mode.Controller interface (m.board.Update(msg) returns (Controller, tea.Cmd) and reassigns m.board). With concrete types, this forwarding must change to call board/search-specific Update methods and handle concrete return types. Why: this is load-bearing shell wiring, not just cosmetic. The forwarding pattern plus shouldForwardToBoard/shouldForwardToSearch is where the rubber meets the road. Suggested action: explicitly note in instructions that message forwarding in model.go must be refactored alongside the storage change.

title: Simplify UI core architecture and harden package boundaries
description:
## Description
Simplify the UI core architecture before more dashboard provider work lands. Preserve the intentionally small product shape while removing accidental complexity in shell coordination and CLI decoding.

## Goals
- Keep `BeadsGateway` as the only issue IO boundary
- Flatten board, search, and detail ownership instead of carrying a weak generic top level mode abstraction
- Replace map based `bd --json` decoding with typed payload structs
- Preserve the dashboard provider seam but tighten its contract for the upcoming second provider

## Non Goals
- Add new top level modes
- Introduce plugin architecture or arbitrary dashboard query execution
- Change the launcher or editor product flows

## Success Criteria
- [ ] `internal/app` and `internal/mode` no longer rely on a weak generic controller abstraction plus recovery type assertions
- [ ] Selection and detail synchronization use one clear ownership model
- [ ] `internal/gateway/beads` decodes stable `bd --json` payloads through typed structs instead of generic maps
- [ ] `internal/dashboard` has an explicit validated contract that supports multiple providers without widening scope
- [ ] `docs/CODING.md` and `project-plan/ARCHITECTURE.md` match the resulting package boundaries
- [ ] Acceptance review confirms build, vet, and test guardrails still pass
close_reason: all steps complete

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Per package planning decision: create refactor tasks for internal/app, internal/mode, internal/gateway/beads, internal/dashboard, and docs only. Keep cmd/bwb, internal/config, internal/launcher, and internal/ui stable unless follow on implementation reveals coupling that the review did not surface.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Planning note: acceptance is modeled as child task beads-workbench-wz9.6 because bd epics cannot depend on tasks. The acceptance review task is the blocking gate for epic closure after implementation tasks finish.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review finding — dual selection sync is real and clearly visible. What: model.go lines 159-161 show syncSelectionFromControllers() polling via type assertion (lines 489-515), AND lines 302-310 handle SelectionChangedMsg from controllers. These are two independent sync paths for the same state. Both board and search emit SelectionChangedMsg AND expose CurrentSelection() via type-asserted interface. Why: wz9.2 is correctly scoped to address this — confirmed the problem is not hypothetical. No action needed on epic.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review finding — scope boundary is correct but tight. What: epic comment says cmd/bwb, internal/config, internal/launcher, internal/ui should stay stable. This is true today but wz9.1 will change the mode.Controller interface which board/model.go and search/model.go both implement (var _ mode.Controller = (*Model)(nil)). The board also imports dashboard and gateway packages. Why: the file lists on child tasks already cover these, so no scope hole here. Just flagging that the epic comment slightly understates the blast radius — wz9.1 touches mode/board and mode/search, not just app and mode/contracts. Suggested action: none, child tasks already capture this correctly.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review finding — wz9.3 and wz9.4 are correctly independent of wz9.1/wz9.2. This is good — it means gateway decoding and dashboard contract work can proceed in parallel with the app/mode refactoring. The parallelism is real and reduces critical path length. Three parallel tracks: (1) wz9.1 → wz9.2 (serial, app/mode), (2) wz9.3 (independent, gateway), (3) wz9.4 (independent, dashboard). All three converge at wz9.5 (docs) → wz9.6 (acceptance). This is well-structured.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review finding — simplification opportunity: could wz9.1 and wz9.2 be one task? What: wz9.1 removes mode.Controller storage and type assertions. wz9.2 removes the dual selection sync that relied on those type assertions. After wz9.1, the polling-based syncSelectionFromControllers cannot exist in its current form (the type assertion through mode.Controller is gone). So wz9.2's primary target is partially destroyed by wz9.1. Why: splitting closely coupled work into two tasks creates a false checkpoint — wz9.1 cannot be 'done' without also addressing the selection sync path that depends on the removed interface. A tasker completing wz9.1 would need to either break the polling path or convert it, which is wz9.2's job. Suggested action: consider merging wz9.1 and wz9.2 into one task. The combined scope is still manageable (one file cluster, one conceptual change: 'flatten shell ownership and pick one selection sync mechanism'). If you want to keep them separate for review granularity, explicitly document that wz9.1 may leave syncSelectionFromControllers in a temporarily broken/stubbed state until wz9.2 lands.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Reviewer feedback incorporated. Tightened wz9.1 around concrete Update return wiring, forwarding, and the full shell type-assertion inventory; tightened wz9.2 to keep  as the single selection path and remove polling; tightened wz9.4 to require standalone dashboard validation with enumerated invalid states; added minor docs and acceptance clarifications in wz9.5 and wz9.6. Kept wz9.1 and wz9.2 separate and ordered because both heavily touch  and the concrete ownership refactor is the intended foundation before selection-flow cleanup.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Correction to prior planning note: reviewer feedback incorporated. Tightened wz9.1 around concrete Update return wiring, message forwarding, and the full shell type-assertion inventory. Tightened wz9.2 to keep SelectionChangedMsg as the single selection path and remove polling via syncSelectionFromControllers. Tightened wz9.4 to require standalone dashboard validation with enumerated invalid states. Added minor clarifications in wz9.5 (conditional docs/TESTING.md update) and wz9.6 (explicit architecture guardrail test). Kept wz9.1 and wz9.2 separate and ordered because both heavily touch internal/app/model.go and the concrete ownership refactor is the intended foundation before selection-flow cleanup.

title: Detail inspector shows owner instead of assignee
description:
## Description
The structured detail inspector reads the issue owner into `IssueSummary.Assignee` when `bd show --json` includes both `owner` and `assignee`. As a result, the right rail displays the creator/owner instead of the actual assignee for real issues.

## Evidence
- Runtime verification against seeded fixture showed `Assignee  : hans.kohlreiter@dynatrace.com` for `bwf-2`, while `bd show bwf-2 --json` reports `assignee: bob` and `owner: hans.kohlreiter@dynatrace.com`.
- Gateway decoding in `internal/gateway/beads/read_gateway.go` currently prefers `owner` before `assignee`.

## Impact
This breaks the epic success criterion that the right rail correctly covers assignee data.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Gateway decoding prefers the real assignee field when present
- [ ] Detail right rail shows the actual assignee from `bd show --json`
- [ ] Automated coverage fails if owner is shown in place of assignee
close_reason: Fixed assignee decoding precedence to prefer assignee with owner fallback; added gateway and detail-view coverage to prevent owner appearing as assignee.

title: Fix GitHub releases to publish installable binaries for mise
description:
## Description
GitHub release `v0.1.0` exists without any attached binary assets, so `mise install github:hk9890/beads-workbench@0.1.0` fails with `No matching asset found for platform linux-x64`.

## Impact
- Release artifacts are unusable for binary installation.
- The documented release process creates tags/releases without shipping binaries.
- Current and future users cannot install `bwb` via mise GitHub backend.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Run `mise install github:hk9890/beads-workbench@0.1.0` on Linux x64.
2. Observe failure because the release has no matching assets.

## Fix Requirements
1. Add release automation that builds archives for supported platforms and attaches them to GitHub releases.
2. Ensure archive naming is compatible with mise's GitHub backend platform matching.
3. Update release documentation to reflect the automated artifact publishing flow.
4. Backfill `v0.1.0` release assets so the existing release is installable.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Repository contains automated release packaging/upload configuration.
- [ ] Release documentation explains how binaries are produced and verified.
- [ ] GitHub release `v0.1.0` has installable assets for at least linux amd64 and darwin arm64/amd64.
- [ ] `mise install github:hk9890/beads-workbench@0.1.0` succeeds on supported platforms once assets are available.

## Files to Modify
- .github/workflows/ (add/update release workflow)
- release packaging config file (create)
- docs/RELEASING.md (update)
close_reason: Closed

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Investigation: release tag v0.1.0 exists on GitHub but has zero attached assets. The repository currently has CI only and a manual release doc, but no packaging/upload automation (no GoReleaser config and no release workflow). This is why mise github backend cannot match linux-x64 assets. Plan: add tag-triggered GitHub release automation that builds archives for supported platforms, update release docs, and backfill v0.1.0 assets.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
