comments.text: Update after fix: repository-side release problem is corrected. v0.1.0 now has uploaded assets (linux/macos x64+arm64 plus checksums), and the repo now has tag-triggered GoReleaser automation. However, local verification with mise 2026.3.5 still fails and reports an empty available-asset list even though GitHub API/gh release view show the assets. This points to an additional mise/private-GitHub backend issue or stale/private-auth behavior outside this repo. The repo-side bug remains fixed for future releases and for consumers that can see private release assets correctly.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Verification complete: tag-triggered release automation is in the repo, v0.1.0 now has linux/macos assets attached, and mise install succeeds when using a fresh cache. Existing local mise caches created before the backfill can remain stale; on a fresh machine or after clearing the GitHub release cache, install works.

title: Acceptance Review: Rich markdown viewing for issue content
description:
## Description
Verify the markdown viewing epic outcome before the epic is closed.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Shared markdown renderer is used for read only issue viewing
- [ ] Detail view renders description and notes with markdown styling
- [ ] Width handling and fallback behavior are covered by tests
- [ ] No editing or external editor behavior changed
close_reason: All criteria verified

title: Add shared markdown renderer for read only issue viewing
description:
## Description
Create a shared markdown renderer package for Beads Workbench viewing surfaces. Use a terminal focused Go library rather than hand formatting markdown in each component. This task is strictly about read only viewing inside the app.

## Instructions
1. Add a dedicated markdown rendering package under internal/ui/shared or equivalent.
2. Prefer charmbracelet/glamour as the default integration choice for ANSI terminal markdown rendering.
3. Expose a small width aware API for read only rendering.
4. Include deterministic fallback behavior when rendering fails, content is empty, or input is effectively plain text.
5. Decide and document the renderer test strategy up front: either assert raw ANSI output intentionally or strip ANSI sequences and assert readable text plus structural markers.
6. Flag that downstream detail and search goldens may need refresh after integration.
7. Do not touch external editing or issue edit document flows.

## Files to Modify
- go.mod
- go.sum
- internal/ui/shared/markdown/renderer.go
- internal/ui/shared/markdown/renderer_test.go

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Shared markdown renderer package exists
- [ ] Renderer is width aware and test covered
- [ ] Fallback behavior is documented in tests
- [ ] Plain text content remains at least as readable as the current plain text rendering path
- [ ] Renderer test strategy is explicit and stable for future golden or assertion based tests
- [ ] Scope remains viewing only
design: Prefer charmbracelet/glamour for ANSI terminal markdown viewing. goldmark is a strong parser but would require more custom terminal rendering work. chroma is only a future option if fenced code rendering needs extra control.
close_reason: Implemented shared read-only markdown renderer with width-aware API, deterministic fallback behavior, and focused stable tests using glamour integration.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Design note: glamour ANSI output can break golden test comparisons. What: glamour produces ANSI escape sequences in its output. The project relies heavily on golden file testing (details_test.go, search_test.go) with AssertMatchesGolden and AssertMatchesGoldenNormalized. Why: all existing golden files for detail rendering will need regeneration, and the test strategy for the renderer itself needs to decide whether to test raw ANSI output or stripped output. Suggested action: add a testing note to spi.1 instructions specifying whether the renderer tests will use golden files with ANSI output or test via stripped-text assertions, and flag that spi.2 will invalidate existing detail goldens.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Acceptance criteria gap: no criterion for what happens when glamour is given non-markdown plain text. What: beads issue descriptions are not guaranteed to be valid markdown — some may be plain text or mixed. Why: if the renderer chokes on plain text or produces worse output than the current renderMultiline, that is a regression. The fallback criterion says 'when rendering fails' but doesn't cover the case where rendering 'succeeds' but produces ugly output for non-markdown input. Suggested action: add an acceptance criterion like 'plain text content renders at least as readably as the current plain text renderer'.

title: Apply markdown viewing to issue detail content
description:
## Description
Apply the shared markdown renderer to the issue detail presentation so description and notes are displayed as styled markdown inside the app.

## Instructions
1. Update detail view rendering to call the shared markdown package for description and notes.
2. Preserve compact detail behavior and sensible truncation on narrow widths.
3. Keep comments and metadata readable; comment bodies remain plain text in this task and any markdown comment rendering is out of scope for now.
4. Keep scrolling stable in detail mode after the rendered output changes.
5. Verify the compact detail path used by search preview remains readable after markdown integration.
6. Revisit line-count cache assumptions in internal/mode/details/model.go so width dependent markdown rendering does not produce stale scroll calculations.
7. Do not change any editing flows.

## Files to Modify
- internal/ui/details/details.go
- internal/ui/details/details_test.go
- internal/mode/details/model.go
- internal/ui/search/search.go
- internal/ui/search/search_test.go

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Description and notes render as markdown in the detail surface
- [ ] Compact and full detail modes still render within width constraints
- [ ] Search preview remains coherent after compact detail markdown rendering changes
- [ ] Detail scroll behavior remains correct
- [ ] Comment bodies remain readable without expanding scope into markdown comment rendering
- [ ] No editing behavior changes
close_reason: Implemented markdown rendering for detail description/notes with compact/search preview coherence and width-aware scroll cache fixes; tests and quality gates pass.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Missing scope: compact detail path in search preview will also render markdown. What: spi.2 lists files to modify including details.go and mode/details/model.go, but the search preview at internal/ui/search/search.go lines 174-201 calls uidetails.Render with Compact: true. If markdown rendering changes the output of Render(), search preview changes implicitly. Why: the implementer needs to know that search preview is a consumer of the compact detail path and must verify its appearance after markdown integration. Suggested action: add a note in spi.2 instructions that search preview uses compact detail rendering and must be visually verified, or add internal/ui/search/search_test.go to the files-to-modify list.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Scroll cache invalidation concern. What: mode/details/model.go uses a content-based cache (cacheMatchesCurrentState with reflect.DeepEqual) to avoid re-rendering for scroll calculations. After markdown rendering, the same IssueDetail will produce different rendered output if the renderer is width-dependent or style-dependent. Why: the cache key is the raw domain data, not the rendered output. If glamour rendering introduces non-determinism (e.g., different terminal color capabilities) or if the width/style changes, the cached line count will be stale and scrolling will break. Suggested action: verify that the line-count cache invalidation still works correctly after markdown integration, and consider whether the cache key needs to include a renderer version or style hash.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Missing: comments rendering under markdown. What: instruction #3 says 'Keep comments and metadata readable even if they stay plain text for now' but this is ambiguous about whether comment bodies should also get markdown rendering. Comment bodies in beads often contain markdown-formatted text. Why: if comments are excluded from markdown rendering in spi.2 but included later, that is a separate task not tracked anywhere. If they should be included, the acceptance criteria need to cover it. Suggested action: make an explicit decision — either add 'comment bodies also render as markdown' to spi.2 scope, or create a follow-up task for it.

title: Rich markdown viewing for issue content
description:
## Description
Replace plain text issue content rendering in Beads Workbench with rich ANSI markdown viewing for read only surfaces. Scope is viewing only inside the app. External edit documents and editor flows stay unchanged.

## Goals
- Render issue description and notes with readable markdown styling
- Reuse one shared renderer across viewing surfaces
- Keep graceful fallback when markdown rendering fails

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Shared markdown renderer exists for read only TUI viewing
- [ ] Detail view uses markdown rendering for description and notes
- [ ] Viewing remains width aware and test covered
- [ ] No editing or external editor behavior changes
close_reason: all steps complete

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Simplification question: could spi.1 and spi.2 be a single task? What: spi.1 creates the renderer package, spi.2 wires it into the detail view. These are the only two implementation tasks and they will almost certainly be done by the same person in the same session. Why: splitting renderer creation from renderer integration adds coordination overhead (acceptance review sees partial integration), and the renderer API cannot be validated without a consumer. A renderer that is only tested in isolation might have the wrong API shape. Suggested action: consider merging spi.1 and spi.2 into a single task 'Add markdown rendering to issue detail viewing' that delivers both the package and the integration. The acceptance review stays separate.

title: Missing CI workflow/provenance tracking for release reconciliation
description:
## Description
Release reconciliation found that this repository has no `.github/workflows` directory and no GitHub Actions runs, which means releases have no automated CI provenance. That is a process gap: future releases depend entirely on local manual verification and there is no repository-native signal showing that build, vet, and test passed on the candidate commit.

## Evidence
- Release reconciliation task `beads-workbench-j8m.1` confirmed local quality gates passed.
- The same task also confirmed there are no GitHub Actions workflows and no recorded runs.
- `docs/CODING.md` defines the expected quality gates, but the repository does not automate them.
- `go.mod` currently declares Go `1.24.2`, so CI should use that toolchain series unless the repository deliberately chooses a different supported policy.

## Instructions
1. Add a minimal GitHub Actions CI workflow under `.github/workflows/` for this Go repository.
2. Trigger it on at least `push` and `pull_request`.
3. Configure the workflow to use the Go version from `go.mod` (`1.24.2`, or the corresponding `1.24.x` setup-go version syntax if required by the action).
4. Have the workflow run the repository's baseline quality gates:
   - `go build ./cmd/bwb`
   - `go vet ./...`
   - `go test ./...`
5. Name the workflow clearly so future release verification can reference it.
6. Note in the implementation or issue comment that this repository is currently private, so GitHub Actions minute usage should be considered; this is not a blocker to adding CI, but it should not be ignored.
7. If `docs/RELEASING.md` exists by the time this lands, update it to reference the CI workflow as part of release provenance; otherwise note the expected doc follow-up in the issue comment.
8. If workflow setup reveals repository-specific constraints, document them in the issue and create follow-up work rather than silently narrowing scope.

## Files to Modify
- `.github/workflows/ci.yml` (create)
- `docs/RELEASING.md` (update if present; otherwise record follow-up in issue comment)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A GitHub Actions workflow exists under `.github/workflows/`
- [ ] The workflow runs on `push` and `pull_request`
- [ ] The workflow uses the Go version policy derived from `go.mod`
- [ ] The workflow executes `go build ./cmd/bwb`, `go vet ./...`, and `go test ./...`
- [ ] The workflow is named/documented clearly enough to use as release provenance
- [ ] Private-repo GitHub Actions usage is acknowledged in the implementation notes or issue comment
- [ ] Any repository constraint discovered during setup is tracked explicitly rather than hidden
acceptance_criteria: Current CI/provenance gap is explicitly recorded from the verified evidence (no .github/workflows and no workflow runs); A concrete decision exists for future release provenance (either add CI or explicitly document manual-only release verification); Follow-up implementation/documentation work needed to satisfy that decision is identified and linked
close_reason: Added CI workflow for push/pull_request with go.mod-derived Go policy, wired build/vet/test gates, and updated release docs to use CI run as provenance evidence.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Type mismatch: bug vs task. What: This is typed as 'bug' but it describes a missing capability (no CI workflow), not broken existing functionality. The repo never had CI, so nothing is regressed. Why: Bug type implies a defect to fix, which sets the wrong expectation for triaging and prioritization. Missing CI on a project that never had it is a task or chore, not a bug. Suggested action: Retype to 'task'.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Contradictory acceptance criteria. What: The description body has 5 concrete AC checkboxes about creating a CI workflow. The separate 'ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA' field says something completely different — it talks about 'recording the gap' and 'making a decision for future provenance' as if this were a discovery/decision task, not an implementation task. These two sets of AC point at different scopes of work. Why: An agent executing this will not know which AC to satisfy. The body says 'build the workflow,' the field says 'record a decision.' Suggested action: Align on one scope. If this is 'implement CI,' delete the decision-oriented AC field and keep the implementation checkboxes. If this is 'decide on CI strategy,' rewrite the body to match.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Missing dependency on private repo constraint. What: This issue says to add GitHub Actions CI, but beads-workbench-2rj flags that the repo is currently private. GitHub Actions minutes for private repos are limited (2000 min/mo on free plan). The issue does not acknowledge this constraint or ask whether CI should be added before the visibility decision. Why: An agent could add CI that silently burns through Actions minutes on a private repo, or the CI may behave differently once the repo goes public. Suggested action: Add a note acknowledging the private-repo Actions-minutes constraint, or add beads-workbench-2rj as a soft dependency (not blocking, but awareness).

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Go version not specified. What: The instructions say to create a CI workflow running go build/vet/test but do not specify which Go version to use. There is no go.mod-level Go version constraint mentioned either. Why: An agent will have to guess the Go version for the workflow matrix. Wrong version choice could cause CI to pass/fail differently than local. Suggested action: Check go.mod for the Go version directive and explicitly require the workflow to use that version.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Implemented CI provenance workflow: added .github/workflows/ci.yml (push + pull_request) using actions/setup-go with go-version-file=go.mod (go 1.24.2 policy), and running build/vet/test gates. Updated docs/RELEASING.md to reference the CI workflow as release provenance. Note: repo is currently private, so Actions minute usage should be monitored.

title: Document release process in docs/RELEASING.md
description:
## Description
The repository has no `docs/RELEASING.md`, so the release process currently lives only in operator memory and ad-hoc issue comments. Add a concrete releasing guide that captures the commands, checks, and expectations already used in this repository so future releases are repeatable.

## Instructions
1. Create `docs/RELEASING.md`.
2. Document the release prerequisites:
   - clean working tree
   - branch synced with remote
   - required quality gates from `docs/CODING.md` (`go build ./cmd/bwb`, `go vet ./...`, `go test ./...`)
3. Document when runtime verification is required for user-facing changes, linking to `docs/TESTING.md` and `docs/RUNTIME_UI_VERIFICATION.md`.
4. Document the release steps for this repository, including:
   - choosing the version/tag
   - confirming whether release notes/changelog updates are needed
   - creating/pushing the tag
   - creating or editing the GitHub release with `gh release`
   - post-release verification
5. Explicitly note the current CI reality discovered during release reconciliation: there is no `.github/workflows` automation yet, so releases currently rely on manual local verification until CI is added.
6. Explicitly note that release visibility/public-vs-private policy is still unresolved and tracked separately in `beads-workbench-2rj`; the guide should avoid inventing a policy and instead mark that decision point clearly.
7. Update `README.md` to link to `docs/RELEASING.md`.
8. Keep the document command-oriented and specific to this repo rather than generic release advice.

## Files to Modify
- `docs/RELEASING.md` (create)
- `README.md` (update: add releasing guide link)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `docs/RELEASING.md` exists
- [ ] The guide includes the exact quality-gate commands from `docs/CODING.md`
- [ ] The guide links to runtime verification guidance for user-facing changes
- [ ] The guide documents the actual `gh`/git release flow used by this repo
- [ ] The guide calls out the current absence of CI automation
- [ ] The guide notes that release visibility policy is still pending `beads-workbench-2rj`
- [ ] `README.md` links to the releasing guide
close_reason: Completed: added docs/RELEASING.md and README link; verified with go test ./...

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review pass: well-formed and executable. What: Instructions are specific, reference concrete existing docs, list exact files to create/modify, and AC checkboxes are testable. The conditional dependency on f51 is handled gracefully (instruction #5 says to note CI absence rather than block). This is the strongest issue of the four.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Missing ordering dependency on 2rj (visibility decision). What: Instruction #4 says to document the 'gh release' flow, and the releasing guide should presumably address whether releases are public or internal. But 2rj (visibility policy) is blocked on discussion and has no resolution yet. If vr5 lands first, it will either have to dodge the visibility question or document an incomplete release flow. Why: The releasing guide will be immediately stale or incomplete on the visibility dimension. Suggested action: Add a placeholder instruction acknowledging that release visibility policy is pending (2rj), and add an AC checkbox that the doc explicitly states the visibility model is TBD pending 2rj resolution. This way vr5 is not blocked but is honest.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Implemented release docs task: added docs/RELEASING.md with repo-specific manual git/gh flow, exact quality gates, runtime verification links, explicit no-CI note, and pending visibility policy note (beads-workbench-2rj). Updated README.md to link the new guide. Verification: go test ./... (pass).

title: Fix DisallowUnknownFields breaking on bd CLI upgrades
description:
## Description
`internal/gateway/beads/runner.go` (around line 136) uses `json.Decoder.DisallowUnknownFields()` when decoding `bd` CLI JSON output. This means any new field that `bd` adds to its JSON output will cause a hard decode error, breaking the app on minor `bd` upgrades. The gateway already handles dynamic fields via `map[string]any` extraction, making strict decoding both redundant and harmful.

## Instructions
1. Open `internal/gateway/beads/runner.go`
2. Find the `DisallowUnknownFields()` call (around line 136)
3. Remove it — the decoder should tolerate unknown fields
4. Verify existing gateway tests still pass (they test the fields we DO parse)

## Files to Modify
- `internal/gateway/beads/runner.go` (remove DisallowUnknownFields call)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `DisallowUnknownFields()` removed from JSON decoder
- [ ] `go test ./internal/gateway/beads/...` passes
- [ ] Gateway correctly parses JSON with extra unknown fields (existing tests cover known fields)
close_reason: Removed strict JSON unknown-field rejection in gateway decoder and verified gateway beads tests pass.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Priority should be P1, not P2. What: this bug means ANY new field bd adds to its JSON output hard-crashes the TUI. bd is actively developed and fields get added regularly. Why: the app will break on the next bd upgrade with zero user-actionable feedback (decode error). This is a ticking time bomb with real user impact — worse than the modal n-key bug (which requires a specific unusual key press). Suggested action: upgrade to P1.

title: Fix modal 'n' key bypassing Required guard
description:
## Description
In `internal/ui/modal/modal.go`, pressing `n` always emits `CancelMsg`, even when `config.Required == true`. The `Escape` key handler correctly checks `!m.config.Required` (line ~205), but the `n` shortcut (line ~219-222) skips this check entirely. Users can dismiss Required modals (e.g. confirmations) by pressing `n`.

## Instructions
1. Open `internal/ui/modal/modal.go`
2. Find the `n` key handler (around line 219-222) in the `Update` method
3. Add the same `!m.config.Required` guard that the Escape key uses:
   ```go
   case "n":
       if !m.config.Required {
           return m, func() tea.Msg { return CancelMsg{} }
       }
   ```
4. Add a test in `internal/ui/modal/modal_test.go` that verifies pressing `n` on a Required modal does NOT produce a `CancelMsg`

## Files to Modify
- `internal/ui/modal/modal.go` (add Required guard to `n` handler)
- `internal/ui/modal/modal_test.go` (add test for `n` on Required modal)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Pressing `n` on a Required modal does not emit CancelMsg
- [ ] Pressing `n` on a non-Required modal still emits CancelMsg (existing behavior preserved)
- [ ] Pressing Escape on a Required modal still blocked (existing behavior)
- [ ] New test covers the Required + `n` scenario
- [ ] `go test ./internal/ui/modal/...` passes
close_reason: Added Required guard to 'n' cancel shortcut and covered with regression test; modal package tests pass.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Solid task. Clear bug, clear fix, clear test criteria. One thing to verify: are there other shortcut keys besides 'n' and Escape that should also respect the Required guard? If 'y' bypasses Required too (forcing confirmation), that might be a separate issue — but 'y' confirming a Required modal is probably correct behavior. No action needed unless 'y' also has an unintended bypass path.

title: Fix process_runner.go env wipe bug
description:
## Description
`internal/launcher/process_runner.go` sets `cmd.Env` to ONLY the launcher's env entries, which **replaces** the parent process's entire environment (PATH, HOME, SHELL, etc.). Any launcher definition with `Env` entries produces a broken child process that can't find executables.

This is a real runtime bug — launching `opencode` or any custom launcher with env vars will fail silently or produce confusing "command not found" errors.

## Instructions
1. Open `internal/launcher/process_runner.go`
2. Find the line where `cmd.Env` is set (around line 19-20)
3. Change it to append launcher env entries to the parent environment:
   ```go
   cmd.Env = append(os.Environ(), envPairs...)
   ```
4. Add `"os"` to imports if not already present
5. Update `internal/launcher/process_runner_test.go` to verify that parent env vars are preserved when launcher env is set

## Files to Modify
- `internal/launcher/process_runner.go` (fix env assignment)
- `internal/launcher/process_runner_test.go` (add test for env inheritance)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `cmd.Env` includes parent environment plus launcher env entries
- [ ] New test verifies a parent env var (e.g. PATH) survives when launcher env is set
- [ ] Existing process_runner tests still pass
- [ ] `go test ./internal/launcher/...` passes
close_reason: Fixed process runner to merge parent env with launcher env and added regression test for env inheritance.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Solid task — clear, specific, testable, correct fix. One minor note: the instruction says 'Add os to imports if not already present' but os/exec is already imported (which is in the os subtree, not the os package itself). The tasker needs to add 'os' as a separate import since os.Environ() requires it. No action needed — just confirming this is a well-specified bug fix.

title: Code Review Cleanup: Bugs, Dead Code, Duplication, Test Gaps
description:
## Description
Comprehensive cleanup epic based on full codebase review. Addresses real bugs, removes ~300 lines of dead code, eliminates duplication (6+ helper functions written 3 times), fills test coverage gaps (overlay has zero tests), and tightens Go conventions.

## Goals
- Fix 3 real bugs (env wipe, modal bypass, strict JSON decode)
- Remove all identified dead code (~200+ lines)
- Eliminate DRY violations (maxInt x3, normalizeToken x2, containsString x3, test helpers x3)
- Fill critical test gaps (overlay, editor opener fallback, renderhelpers)
- Tighten Go conventions (naming, exports, error messages)

## Success Criteria
- [ ] All child tasks completed
- [ ] `go test ./...` passes
- [ ] `go vet ./...` clean
- [ ] `go build ./cmd/bwb` succeeds
- [ ] No regressions in existing test coverage
- [ ] Acceptance review task passed
close_reason: all steps complete

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Merge conflict risk in Wave 1: tasks eb3 (process_runner.go) and xcp (overlay tests) are safe, but 2t4 (dead code removal) touches 13 files including app/model.go, config/keybindings.go, overlay/overlay.go. Meanwhile xcp creates overlay_test.go to test overlay functions that 2t4 is deleting (BottomLeft, Top positions). If a tasker runs xcp and 2t4 concurrently, xcp might write tests for code 2t4 removes. Why: the plan declares all Wave 1 tasks as 'independent and parallel' but xcp and 2t4 conflict on the overlay package. Suggested action: either add a dependency (xcp blocked by 2t4, or vice versa) or explicitly note in both tasks that overlay.BottomLeft and overlay.Top are being deleted and tests should only cover surviving positions (Center, Bottom).

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Missing finding from code review: 'issue edit edge cases: missing (ClearLabels, priority boundaries, assignee clearing)' was identified as a test gap but no task covers it. ClearLabels IS implemented in the production code (domain/mutations.go:28, writes.go:106, app/model.go:1229) and has one basic test in writes_test.go:141, but priority boundary validation and assignee clearing have zero test coverage. Why: these are write-path behaviors that affect data integrity. Suggested action: add this to 9uk's scope, or create a dedicated task. It's small — 3-5 test cases in writes_test.go and issue_edit_document_test.go.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Dependency chain question: why does qyz (extract test helpers) depend on 2t4 (remove dead code)? The test helpers being extracted (hasGatewayCall, controllerAdapter, ansiEscapePattern, FakeProcessRunner) are not dead code — they're live duplicated code in test files. Dead code removal doesn't touch test helper files. Why: this artificial dependency means qyz can't start until the entire 13-file dead code removal completes, delaying Wave 2 by the duration of 2t4. Suggested action: verify whether qyz actually depends on 2t4. If the dependency is 'dead code removal might change test files that qyz modifies' — that's a merge conflict concern, not a logical dependency. If there's no real dependency, remove it and move qyz to Wave 1.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Follow-up on qyz→2t4 dependency: verified that 2t4's test file modifications (builtin_provider_test.go, format_test.go) do NOT overlap with qyz's test files (model_test.go in app/mode/search/board, service_test.go in launcher, row_test.go, etc.). There is no file-level conflict. The dependency appears unnecessary. Removing it would let qyz run in Wave 1, which would also unblock 9uk sooner (Wave 2 instead of Wave 3). Net effect: the 4-wave plan could collapse to 3 waves.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Simplification opportunity: 14 tasks can be reduced to 11-12. (1) Merge ia6 (gateway dedup) and 0jc instruction #4 (buildFilterArgs) — they're the same work. Remove instruction #4 from 0jc. (2) If qyz→2t4 dependency is removed, the 4-wave structure collapses to 3 waves, reducing total elapsed time. (3) Consider merging 0jc instruction #2 (containsString→slices.Contains in read_gateway.go) into ia6 since it's the same file. This leaves 0jc focused on maxInt, normalizeToken, and MergeKeyBindings — cleaner scope.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Overall assessment: the plan is solid and well-structured for a code review cleanup. The breakdown is logical, priorities are mostly right, and task descriptions are detailed enough for agent execution. Main issues found: (1) ia6/0jc have duplicate scope on buildFilterArgs, (2) xcp/2t4 conflict on overlay package, (3) qyz→2t4 dependency appears spurious and adds an unnecessary wave, (4) rit has wrong field names in code sample, (5) 2t4 has wrong terminology (calls constants 'functions'), (6) 0jc references a file (renderhelpers) that doesn't have the code it claims, (7) ehq should be P1 not P2, (8) issue edit edge case test coverage is missing from the plan. None of these are plan-killers — they're fixable with task description updates before execution begins.

title: Code review: modal.go 'y'/'n' shortcut keys bypass Required validation for 'n' case
description: modal/modal.go:219-222: pressing 'n' when not in an input field always emits CancelMsg regardless of whether config.Required is true. But line 204-207 for the Escape key respects Required and blocks cancel. This is inconsistent: Required should mean the user can't dismiss via any path, but 'n' bypasses that guard. Suggested action: add 'if !m.config.Required' check before the 'n' cancel path, matching the Escape behavior on line 205.
close_reason: Covered by beads-workbench-6am in cleanup epic

title: Code review: overlay package has zero tests
description: internal/ui/overlay/overlay.go has 99 lines of ANSI-aware overlay logic (Place, calculatePosition) with no tests at all. This is the foundation for both toaster and modal overlay rendering. The position calculation has boundary clamping, padding math, and ANSI-width-aware string slicing that are all untested. A bug here silently corrupts both toaster and modal UI. Suggested action: add unit tests for Place() covering at minimum: center placement, bottom placement, foreground wider than background, negative offset clamping, and ANSI-styled foreground strings.
close_reason: Covered by beads-workbench-xcp in cleanup epic

title: Verifier: board and search still use separate compact row renderers
description:
## Description
Verifier review of beads-workbench-ekp found that board and search still assemble compact issue rows through separate code paths.

## Evidence
- internal/ui/board/board.go:202-235 still defines renderRowLine(Row, maxWidth)
- internal/ui/search/search.go:161-183 still assembles prefix/meta/title inline inside renderResultsContent
- internal/ui/shared/ contains only renderhelpers/helpers.go; there is no shared row component
- board still uses local uiboard.Row input while search renders domain.IssueSummary directly
- id width logic still diverges: board caps with minInt(12, MaxInt(7, maxWidth/5)) while search uses MaxInt(7, width/5) with no cap

## Why
This fails the epic expectation that board and search share one compact row renderer implementation path, leaves row-level duplication in place, and keeps the input/id-width decisions implicit instead of explicit.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Board and search call the same shared compact row renderer for selection indicator, type/priority/status/ID token assembly, and width-aware title truncation.
- [ ] Input type choice is explicit and coherent (reuse domain.IssueSummary or document a dedicated shared input type) and redundant board-only row structs are removed or justified.
- [ ] ID width behavior is unified or any intentional divergence is documented as a renderer parameter.
- [ ] Tests prove both call sites use the shared path and preserve deterministic truncation.
close_reason: Verified on current tree: board and search both call internal/ui/shared/issuerow.RenderCompact with shared tests covering both call sites

title: Acceptance review gap: shared row/list epic lacks required runtime UI verification
description:
## Description
The acceptance review task for `beads-workbench-ekp` currently requires `go test ./...`, `go build ./cmd/bwb`, and `go vet ./...`, but it does not require the repository's built-binary runtime UI verification flow for this user-visible rendering change.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Read `docs/TESTING.md` and `docs/RUNTIME_UI_VERIFICATION.md`.
2. Note that full-app verification is required for layout and search/rendering changes.
3. Read `beads-workbench-ekp.4`.
4. Observe that `.4` does not require the embedded-fixture built-binary verification workflow.

## Why
Shared row/list rendering changes are operator-visible board/search UI changes. Final acceptance should require direct runtime proof, not only unit/golden/build checks.

## Scope
- Update `beads-workbench-ekp.4` so final acceptance explicitly includes the built-binary embedded-fixture runtime verification from the repo runbook.
- Keep the acceptance review open until that verification is executed and recorded.
acceptance_criteria:
- `beads-workbench-ekp.4` instructions explicitly require the built-binary runtime UI verification workflow for this epic.
- `beads-workbench-ekp.4` acceptance criteria include successful runtime verification evidence for board/search/detail behavior relevant to the shared row/list change.
close_reason: Acceptance review task ekp.4 now explicitly requires runtime UI verification

title: Acceptance Review: Shared issue row/list rendering architecture

title: Extract a shared compact issue row renderer and adopt it in board and search
description:
## Description
Create one shared compact issue-row renderer so board and search use the same metadata hierarchy, selection styling, truncation rules, and updated architecture documentation.

## Instructions
1. Introduce a small shared UI component under `internal/ui/shared/` for rendering one compact issue row with these inputs: id, title, type, status, priority, selected state, and target width.
2. Decide whether the shared renderer accepts `domain.IssueSummary` directly or defines its own input struct. If a new struct is introduced, document why `domain.IssueSummary` is insufficient. Eliminate `board.Row` if it becomes redundant.
3. Move row-level responsibilities into that component:
   - selected-row prefix rendering
   - type/priority/status/id/title assembly
   - width-aware title truncation
   - styled vs plain width-safe rendering behavior
4. Keep token-level concerns in existing helpers where appropriate (normalization, compact token text, compact ID derivation), but avoid splitting final row assembly across board/search again.
5. Explicitly unify the existing id-width discrepancy: board currently uses `minInt(12, MaxInt(7, width/5))` while search has no upper cap. Adopt one shared formula unless a documented reason for divergence remains.
6. Update `internal/ui/board/board.go` to use the shared row component instead of assembling rows inline.
7. Update `internal/ui/search/search.go` to use the shared row component so search inherits the same compact metadata styling semantics as board.
8. Board and search must both use the shared row renderer for selection indicator, type/priority/status/ID token assembly, and width-aware truncation. Any intentional rendering difference must be documented as a renderer parameter, not a separate code path.
9. Fold in the documentation work originally scoped in `beads-workbench-ekp.1`: update `docs/CODING.md` (and `project-plan/ARCHITECTURE.md` only if truly needed) so row, optional list, panel, and detail responsibilities reflect the implemented extraction.
10. Add tests for the shared row component plus board/search integration tests that prove rendering consistency and deterministic truncation. Regenerate impacted search goldens and verify the diff because search snapshots will change when it adopts the shared renderer.
11. Resolve or supersede follow-up bug `beads-workbench-b9u` as part of this work.

## Files to Modify
- `internal/ui/board/board.go`
- `internal/ui/search/search.go`
- `internal/ui/shared/renderhelpers/helpers.go`
- a new shared component under `internal/ui/shared/` (for example `issuerow/`)
- `docs/CODING.md`
- board/search/shared renderer tests and goldens as needed
- `beads-workbench-b9u` tracking state/comments

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Board and search both use the same shared compact row renderer.
- [ ] Board and search use the shared row renderer for selection indicator, type/priority/status/ID token assembly, and width-aware truncation; any intentional difference is implemented as a documented renderer parameter.
- [ ] Deterministic truncation behavior is preserved in board and search tests.
- [ ] Shared renderer tests cover selection, width edge cases, and styled rendering.
- [ ] Documentation explicitly identifies row, optional list, panel, and detail responsibilities based on the implemented design.
- [ ] `beads-workbench-b9u` is closed or explicitly superseded by this task.
close_reason: Shared compact issuerow extracted and adopted in board/search with tests/docs; b9u resolved

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review finding: acceptance criterion is missing a specific consistency definition. What: 'Search results render the same metadata hierarchy as board' is not testable without defining what 'same' means. The actual differences I found in code: (1) board uses styled tokens (CompactIssueTypeStyled etc) while search uses plain tokens (CompactIssueType etc), (2) board uses SelectionIndicatorStyle for '›' while search uses a plain string '› ', (3) board caps idWidth at minInt(12, ...) while search has no upper cap. The criterion does not specify which of these are bugs vs intentional. Why: a tasker will have to guess whether 'same metadata hierarchy' means identical ANSI output or just identical token ordering. Suggested action: replace the criterion with: 'Board and search both use the shared row renderer for selection indicator, type/priority/status/ID token assembly, and width-aware truncation. Any intentional rendering difference (e.g. styled vs plain) is documented as a parameter of the shared renderer, not a separate code path.'

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review finding: the input type mismatch is the real extraction risk. What: board feeds board.Row (a local struct with typed Priority int), while search feeds domain.IssueSummary directly. The shared row renderer will need to accept either a common interface/struct or require callers to map into it. board.Row exists because the mode controller (mode/board/model.go) maps domain.IssueSummary → board.Row before rendering. Search skips this step and passes domain.IssueSummary straight to the renderer. Why: the task instructions say 'create a shared component under internal/ui/shared/' but do not address the input type question. If the shared renderer accepts board.Row, search needs a mapping step. If it accepts domain.IssueSummary, board.Row becomes redundant. If it introduces a new RowInput type, that is a third struct for the same data. Suggested action: add an instruction step: 'Decide whether the shared renderer accepts domain.IssueSummary directly or defines its own input struct. If a new struct, document why domain.IssueSummary is insufficient. Eliminate board.Row if it becomes redundant.'

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review finding: idWidth cap inconsistency should be called out explicitly. What: board uses minInt(12, renderhelpers.MaxInt(7, maxWidth/5)) for ID width, search uses renderhelpers.MaxInt(7, width/5) with no upper cap. This means at large widths, search gives IDs more space than board does. This looks like an accidental omission, not a design choice. Why: the shared renderer should resolve this, but the task does not call it out as a known discrepancy. Making it explicit prevents the tasker from accidentally preserving the search behavior. Suggested action: add a note under instructions that the idWidth cap logic should be unified to the board's minInt(12, ...) formula unless there is a documented reason for search to differ.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Implemented shared compact row renderer at internal/ui/shared/issuerow using domain.IssueSummary; board/search now both call it with styled=true and shared bounded ID width (min(12,max(7,width/5))). Added shared renderer tests + board/search integration coverage; updated docs/CODING.md responsibility boundaries.

title: Unify issue list rendering around shared row and list components
description:
## Description
Consolidate Beads Workbench issue-list rendering so board and search share the same compact issue-row presentation model instead of drifting through separate hand-built row formatting paths.

## Goals
- Introduce a shared compact issue-row renderer used by multiple list-style issue views.
- Decide whether a lightweight shared issue-list component should also exist above the row level.
- Keep existing detail rendering as a separate, richer component while clarifying its relationship to row/list/panel building blocks.
- Preserve deterministic truncation, selection semantics, and width-aware layout behavior across board and search.

## Decisions
- `internal/ui/details` already acts as the issue detail component and should remain separate from compact list rendering work.
- The most urgent gap is the missing shared issue-row component; issue-list abstraction should only be introduced if it reduces real duplication after row extraction.
- Generic bordered panel rendering is already provided by `internal/ui/styles.FormSection`; do not replace it unless a concrete limitation is found.
- Board and search should share one compact row contract for type/priority/status/id/title/selection rendering.

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Architecture decisions for row/list/panel/detail responsibilities are explicitly documented in code-facing project docs.
- [ ] Board and search no longer maintain separate hand-built compact issue-row rendering logic.
- [ ] Any introduced list abstraction has a clear payoff and does not add speculative complexity.
- [ ] Existing detail rendering continues to work as the dedicated issue detail component.
- [ ] Tests cover shared row/list behavior and board/search integrations.
- [ ] `go test ./...`, `go build ./cmd/bwb`, and `go vet ./...` pass.
close_reason: Verified: epic success criteria met

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Plan review: Task boundaries are correct for the scope. What: ekp.1 (docs), ekp.2 (extract row), ekp.3 (evaluate list), ekp.4 (acceptance) is a clean decomposition with the right sequencing. ekp.2 is where the actual value lives; ekp.1 and ekp.3 are support tasks. No merge or split needed. Why: the tasks map 1:1 to the decisions that need to be made and the code that needs to change.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Plan review — simplification opportunity. What: ekp.1 (documentation task) should be folded into ekp.2 as a deliverable, not a separate task. The docs produced by ekp.1 describe architectural decisions that only become real when ekp.2 lands. A standalone doc-writing task before implementation risks producing aspirational architecture that gets revised during extraction anyway. Why: separating 'write docs about what we will do' from 'do it and document it' adds a round-trip for no value. The tasker implementing ekp.2 will need to make the real boundary decisions during extraction, and those decisions should drive the docs. Suggested action: merge ekp.1 acceptance criteria into ekp.2 (add 'update docs/CODING.md with row/list/panel/detail architecture guidance' as a final step in ekp.2's instructions) and close ekp.1 as folded into ekp.2.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review finding: hidden risk — search golden files will break and that is the point, but the task should say so. What: search has 4 golden tests (search_results_preview_w120, search_empty_results_w120, search_results_narrow_w80, search_default_all_results_w120) that assert the current plain-text row format. When ekp.2 switches search to the styled shared renderer, every one of these goldens will need to be regenerated (the test harness supports BWB_UPDATE_GOLDEN=1 for this). The board also has goldens but those should only change if the row contract changes. Why: ekp.2's instructions mention 'board/search/shared renderer tests and goldens as needed' but do not call out that search golden regeneration is mandatory, not optional. A tasker might treat golden breakage as a bug rather than expected. Suggested action: add to ekp.2 instructions: 'Search golden files will break when adopting the styled shared renderer. Regenerate them with BWB_UPDATE_GOLDEN=1 and verify the diff shows the expected styling change.'

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review finding: no missing tracker updates needed right now. What: the related bug beads-workbench-b9u is properly cross-referenced from ekp.2, with ekp.2 explicitly instructed to resolve or supersede it. The epic's success criteria are testable. The quality gate commands match docs/CODING.md. No orphaned or missing issues. Why: tracker hygiene looks clean — no action needed.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Reviewer feedback applied: merged ekp.1 into ekp.2, clarified shared-row acceptance criteria and input-type/id-width decisions, and added a formal dependency from ekp.3 to ekp.2.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
Verifier final: PASS.
- Epic success criteria verified against current tree and tracker state.
- Child tasks closed; shared compact row path is real; detail remains dedicated; docs and runtime verification now pass.
Artifact: /tmp/bwb-ekp4-final-verify-TffBx5
Next: epic can close.

title: Document the UI component architecture for row, list, panel, and detail rendering
description:
## Description
Write down the intended UI component boundaries so future rendering work does not keep reinventing rows, lists, and detail surfaces independently.

## Instructions
1. Review the current rendering packages under `internal/ui/` and summarize the current responsibilities of board, search, details, shared helpers, and `styles.FormSection`.
2. Update the appropriate project-facing doc surface with a concise architecture section that defines these responsibilities:
   - shared compact issue row component
   - optional shared issue list component
   - generic panel/section container
   - dedicated issue detail component
3. Document the decision that `internal/ui/details` is the issue detail component and should stay separate from compact list rendering.
4. Document the rule that a list abstraction should only be introduced when it removes proven duplication above the row level.
5. Include a short dependency diagram or package tree snippet showing how board/search/details should compose shared UI primitives.

## Files to Modify
- `docs/CODING.md` — add or update UI component architecture guidance.
- optionally `project-plan/ARCHITECTURE.md` if a deeper architecture note is needed.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Documentation explicitly identifies row, list, panel, and detail responsibilities.
- [ ] Documentation states that `internal/ui/details` is the dedicated issue detail component.
- [ ] Documentation states that list abstraction is conditional on proven duplication.
- [ ] The guidance is specific enough that a tasker can implement row/list extraction without guessing boundaries.
close_reason: Merged into beads-workbench-ekp.2 so docs reflect implemented extraction rather than pre-implementation intent

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review finding: this task should be merged into ekp.2. What: writing architecture docs before implementing the extraction means the docs describe intent, not reality. The actual row/list boundary decisions will be made during ekp.2 implementation. Why: a standalone pre-implementation doc task adds overhead without adding clarity — the tasker doing ekp.2 will make the real tradeoffs and should document them in the same pass. Suggested action: fold ekp.1's acceptance criteria into ekp.2 as a final documentation step, then close ekp.1.

title: Extend configurable keymaps to search detail and modal flows
description:
## Description
Extend the central keymap system to the remaining interaction-heavy contexts so search, detail, and modal flows also use configurable bindings without regressing typing or navigation behavior.

## Instructions
1. Refactor `internal/mode/search/model.go` to use the central keymap for supported search actions while preserving text entry semantics in the query field.
2. Update `CapturesShellKey` so it derives from configured search bindings and still allows normal query typing for non-action keys.
3. Refactor `internal/mode/details/model.go` to use central keybindings for scroll/navigation actions.
4. Refactor `internal/ui/modal/modal.go` so modal navigation bindings come from the resolved keymap instead of package-level hardcoded defaults.
5. Update help text and any related rendering/tests so configured bindings are reflected for search, detail, and modal workflows.
6. Add tests for default behavior, configured overrides, and conflict-sensitive behavior in search/detail/modal flows.

## Files to Modify
- `internal/mode/search/model.go` and tests — search bindings and shell-capture behavior
- `internal/mode/details/model.go` and tests — detail scroll bindings
- `internal/ui/modal/modal.go` and tests — modal navigation bindings
- `internal/app/model.go` and tests — help text updates for search/detail/modal bindings
- `internal/config/*.go` — extend keymap usage/tests as needed
- `docs/CODING.md` and/or `README.md` — document search/detail/modal keybinding examples

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Search actions use configurable bindings without breaking free-form query typing.
- [ ] `CapturesShellKey` respects configured bindings and still allows text entry for non-action keys.
- [ ] Detail scroll/navigation bindings are configurable through the central keymap.
- [ ] Modal navigation bindings are configurable through the same config surface.
- [ ] Tests cover defaults, overrides, and interaction-sensitive behavior for search/detail/modal flows.
close_reason: Closed

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Implemented remaining configurable keymaps for search/detail/modal. Fixed search query typing so configured shell-pass-through keys no longer suppress normal query text entry, and updated modal bindings to honor configured space/enter aliases. Added config docs/examples for all keybinding contexts. Quality gates pass: go build ./cmd/bwb, go vet ./..., go test ./....

title: Add compact color-aware issue badges to board rows
description:
## Description
Make board issues easier to scan by adapting only the donor's compact colored metadata ideas to Beads Workbench's standalone renderer.

## Instructions
1. Review donor patterns in `/home/hans/dev/github/perles/internal/ui/shared/issuebadge/issuebadge.go` and `/home/hans/dev/github/perles/internal/ui/styles/styles.go`, but adapt only the minimal rendering ideas relevant to BWB.
2. Do **not** introduce a full donor-style `issuebadge` package, config struct, readiness glyph system, or multi-call-site abstraction. Keep the implementation small and local to BWB's existing board/renderhelpers flow.
3. Define BWB-owned issue colors in `internal/ui/styles/colors.go` for the currently used compact metadata set:
   - types: bug red, task blue, feature green, epic purple, chore gray
   - priorities: P0 red/bold, P1 orange, P2 yellow, P3/P4 gray
   - statuses: open green, in_progress blue, blocked red, closed gray
4. Update board row rendering so type, priority, and status tokens render with those colors; issue IDs stay muted; titles remain neutral and truncated.
5. Keep selected-row emphasis explicit and simple: preserve the `›` prefix and render it with a stronger highlighted style; do not add full-row background fills.
6. Preserve compact density and deterministic truncation behavior.
7. Update board renderer tests and goldens. Adjust golden normalization so ANSI color output does not make golden files unreadable.

## Files to Modify
- `internal/ui/board/board.go` — adopt colored metadata rendering.
- `internal/ui/shared/renderhelpers/helpers.go` — add compact styled metadata helpers.
- `internal/ui/styles/colors.go` — define issue type/priority/status colors and reusable styles.
- `internal/ui/board/board_test.go` — update normalization/assertions and goldens.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Board rows render distinct foreground colors for type, priority, and status tokens.
- [ ] Issue IDs render in muted foreground relative to type/priority/title.
- [ ] Selected rows use a stronger highlighted `›` prefix without full-row background fill.
- [ ] Width/truncation behavior remains deterministic in existing width-matrix tests.
- [ ] Board renderer tests/goldens pass with ANSI-safe comparison behavior.
close_reason: Implemented compact color-aware board metadata: added local type/priority/status styles, muted IDs, highlighted selected › indicator, and ANSI-safe board golden normalization with focused renderer tests.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Scope creep risk: 'introduce shared helpers or a dedicated UI helper' is vague enough to over-engineer. What: instruction #2 says to introduce a shared helper or dedicated issuebadge package. The donor's issuebadge package (perles/internal/ui/shared/issuebadge) is 140 lines with Config struct, readiness glyphs, pinned badges, and a RenderBadge + Render split. Importing this pattern wholesale would be over-engineered for BWB's current needs — BWB has exactly one call site (board.go:renderRowLine). Why: a tasker following the 'adapt donor patterns' instruction may build a full issuebadge package with Config, ShowSelection, ShowReadinessReason, etc., when BWB only needs colored type/priority/status tokens in renderRowLine. Suggested action: explicitly state that BWB does NOT need the full issuebadge package pattern. The goal is: (a) define color tokens for type/priority/status in styles/colors.go, (b) apply those colors in renderRowLine. No new package, no Config struct, no readiness glyphs. Keep the helper functions in renderhelpers/helpers.go and just make them return styled strings.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Missing specification: what colors map to what. What: the task says 'define BWB-owned issue type/priority/status colors' but does not specify what those colors are. The donor has ~30 color definitions for types, priorities, and statuses (perles styles.go:107-154). BWB currently has no issue-specific colors at all — only generic UI colors. Why: the tasker will either copy the donor's full color palette (including types BWB does not use, like Molecule, Convoy, Agent, Decision, Story, Milestone) or have to decide on a subset. Either way, they are making undocumented design decisions. Suggested action: specify the minimum color set needed: types = bug (red), task (blue), feature (green), epic (purple), chore (gray); priorities = P0 (red/bold), P1 (orange), P2 (yellow), P3+ (gray); statuses = open (green), in_progress (blue), blocked (red), closed (gray). This matches what BWB's renderhelpers already handles.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Golden file testing will be harder with ANSI color codes. What: the current board_test.go uses assertGoldenNormalized which strips trailing whitespace but does NOT strip ANSI escape codes. Adding colors to renderRowLine means golden files will contain ANSI sequences, making them hard to read and maintain. Why: the existing golden comparison approach will produce unreadable golden files when colors are added. The tasker needs guidance on whether to (a) update goldens to include ANSI codes, (b) add an ANSI-stripping normalizer, or (c) test coloring separately from layout. Suggested action: add a note to instructions that board golden tests should strip ANSI codes before comparison (update the normalizer), and add separate assertions for color presence if needed.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Task 3 has no dependency on tasks 1 or 2 — this is correct and good. The board coloring is independent of the header/footer changes. However, task 3 and task 1 both touch renderRowLine indirectly (task 1 changes footer and header but task 3 changes board rows). Since task 3 modifies board.go and task 1 modifies model.go, there should be no merge conflicts. This parallelism is well-structured.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: AC says 'selected rows remain readable' but does not define selected-row styling. What: the current selected row uses '› ' prefix (board.go:204-206) with no color treatment. The donor uses a SelectionIndicatorStyle (bold white '>') and a SelectionBackgroundColor for the entire row. The task says 'improve selected-row emphasis' but does not say how. Why: the tasker may add a background highlight, change the prefix character, make the row bold, or do nothing — all are valid interpretations. Suggested action: specify selected-row treatment explicitly: e.g., bold white '›' prefix (matching donor's SelectionIndicatorStyle), no background fill, or add a subtle background highlight. Pick one.

title: Acceptance Review: Header simplification and board issue color polish
description:
## Description
Verify that the shell header/footer and board issue rendering match the approved UX direction before the epic is closed.

## Instructions
1. Confirm the shell header is a single line with only `Board` and `Search` as top-level tabs.
2. Confirm `tab` / `shift+tab` cycle only between `Board` and `Search`, and `3` opens detail for the selected issue instead of acting like a third tab switch.
3. Confirm detail mode keeps clear selected-issue context in the header without rendering a `Detail` tab.
4. Confirm the footer owns concise mode-specific help and includes the minimum hints defined in `beads-workbench-2sh.1`.
5. Confirm board rows render distinct type/priority/status colors, muted IDs, and the stronger selected-row prefix while preserving truncation behavior at width test sizes.
6. Run the project runtime verification required for user-visible shell/layout changes using the built binary + embedded fixture workflow documented in `docs/TESTING.md` and `docs/RUNTIME_UI_VERIFICATION.md`.
7. Run `go test ./...`, `go build ./cmd/bwb`, and `go vet ./...`.

## Files to Inspect
- `internal/app/model.go`
- `internal/ui/board/board.go`
- `internal/ui/shared/renderhelpers/helpers.go`
- `internal/ui/styles/colors.go`
- `internal/app/model_test.go`
- `internal/ui/board/board_test.go`
- `docs/TESTING.md`
- `docs/RUNTIME_UI_VERIFICATION.md`

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All child implementation tasks are closed.
- [ ] Header/footer behavior matches the approved one-line header + footer-help direction.
- [ ] Detail is contextual rather than a top-level tab, including keyboard behavior.
- [ ] Type, priority, and status tokens render with distinct colors; IDs are muted; selected prefix emphasis is present.
- [ ] Board golden tests show no truncation regressions at the existing width matrix.
- [ ] Runtime verification for the built binary + embedded fixture workflow passes for the shell/header/footer/board UX changes.
- [ ] `go test ./...`, `go build ./cmd/bwb`, and `go vet ./...` pass.

## Owner
verifier
close_reason: All criteria verified: header/footer UX, detail context, board colors/truncation, runtime workflow, and Go quality gates pass

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Acceptance review task is well-structured. The dependency chain (depends on all three implementation tasks) is correct. The inspection file list covers the right files. One gap: the acceptance review should also inspect renderhelpers/helpers.go and any new files in styles/ since task 3 explicitly modifies those. Suggested action: add internal/ui/shared/renderhelpers/helpers.go to 'Files to Inspect'.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: AC 'Board issue row color hierarchy improves scanability without overflow regressions' is not testable without a baseline. What: the verifier needs to compare before/after board rendering to assess 'improvement'. How does the verifier judge that scanability improved? Why: 'improves scanability' is subjective. The verifier can check that colors are present and that truncation still works, but cannot measure improvement. Suggested action: replace with concrete criteria: (a) type, priority, and status tokens render with distinct foreground colors, (b) issue IDs render in muted foreground, (c) board golden tests pass with no truncation regressions at w80, w120, w180.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Verifier pre-implementation check: MOSTLY READY. Tasks .1 and .3 are concrete, but acceptance review is still stale after the merge. Remaining gaps tracked in beads-workbench-lxv: drop the closed .2 dependency and require runtime full-app verification alongside go test/go build/go vet. Next: refresh .4 metadata/instructions before final execution/acceptance.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Verifier PASS. Focused shell/board acceptance tests, built-binary embedded-fixture runtime review, and full go test/build/vet all passed. Artifacts: /tmp/beads-workbench-2sh.4-review/runtime_capture.log, /tmp/beads-workbench-2sh.4-review/runtime_shift_tab.log, /tmp/beads-workbench-2sh.4-review/startup.typescript. Note: beads-workbench-cja no longer reproduces; go test ./... passes.

title: Simplify shell header and move help to the footer
description:
## Description
Implement the approved shell information architecture so the app chrome becomes one line, contextual guidance moves to the footer, and shell chrome styling is handled in the same task.

## Instructions
1. Update `internal/app/model.go` so the shell header renders a single-line app bar with `Beads Workbench`, two top-level tabs (`Board`, `Search`), and right-aligned context text.
2. Remove `Detail` from top-level tab rendering and mode cycling. `tab` / `shift+tab` should cycle only between `Board` and `Search`.
3. Re-map the `3` hotkey so it opens detail for the currently selected issue (same conceptual action as `enter`/`o`) rather than behaving like a third top-level tab switch.
4. When detail mode is active, keep `Board` and `Search` as the only tabs and use the header context area to show selected issue context (`Detail: <issue-id> · <status>` when available; otherwise `Detail`).
5. Replace the existing multi-line `headerHelpLines` behavior with a single-line footer that owns concise mode-specific help:
   - Board footer includes at least `h/l`, `j/k`, `enter`, `ctrl+space`, `?`, `q`
   - Search footer includes at least `type`, `/`, `tab`, `j/k`, `enter`, `esc`, `?`, `q`
   - Detail footer includes at least `j/k`, `pgup/pgdn`, `e`, `esc`, `?`, `q`
6. `UI.ShowModeSwitcherHelp` now controls footer-help visibility instead of the removed header-help lines; preserve the config flag semantics and update tests accordingly.
7. Move the existing selection/loading summary out of the footer and into the header context area. In browse modes show board/search context first and selected/loading summary as space allows; do not reintroduce a two-line header.
8. Apply shell chrome styling in the same change: app title emphasized, active tab highlighted, inactive tabs dimmed, right-side context muted, footer help dim.
9. Preserve readable narrow-width behavior by shortening context/help text rather than reintroducing a tall header.
10. Update shell tests and impacted goldens.

## Files to Modify
- `internal/app/model.go` — update header/footer rendering, key handling, mode cycling, and responsive text.
- `internal/ui/styles/colors.go` — add or reuse shell chrome color tokens used by the header/footer.
- `internal/ui/styles/format.go` — add tiny formatting helpers if needed.
- `internal/app/model_test.go` — update header/footer expectations and hotkey behavior.
- `internal/app/testdata/model_mode_switch_detail.golden` — update for the new shell chrome.
- `internal/app/testdata/model_embedded_board_w120.golden` — update for the new shell chrome.
- `internal/app/testdata/model_roundtrip_board_back_w120.golden` — update for the new shell chrome.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Header is one line at normal widths and renders only `Board` and `Search` as top-level tabs.
- [ ] `tab` / `shift+tab` cycle only between `Board` and `Search`.
- [ ] `3` opens detail for the selected issue instead of acting like a third tab switch.
- [ ] Detail mode does not render a `Detail` tab and shows selected issue context in the header.
- [ ] Footer shows mode-specific help containing the minimum key hints listed above, and `UI.ShowModeSwitcherHelp` now controls footer visibility.
- [ ] Active tab uses the existing highlighted shell treatment; inactive tabs, right-side context, and footer hints use muted/lower-emphasis styling.
- [ ] Existing shell tests and impacted goldens reflect the new layout.
close_reason: Implemented one-line shell header with contextual detail, moved mode help to footer, remapped 3 to open detail, and updated shell styling/tests/goldens.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Task 1 still renders Detail as a tab via hotkey '3' — task does not address removing the '3' keybinding. What: the current code at model.go:362-367 handles '3' as a Detail mode switch, and model.go:783-804 has nextMode/prevMode cycling through Board→Search→Detail. The task instructions say 'Remove Detail from top-level tab rendering' but do not instruct removal or re-mapping of the '3' hotkey or tab/shift+tab cycling behavior. Why: if '3' still opens Detail, users will perceive Detail as a tab even if it is visually absent, and the tab/shift+tab cycle will still include Detail as a stop. The tasker will either leave this inconsistency or make an undocumented decision. Suggested action: add instruction to remap '3' behavior (e.g., open detail for selected issue only, same as enter) and update tab/shift+tab cycle to skip Detail and cycle Board↔Search only. Detail entry becomes enter/o only.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Header right-side context is underspecified. What: instruction #1 says 'right-aligned context text' but does not define what the context text contains — is it the selected issue ID, the dashboard name, a combined selection+loading summary, or something else? The current footer already shows 'Selected: bwf-2 (blocked)' and loading state. Why: the tasker will have to guess what goes on the right side of the header, leading to either a wrong implementation or a design discussion mid-implementation. Suggested action: specify exactly what the right-side context contains: e.g., selected issue ID + status when an issue is selected, blank otherwise. Alternatively, move this definition into task 2 (chrome styling) and note task 1 only needs a placeholder.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Acceptance criterion 'Footer shows board/search/detail-specific help hints' is not testable as written. What: it says mode-specific help but does not specify what text should appear for each mode, or minimum key hints that must be present. Why: the verifier cannot distinguish a correct implementation from a wrong one without knowing expected content. A tasker could put 'press ? for help' in every mode and technically pass. Suggested action: specify at minimum the key hints that must be present per mode, e.g., Board footer must include h/l, j/k, enter, ?, q; Search footer must include /, esc; Detail footer must include j/k, e, esc.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: The footer currently shows selection + loading state (model.go:647-669). Task instructions do not address what happens to this existing footer content. What: the current renderFooter() renders 'Selected: ID (status) · loading summary'. The task says to put mode-specific help in the footer but does not say whether the selection/loading info stays, moves, or is removed. Why: the tasker will either clobber the existing selection/loading footer info or keep two footer lines — both are wrong without explicit direction. Suggested action: specify that the footer has two sections: left = mode-specific help keys (new), right = selected issue + loading summary (existing), on a single line. Or explicitly say the selection info moves to the header context area and footer is help-only.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Golden file impact is larger than 'Files to Modify' suggests. What: the task says to modify model.go and model_test.go, but the header/footer changes will break 3 existing golden files (model_mode_switch_detail.golden, model_embedded_board_w120.golden, model_roundtrip_board_back_w120.golden) plus the headerHelpLines test (TestModelBoardHeaderUsesCompactHelpAt120Cols). Why: if the golden update scope is not mentioned, tasker may be surprised by unexpected test failures and may not regenerate all goldens correctly. Suggested action: add the 3 golden files to 'Files to Modify' and note that TestModelBoardHeaderUsesCompactHelpAt120Cols will need to change its assertions.

title: Streamline shell header and improve board issue color hierarchy
description:
## Description
Refresh the app shell chrome so the board becomes the focal point instead of a stack of persistent help lines.

## Goals
- Replace the tall multi-line header with a single-line app bar.
- Treat Board and Search as the only top-level tabs; Detail is a contextual view, not a tab.
- Move mode-specific help into the footer.
- Improve board issue scanability with compact color cues for type, priority, and status inspired by Perles.

## Decisions
- Approved header direction: app name + tabs + right-side context/status on a single line.
- Header tabs are only `Board` and `Search`.
- When detail is open, keep the browse tabs and use the right-side context area to show selected-issue/detail state instead of a Detail tab.
- Footer owns concise, mode-specific key hints.
- Board issue coloring should adapt small donor-style badge ideas from `/home/hans/dev/github/perles` without importing donor-only runtime/orchestration code.

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Shell header renders as a single line with only Board and Search tabs.
- [ ] Footer renders concise mode-specific help instead of persistent multi-line header help.
- [ ] Detail mode no longer appears as a top-level tab and still preserves clear selected-issue context.
- [ ] Board issue rows gain readable color hierarchy for type/priority/status metadata while preserving compact truncation behavior.
- [ ] Updated UI tests/goldens cover header/footer and board row rendering.
- [ ] `go test ./...`, `go build ./cmd/bwb`, and `go vet ./...` pass.
close_reason: all steps complete

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Plan simplification recommendation: merge task 2 into task 1. Task 2 (chrome styling) cannot meaningfully exist as a separate task from task 1 (header restructuring). The current code already applies tab styling — task 1 must make styling decisions to render a working one-line header. Task 2 as a P2 polish pass after task 1 will either find the work already done or create churn re-styling what task 1 just built. Merging them keeps the epic at 3 beads (structural header+footer+styling, board coloring, acceptance) instead of 4. The implementation is small enough to be one task.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Hidden risk: the UI.ShowModeSwitcherHelp config flag (model.go:600-601) gates the current help lines. Task 1 moves help to the footer, but this config flag check still applies. If this flag is false, the user gets no help at all — neither header nor footer. The plan does not address this. Suggested action: task 1 instructions should state that the footer help is always shown (remove the config gate) or that ShowModeSwitcherHelp now controls footer visibility instead. The config field name may need updating too.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Donor migration rules compliance: the plan correctly scopes donor reuse to UI primitives only. The referenced donor paths (issuebadge, styles) are within the allowed donor paths listed in CODING.md. The column.go reference is informational context only and imports bql which is on the forbidden list — as long as only the rendering pattern is adapted and no code is copied from column.go's BQL/orchestration paths, this is fine. No compliance issue found.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Reviewer feedback applied: merged 2sh.2 into 2sh.1, clarified header/footer keyboard behavior, specified header context/footer hints, constrained board-coloring scope, and made acceptance checks concrete.

title: Acceptance Review: Runtime config and configurable keybindings
description:
## Description
Verify the new runtime configuration system works end to end for config loading, launcher overrides, and fully configurable keybindings before the epic is closed.

## Verification Steps
1. Run BWB with no config file and confirm startup still uses defaults.
2. Run BWB with a representative config that overrides `editor.command`, one built-in launcher action, shell/board keys, and search/detail/modal keys.
3. Confirm unknown YAML keys generate warnings but do not prevent startup.
4. Confirm invalid YAML, invalid key names, and conflicting keybindings fail startup with clear errors.
5. Run the required quality gates.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All implementation tasks in this epic are closed.
- [ ] A representative config file successfully overrides editor, launcher, and keybinding settings.
- [ ] Unknown YAML keys generate startup warnings without aborting launch.
- [ ] Invalid config and conflicting keybindings fail startup with clear errors.
- [ ] Quality gates pass: `go build ./cmd/bwb`, `go vet ./...`, and `go test ./...`.

## Owner
verifier
close_reason: All criteria verified

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Acceptance review lacks specific verification steps — currently not executable by a verifier

**What**: ni1.4 acceptance criteria say "a representative config file successfully overrides editor, launcher, and keybinding settings" — but there's no definition of what "representative" means, no specific config file content to test, and no expected observable behavior to verify.

**Why**: A verifier needs concrete scenarios. "It works with a config file" is not acceptance testing — it's vibes-based approval. The whole point of an acceptance review task is to be specific enough that a different person can verify without guessing.

**Suggested action**: Replace with concrete verification steps:
1. Create a test config that overrides `editor.command` to `nano`, adds a custom launcher action, remaps `q` to `ctrl+q` for quit, and remaps board navigation from `h/l` to `left/right` only
2. Start BWB with this config and verify: editor opens nano, custom launcher appears, original `q` no longer quits but `ctrl+q` does, `h/l` no longer navigate board columns but arrow keys still do
3. Create a config with duplicate bindings (`j` mapped to two actions in board context) and verify startup fails with an error message naming the conflict
4. Delete the config file and verify BWB starts with all defaults working normally
5. Quality gates: `go build ./cmd/bwb`, `go vet ./...`, `go test ./...` all pass

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Verifier PASS. Checked epic tasks closed (ni1.1, ni1.2 superseded/closed, ni1.3, ni1.5), re-ran focused config/keybinding verification plus quality gates, and validated startup warning/error paths with built binary. Artifact: /tmp/ni14-verify-GtinQu

title: Move editor and launcher settings to file-backed config
description:
## Description
Move editor and launcher runtime settings onto the new file-backed config surface so users can choose which tools BWB launches without changing code.

## Instructions
1. Extend the config model so `editor.command` and launcher definitions can be read from YAML and merged with defaults.
2. Preserve the existing built-in launcher actions (`editor`, `nvim`, `opencode`, `shell-command`) as defaults while allowing config to override their command, args, env, and workdir fields.
3. Ensure the app services layer continues to construct launcher and editor services from the resolved merged config.
4. Add tests that prove launcher and editor settings can be overridden from config while unspecified built-ins remain available.
5. Update user-facing docs with example YAML for editor and launcher customization.

## Files to Modify
- `internal/config/model.go` — runtime config structures and defaults
- `internal/config/*.go` — YAML decoding and launcher merge behavior
- `internal/app/services.go` — consume resolved editor and launcher config
- `internal/app/services_test.go` — verify merged launcher/editor config usage
- `internal/config/model_test.go` and related tests — validate defaults and overrides
- `docs/CODING.md` and/or `README.md` — document editor and launcher config examples

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `editor.command` can be overridden from YAML.
- [ ] Built-in launcher actions remain available by default.
- [ ] A config file can override built-in launcher action definitions without redefining every action.
- [ ] Services use the merged launcher/editor config when constructing runtime dependencies.
- [ ] Tests cover launcher and editor override behavior.
close_reason: Superseded by merged scope in beads-workbench-ni1.1 after planning review

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
