comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Resolved by beads-workbench-7qz: startup focus is now deterministic and stable in dashboard order during async loads.

title: Synchronize startup selection context with visible board content
description:
## Description
Runtime verification found that during startup the board can render visible issue rows while the shell still shows `Selected: none` and without a meaningful selected-issue detail/context preview. This creates a confusing browsing state where content appears selectable before the shell's shared selection context is visibly established.

## Evidence
- Real PTY/runtime verification on startup
- Verifier observed visible issue rows while footer selection context still lagged behind content
- This weakens focus/selection clarity in the main browsing screen

## Instructions
1. Ensure startup board loading establishes shell selection context as soon as visible content becomes selectable.
2. Ensure selected-issue footer/detail-preview state stays synchronized with the first visible/active board selection during startup.
3. Add or update app-level tests that cover startup selection synchronization.
4. Re-check the startup runtime in a PTY after the fix.

## Files to Modify
- `internal/app/model.go`
- `internal/app/model_test.go`
- related board/app integration tests if needed

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Once visible board content is rendered on startup, shell selected-issue context is synchronized with the active board selection
- [ ] The startup browsing screen does not show visible issues while still reporting `Selected: none`
- [ ] Automated tests cover startup selection synchronization
- [ ] Real runtime/PTTY re-check confirms startup selection clarity
close_reason: Verified startup selection synchronization fix

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Verifier PASS: once visible board content renders on startup, shell selected-issue context now stays synchronized and the runtime no longer shows visible issues while the footer still says Selected: none. Remaining async startup focus wobble has been split into follow-up bug beads-workbench-3tm.

title: Fix runtime search initialization and key handling
description:
## Description
Verifier found that the new search mode implementation is not yet correct in the active runtime path. Search controller wiring exists and tests pass, but the real app has two runtime failures: (1) search init invokes empty `bd search --json` and surfaces `search query is required`, and (2) shell-level hotkeys intercept search text entry so characters like `b` switch modes instead of editing the search/filter input.

## Instructions
1. Prevent empty-query search init from calling unsupported `bd search` behavior. Use a supported empty-state or gateway-backed fallback that preserves the standalone architecture.
2. Ensure active search-mode key handling gets first chance to consume text-entry keys before shell-level mode-switch hotkeys fire.
3. Preserve gateway-only data access; do not shell out from the UI layer.
4. Add or update tests that cover the runtime regressions:
   - search startup does not error on empty query
   - typing search text does not trigger shell mode-switch hotkeys
5. Re-run `go build ./cmd/bwb`, `go vet ./...`, and `go test ./...`.

## Files to Modify
- `internal/app/model.go`
- `internal/mode/search/model.go`
- `internal/mode/search/model_test.go`
- `internal/app/model_test.go`
- related search/ui files if needed

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Search mode no longer errors on empty-query initialization in the active app runtime
- [ ] Search text entry is not hijacked by shell mode-switch hotkeys
- [ ] Search still routes through gateway-only methods
- [ ] Automated tests cover both runtime regressions
- [ ] `go build ./cmd/bwb`, `go vet ./...`, and `go test ./...` pass
close_reason: Verified search runtime regressions fixed

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Verifier PASS: search init no longer errors on empty query in the active runtime path; search text entry is no longer hijacked by shell hotkeys; search remains gateway-only; regression tests cover both failures; go build ./cmd/bwb, go vet ./..., and go test ./... all pass.

title: Wire real board mode into app shell runtime
description:
## Description
Verifier found that the standalone board controller and renderer exist, but the actual app runtime still uses the older list-only board path inside `internal/app/model.go`. As a result, the closed board task is not truly integrated into the shipped app path.

## Instructions
1. Replace the root app's list-only board rendering/loading path with the real `internal/mode/board` controller.
2. Keep the app shell as the owner of shared selected-issue/detail state, but forward board controller messages so real board navigation updates shell selection and detail loading.
3. Ensure the app's board surface renders dashboard columns/sections from the built-in dashboard provider and supported gateway query types.
4. Remove or refactor duplicate list-only board code from `internal/app/model.go` as needed.
5. Add or update app-level integration tests proving the real board mode is active in the runtime path and board navigation updates shell detail state.
6. Run `go build ./cmd/bwb`, `go vet ./...`, and `go test ./...`.

## Files to Modify
- `internal/app/model.go`
- `internal/app/model_test.go`
- `internal/mode/board/` (update if needed for integration)
- `internal/dashboard/` (integration updates only if needed)
- related testdata/golden files if needed

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The actual `cmd/bwb` runtime uses the standalone board mode controller
- [ ] Board columns/sections in the app shell come from the dashboard provider and supported gateway query types
- [ ] Real board navigation updates the shell's shared selected-issue/detail state
- [ ] Duplicate list-only board runtime logic is removed or no longer drives the active app board path
- [ ] App-level automated tests cover real board-to-shell integration
- [ ] `go build ./cmd/bwb`, `go vet ./...`, and `go test ./...` pass
close_reason: Verified board controller now drives active app runtime

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Verifier PASS: cmd/bwb now uses the standalone board controller in the active runtime path; board columns/sections come from the dashboard provider and supported query types; real board navigation updates shell selection/detail state; duplicate list-only board runtime logic no longer drives the app path; go build ./cmd/bwb, go vet ./..., and go test ./... all pass.

title: Surface app-shell loading feedback through shared UI primitive
description:
## Description
Verifier rejected the app-shell shell-layout work because loading feedback is still rendered as inline hard-coded strings in `internal/app/model.go` instead of through a shared UI primitive. Errors already use the shared toaster, but loading state does not.

## Instructions
1. Add a reusable shared UI primitive for loading/status feedback under `internal/ui/`.
2. Update `internal/app/model.go` to render board/search/detail loading states through that shared primitive instead of inline strings.
3. Add or update tests to verify the shell renders loading feedback via the shared primitive.
4. Update `docs/CODING.md` if the shared loading primitive becomes part of the shell conventions.

## Files to Modify
- `internal/ui/` (create/update shared loading primitive)
- `internal/app/model.go` (replace inline loading strings)
- `internal/app/model_test.go` (cover loading rendering)
- `docs/CODING.md` (update if needed)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] App-shell loading feedback is rendered through a shared UI primitive
- [ ] `internal/app/model.go` no longer hard-codes user-facing loading strings for board/search/detail states
- [ ] Tests cover loading feedback rendering in the shell
- [ ] `go test ./...` passes
close_reason: Fixed with shared loading primitive and tests

title: Implement configuration model for editor launcher and UI preferences
description:
## Description
Implement the minimal v1 configuration model needed by the standalone app so editor commands, launcher definitions, and basic UI preferences do not emerge as ad-hoc constants scattered across features.

## Instructions
1. Define the configuration model for:
   - editor command / `$EDITOR` fallback
   - launcher definitions or launcher defaults
   - basic UI preferences needed by the app shell
2. Keep the configuration surface intentionally small and aligned with the v1 architecture.
3. Create a clear package boundary for config loading and access.
4. Document the configuration model and defaults in `docs/CODING.md` or `docs/TESTING.md` where appropriate.
5. Do not introduce a large legacy-style config system.

## Files to Modify
- configuration package under `internal/` (create)
- `internal/app/` and/or `internal/launcher/` (integrate config access as needed)
- `docs/CODING.md` (update)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A minimal v1 configuration model exists
- [ ] Editor command and `$EDITOR` fallback behavior are defined
- [ ] Launcher defaults are defined without a large custom configuration surface
- [ ] Basic UI preferences have a clear configuration home
- [ ] Documentation reflects the configuration model and defaults
close_reason: Configuration model implemented and verified

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## yze.2.6 has no upstream dependency — can be claimed immediately, but requires gateway and app context to design against

**What**: `beads-workbench-yze.2.6` (configuration model) is listed as ready with zero dependencies. It needs to define editor command config, launcher defaults, and UI preferences. However, the configuration model should be informed by the gateway interface shape (omd.2), the app shell structure (yze.2.1), and ideally the editor/launcher task specs. Designing config in a vacuum risks a model that doesn't match the consuming code.

**Why**: An agent claiming this task right now would design a configuration model before the gateway interface, app container, or launcher framework exist. The config model could define fields that don't map to actual runtime needs, or miss fields that later tasks require. This creates rework when the editor (yze.4.1) or launcher (yze.4.3) tasks discover the config model doesn't fit.

**Suggested action**: Add dependency: `yze.2.6 depends-on yze.2.1` (root app model). This ensures the config model is designed after the services container exists and can be integrated immediately. It does not need to wait for the full editor/launcher epic — but it should at least wait for the app shell to know what config consumers look like.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [PASS] Configuration model task closes the ad-hoc config sprawl gap

**What**: beads-workbench-yze.2.6 addresses the first-pass finding about missing configuration coverage. It defines the config model for editor command/$EDITOR fallback, launcher defaults, and basic UI preferences. It blocks the app shell acceptance review, ensuring config exists before features consume it.

**Why**: This prevents the editor task from reading $EDITOR directly and the launcher task from hardcoding definitions independently. A unified config model means all features pull from one place.

**Suggested action**: No structural change needed. One concern about dependency ordering is noted separately.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter

## PASS: Configuration model is now explicitly planned

**What**: Task beads-workbench-yze.2.6 ("Implement configuration model for editor launcher and UI preferences") now exists in the app shell epic. It covers editor command/$EDITOR fallback, launcher defaults, basic UI preferences, and documentation. It blocks the app shell acceptance review (yze.2.5) and the v1 acceptance review (yze.1).

**Why**: This was a gap in the original plan — the architecture doc specified v1 config areas but no task delivered them. Without it, editor and launcher tasks would each invent ad-hoc config approaches. The new task prevents config sprawl and establishes a unified configuration home.

**Suggested action**: None. This adequately addresses the configuration gap.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter

## [PASS] Configuration model task requires documentation

**What**: Task yze.2.6 ("Implement configuration model") includes the instruction "Document the configuration model and defaults in docs/CODING.md or docs/TESTING.md where appropriate" and the acceptance criterion "Documentation reflects the configuration model and defaults." The app shell acceptance review (yze.2.5) then verifies "docs/CODING.md reflects the implemented shell architecture and config model."

**Why**: Configuration is one of the areas most likely to cause documentation drift — users need to know what can be configured, what the defaults are, and where the config file lives. Having documentation as an explicit deliverable of the config task, verified by the acceptance review, ensures this doesn't fall through the cracks.

**Suggested action**: None. This is adequate.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## MINOR: yze.2.6 (config model) has no dependency — could be picked up before the app shell or gateway exists

**What**: Task yze.2.6 (implement configuration model) has no DEPENDS ON entries. The config model needs to integrate with the app services container (yze.2.1) and should reference the gateway's capabilities to know what editor/launcher config fields are needed. But it's currently a free-floating task that could be picked up in any order.

**Why**: Without a dependency on at least the app model (yze.2.1), the config task could be built without knowing what the services container looks like, leading to misaligned integration. Config is a supporting concern that should follow the core app structure, not precede it.

**Suggested action**: Add dependency: yze.2.6 depends on yze.2.1 (root app model). This ensures the config model is shaped to fit the actual services container rather than being designed in isolation.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [MINOR] Config task has no dependencies — it can start before the gateway or app shell exist

**What**: beads-workbench-yze.2.6 has no DEPENDS ON entries. It blocks yze.1 (v1 acceptance) and yze.2.5 (app shell acceptance), but it doesn't depend on any prior task. An agent could start implementing the config model before omd.7 (gateway foundation) or yze.2.1 (root app model) completes.

**Why**: The config model needs to integrate with the services container (yze.2.1) and know the gateway interface shape (omd.2) to define launcher and editor config that aligns with the actual implementation. Starting config before the app model exists risks creating a config structure that doesn't fit the services container. This is a minor ordering concern — the implementer would likely wait anyway — but explicit dependencies prevent execution mistakes.

**Suggested action**: Add dependency: yze.2.6 depends on yze.2.1 (root app model). The config model needs to know the services container shape to integrate properly.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [MINOR] Config task has no dependency — could be started before anything exists

**What**: yze.2.6 "Implement configuration model" has no DEPENDS ON relationship. It blocks yze.2.5 (app shell acceptance) and yze.1 (product acceptance). But it could be picked up before the repository is bootstrapped (omd.1) or before the app shell exists (yze.2.1).

**Why**: Config loading needs the package layout to exist (omd.1) and should align with the services container (yze.2.1). Building config in a vacuum means the implementer won't know what the services container expects. This is minor because the blocking direction is correct (it blocks acceptance), but the "can start anytime" scheduling is misleading.

**Suggested action**: Add dependency: yze.2.6 DEPENDS ON yze.2.1 (root app model). The config model should be built after the services container exists so they can be integrated coherently.

title: Establish automated TUI and end-to-end test harness
description:
## Description
Create the shared automated testing harness that lets agents verify the Bubble Tea UI and full product workflows without relying on manual terminal testing. This task defines the TUI testing strategy, shared test doubles, embedded-mode fixture repos, and testing documentation used by all later UI and product verification tasks.

## Instructions
1. Establish `teatest` plus golden-output verification as the default Bubble Tea UI testing approach for this repo, combined with model/message-driven state-machine tests where behavior needs direct verification. If a surface needs a different technique, document the exception.
2. Create a shared `FakeBeadsGateway` or equivalent deterministic gateway test double for UI-level tests, including configurable per-method error injection for error-path testing.
3. Create deterministic fake seams for external editor launching and launcher process execution so tests never need to spawn real interactive tools.
4. Create a reproducible embedded-mode beads fixture repository with known issues, comments, labels, and dependencies for integration/e2e testing.
5. Define the test vocabulary for this repo: unit, integration, and end-to-end/smoke tests.
6. Create `docs/TESTING.md` and document:
   - test commands
   - test categories
   - Bubble Tea UI testing strategy
   - fake gateway/editor/launcher seams
   - embedded-mode fixture usage
7. Make it explicit that product verification must not require manual human UI inspection.

## Files to Modify
- shared UI test harness packages under `internal/` (create)
- fake gateway/editor/launcher test helpers (create)
- test fixture repo/data for embedded-mode beads testing (create)
- `docs/TESTING.md` (create)
- `docs/CODING.md` (update test vocabulary references if needed)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `teatest` plus golden-output verification is established as the default automated Bubble Tea UI testing approach
- [ ] A shared fake gateway exists for deterministic UI tests
- [ ] The fake gateway supports configurable error injection for downstream error-path tests
- [ ] Fake editor and launcher seams exist for non-interactive testing
- [ ] A reproducible embedded-mode beads fixture exists for integration/e2e tests
- [ ] `docs/TESTING.md` exists and documents the testing strategy and commands
- [ ] The plan no longer relies on manual UI testing to prove product behavior
close_reason: Established shared TUI and end-to-end test harness with fake seams and embedded fixture support

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## yze.5 (test harness) has no upstream dependency — but it needs the repo and gateway interface to exist

**What**: `beads-workbench-yze.5` (TUI and e2e test harness) is listed as ready with zero dependencies. It must create a `FakeBeadsGateway` implementing the `BeadsGateway` interface, create an embedded-mode beads fixture repo, and establish the teatest/golden file harness. None of this is possible until the gateway interface (omd.2) exists and the repo is bootstrapped (omd.1).

**Why**: An agent starting yze.5 right now has no `BeadsGateway` interface to implement a fake for, no `internal/` tree for the test harness packages, and no `go.mod` to add test dependencies to. The task will fail immediately. This is the most dangerous false-ready issue because yze.5 blocks all three downstream test tasks (yze.2.4, yze.3.5, yze.4.5).

**Suggested action**: Add dependency: `yze.5 depends-on omd.7` (Phase 1 acceptance). The test harness needs the full gateway interface (finalized in Phase 1) to create the fake gateway. This also ensures the gateway test strategy from omd.6 is established before the TUI harness layers on top of it. Alternative: `yze.5 depends-on omd.2` if you want it to start earlier, but the fake gateway won't be stable until the interface is accepted.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [PASS] TUI test harness task addresses the primary first-pass gap

**What**: beads-workbench-yze.5 directly addresses the biggest testability gap from the first review: the missing automated TUI testing strategy. It requires a standard Bubble Tea test harness, shared FakeBeadsGateway, fake editor/launcher seams, embedded-mode beads fixture, docs/TESTING.md, and explicit vocabulary for test categories. The task blocks all three downstream test tasks (yze.2.4, yze.3.5, yze.4.5).

**Why**: This is now the keystone of agent-verifiable execution. The dependency wiring is correct — no test task can proceed without the harness existing. The scope covers the five infrastructure items the first review identified as missing (teatest approach, fake gateway, fake editor/launcher seams, embedded fixture, test vocabulary).

**Suggested action**: No structural change needed. This task is well-scoped and correctly positioned. Specific content gaps in this task are addressed in separate findings.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [PASS] docs/TESTING.md is now properly planned with comprehensive scope

**What**: Task yze.5 ("Establish automated TUI and end-to-end test harness") explicitly creates docs/TESTING.md and specifies its content: test commands, test categories, Bubble Tea UI testing strategy, fake gateway/editor/launcher seams, and embedded-mode fixture usage. Additionally, omd.6 says "Create or update docs/TESTING.md if gateway-specific harness/fixture usage needs to be documented there." The acceptance review omd.7 verifies TESTING.md exists and documents the gateway test strategy.

**Why**: The original plan had zero mention of TESTING.md anywhere. The updated plan has it as a deliverable in yze.5, a conditional update in omd.6, and a verification criterion in every acceptance review. The docs/TESTING.md lifecycle is now: created in yze.5 (or omd.6 for gateway-specific content), verified at each acceptance gate, confirmed accurate at v1 acceptance. This is a complete documentation lifecycle.

**Suggested action**: None. This is well-planned.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## PASS: TUI test harness is now a cross-cutting task with correct dependencies

**What**: Task beads-workbench-yze.5 ("Establish automated TUI and end-to-end test harness") now exists as a dedicated cross-cutting task. It requires: a standard Bubble Tea test approach, a shared FakeBeadsGateway, fake editor/launcher seams, a reproducible embedded-mode fixture repo, test vocabulary definition, and docs/TESTING.md creation. It correctly blocks all three UI test tasks (yze.2.4, yze.3.5, yze.4.5) and the v1 acceptance review.

**Why**: This was the single biggest testability gap in the original plan — no shared test infrastructure existed, and every test task would have needed to build its own. The new task centralizes test infrastructure decisions before any UI test task runs, ensuring consistency.

**Suggested action**: None. This is a strong structural improvement.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [MAJOR] yze.5 has no dependency on the gateway foundation — it cannot build the embedded-mode fixture

**What**: beads-workbench-yze.5 creates the shared test harness including "A reproducible embedded-mode beads fixture repository with known issues, comments, labels, and dependencies for integration/e2e testing." To create this fixture, yze.5 needs the gateway interfaces (from omd.2) and possibly the gateway implementation (from omd.4/omd.5) to generate fixture data. But yze.5 has ZERO dependencies — it depends on nothing and blocks yze.1, yze.2.4, yze.3.5, and yze.4.5.

**Why**: Without a dependency on at least omd.7 (Phase 1 acceptance), an agent could start yze.5 before the gateway even exists. The FakeBeadsGateway it creates must implement the BeadsGateway interface — which doesn't exist yet if omd.2 hasn't run. The embedded-mode fixture needs `bd` commands that the gateway implementation validates — those commands aren't proven to work until omd.4/omd.5/omd.6 complete. An agent working on yze.5 in parallel with Phase 1 will be building fakes against an interface that hasn't been defined yet.

**Suggested action**: Add a dependency: yze.5 depends on omd.7 (Phase 1 gateway acceptance). This ensures the gateway interface, implementation, and command runner are all proven before the test harness builds fakes against them. This does not change the critical path — yze.2.4 already depends on yze.2.3 which depends on yze.2.1 which depends on omd.7, so yze.5 is already effectively serialized after Phase 1 through its downstream consumers.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [MAJOR] yze.5 does not specify what the TUI test harness technique actually IS

**What**: The task says "Establish the standard Bubble Tea test approach for this repo (for example: teatest, golden output tests, model-level message driving, or an equivalent fully automated harness)." This is a menu of options, not a decision. The first-pass review specifically called out the missing teatest/golden-file convention as the #1 testability gap. The updated task acknowledges it needs to be decided but leaves it to the implementer.

**Why**: The whole point of this task is to establish a convention BEFORE the test tasks run. If the implementer picks "model-level message driving" without golden files, then yze.3.2's "Automated board tests pass without manual UI inspection" becomes a state assertion that proves the model is correct but not that View() renders correctly. If they pick golden files without model assertions, state machine bugs pass through. The donor repo uses teatest with golden files extensively — this proven pattern should be the default, not one option among several.

**Suggested action**: Replace the parenthetical menu with a decision: "Establish `teatest` golden file testing as the standard View() output verification technique, combined with model-level Init/Update/View state machine tests for behavior verification. Create a `testdata/` golden file convention consistent with standard Go test patterns. If teatest is unsuitable for a specific case, document the exception and alternative."

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## MINOR: yze.5 (test harness) has no explicit dependency — could be picked up before any implementation exists

**What**: Task yze.5 (establish TUI and e2e test harness) has no DEPENDS ON entries. It blocks yze.2.4, yze.3.5, yze.4.5, and yze.1. But it should depend on at least the gateway foundation being accepted (omd.7), because the FakeBeadsGateway it creates needs the real BeadsGateway interface to implement, and the embedded-mode fixture repo needs the gateway to test against.

**Why**: Without a dependency, yze.5 shows as ready work immediately, before the BeadsGateway interface even exists. An agent picking it up would have nothing to implement the fake against. The test harness inherently depends on the interfaces it doubles.

**Suggested action**: Add dependency: yze.5 depends on omd.7 (Phase 1 gateway foundation acceptance). This ensures the gateway interface exists before the fake gateway is built.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [MINOR] Test harness task (yze.5) has no explicit dependency — execution timing is ambiguous

**What**: yze.5 "Establish automated TUI and end-to-end test harness" blocks yze.2.4, yze.3.5, yze.4.5, and yze.1. But it has no DEPENDS ON relationship itself. This means it could theoretically be started before the foundation epic (omd) is complete — before the gateway interface, domain models, or command runner exist. The fake gateway, fake editor seam, and fixture repo all depend on the real interfaces existing first.

**Why**: An agent picking up yze.5 before omd.2 (gateway interface) is closed would be designing test doubles against a non-existent interface. The FakeBeadsGateway can't be built before BeadsGateway is defined. The embedded-mode fixture can't be validated before the gateway read operations exist to test against it. This isn't a blocker because a reasonable agent would wait, but the explicit dependency is missing.

**Suggested action**: Add a dependency: yze.5 DEPENDS ON omd.7 (Phase 1 gateway acceptance). This ensures the gateway interface, domain models, and command runner all exist before test infrastructure is built against them. It still allows yze.5 to run in parallel with yze.2.1-yze.2.3 (app shell implementation), which is the right timing.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [MAJOR] No task requires documenting the test vocabulary before tests are written

**What**: The prior review (comment on yze product epic) flagged that "integration test" is used inconsistently across the plan. Task yze.5 now addresses this: instruction 5 says "Define the test vocabulary for this repo: unit, integration, and end-to-end/smoke tests." However, yze.5 does NOT block omd.6 (gateway tests). omd.6 will be executed during Phase 1, before yze.5 runs. This means the first batch of tests will be written without a defined test vocabulary, and the omd.7 acceptance review will verify tests using undefined terms.

**Why**: The gateway tests in omd.6 are the foundation that all later test tasks build on. If the gateway test task writes tests without the vocabulary definition (because yze.5 hasn't run yet), the patterns established in Phase 1 may conflict with the vocabulary defined later in yze.5. Then either yze.5 has to retroactively align with whatever omd.6 did, or the gateway tests become inconsistent with the documented vocabulary. The test vocabulary definition needs to exist before the first real tests are written.

**Suggested action**: Either: (a) move the test vocabulary definition from yze.5 into omd.6 or omd.8 (so it's defined during Phase 1), or (b) add a dependency: omd.6 depends on yze.5 (but this is structurally awkward since yze.5 is in the product-level epic). Option (a) is cleaner — add to omd.6 or omd.8: "Define and document the test vocabulary (unit/integration/e2e) in docs/TESTING.md." Then yze.5 expands the vocabulary with TUI-specific testing strategy rather than defining it from scratch.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## PASS: yze.5 (test harness) correctly blocks all downstream test tasks

**What**: `beads-workbench-yze.5` (TUI and e2e test harness) blocks yze.2.4 (app shell tests), yze.3.5 (browsing tests), and yze.4.5 (editor/launcher tests). This means the shared test infrastructure — fake gateway, teatest harness, embedded-mode fixture, editor/launcher seams — must be established before any UI test task can proceed.

**Why**: This was the most critical infrastructure gap identified in the prior review: test tasks had no shared infrastructure to build on. By creating yze.5 as a cross-cutting dependency, the plan ensures consistent test patterns across all epics. The fake gateway, golden file conventions, and fixture data will be defined once and used everywhere.

**Suggested action**: No change needed to the blocking relationships. The only issue is yze.5's own missing upstream dependency (noted separately) — it needs omd.7 or at minimum omd.2 before it can create the fake gateway implementation.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [MAJOR] yze.5 FakeBeadsGateway scope doesn't mention error injection — only "deterministic" returns

**What**: yze.5 requires "A shared FakeBeadsGateway or equivalent deterministic gateway test double for UI-level tests." The word "deterministic" implies fixed return values, but doesn't explicitly require error injection capability. Every test task downstream needs to test error paths (gateway unavailable, command failed, parse failure) — but if the fake only supports happy-path returns, error testing requires each test task to build its own error-injecting wrapper.

**Why**: Error injection is the primary reason a fake gateway exists for UI tests. Happy-path testing can be done with any static data. The value of a SHARED fake is that it provides consistent error injection across all UI test tasks. Without explicit error injection support, the fake is underspecified.

**Suggested action**: Add to yze.5 acceptance criteria: "The fake gateway supports configurable error injection (per-method error returns) so downstream test tasks can exercise error-path UI rendering without building custom test doubles."

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [PASS] docs/TESTING.md is now planned at the right moment and verified at every gate

**What**: yze.5 creates docs/TESTING.md with test commands, categories, TUI strategy, seams, and fixture usage. omd.6 also references docs/TESTING.md creation for gateway-specific guidance. Every acceptance review from omd.7 onward verifies docs/TESTING.md reflects the current state. The v1 acceptance (yze.1) checks "docs/CODING.md, docs/TESTING.md, and AGENTS.md match the implemented product."

**Why**: This addresses the first-pass documentation gap. Testing documentation is created when the harness is built and verified at every subsequent gate.

**Suggested action**: No change needed.

title: Add architecture guardrails and quality gates
description:
## Description
Add automated guardrails that enforce the standalone architecture and quality gates during implementation. The current plan repeatedly says "no SQL, no BQL, no orchestration," but those constraints need build-time enforcement so donor transplants cannot silently reintroduce forbidden dependencies.

## Instructions
1. Add automated checks that fail the build or test run when code imports forbidden legacy paths or forbidden dependency classes.
2. Enforce these architectural boundaries at minimum:
   - no direct SQL in the active product path
   - no `internal/bql` dependency in the standalone app
   - no orchestration/control-plane subsystem in the active product path
3. Add standard quality gates for `go build ./cmd/bwb` and `go vet ./...` so acceptance reviews do not rely on implied checks.
4. Document the enforced architectural boundaries and quality gates in `docs/CODING.md`.
5. Keep the enforcement lightweight and automated so agents can run it in CI-like local verification.

## Files to Modify
- project-local build/test verification scripts or Go-based checks (create)
- `docs/CODING.md` (update)
- `*_test.go` or verification helpers as needed

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Automated checks fail if forbidden SQL/BQL/orchestration dependencies are introduced
- [ ] `go build ./cmd/bwb` is part of the standard quality gate
- [ ] `go vet ./...` is part of the standard quality gate
- [ ] `docs/CODING.md` documents the enforced architecture guardrails and quality gates
close_reason: Added architecture guardrails and documented quality gates

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## omd.8 has no upstream dependency — can execute in parallel with omd.1, but SHOULD depend on omd.1

**What**: `beads-workbench-omd.8` (architecture guardrails) is currently ready to work with zero dependencies. It blocks omd.6 and omd.7. However, the guardrails task needs to enforce import constraints across `internal/` and run `go build ./cmd/bwb` — neither of which is meaningful until the repository bootstrap (omd.1) creates the module, the package layout, and the binary entrypoint.

**Why**: If an agent claims omd.8 before omd.1 completes, there is no `go.mod`, no `internal/` tree, and no `cmd/bwb` to build. The task will either fail or produce guardrail scripts that target a nonexistent codebase. The task is structurally independent but practically requires omd.1.

**Suggested action**: Add dependency: `omd.8 depends-on omd.1`. This does not change the critical path (omd.8 already blocks omd.6 which depends on omd.4/omd.5 which depend on omd.3 which depends on omd.2 which depends on omd.1), but it prevents a premature claim.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## PASS: Gateway-first architecture is now protected by automated guardrails task

**What**: Task beads-workbench-omd.8 ("Add architecture guardrails and quality gates") explicitly requires automated checks that fail when forbidden SQL/BQL/orchestration dependencies are introduced. It requires documenting these guardrails in `docs/CODING.md`. It blocks the test verification task (omd.6) and the acceptance review (omd.7), meaning no gateway work can be accepted without these guardrails in place.

**Why**: This was the single biggest structural gap in the original plan — every task said "no SQL" but nothing enforced it. The guardrails task closes that gap. Combined with the existing per-task forbidden-path lists and the acceptance review checks, the gateway-first architecture now has defense in depth: task-level instructions (human-readable), automated import checks (machine-enforceable), and acceptance review gates (verification checkpoints).

**Suggested action**: None. This is a strong protection. The guardrails task is well-placed in the dependency chain and has clear acceptance criteria.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [PASS] Architecture guardrails task closes the automated enforcement gap

**What**: beads-workbench-omd.8 directly addresses the first-pass finding about no automated architectural enforcement. It requires automated checks that fail on forbidden SQL/BQL/orchestration imports, and adds go build/go vet as standard quality gates. It correctly blocks omd.6 (gateway tests) so the guardrails exist before the test verification pass.

**Why**: This converts the repeated "no SQL, no BQL" instructions from prose conventions into build-time enforcement. Agent verifiers can now run the guardrail checks as part of `go test ./...` rather than manually inspecting imports.

**Suggested action**: No structural change needed. The task is correctly scoped and positioned.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [MINOR] CODING.md guardrails documentation is now planned but only in omd.8 — not in any acceptance review criterion

**What**: Task omd.8 ("Add architecture guardrails and quality gates") correctly requires: "docs/CODING.md documents the enforced architecture guardrails and quality gates." However, the Phase 1 acceptance review (omd.7) says "docs/CODING.md matches the implemented foundation architecture" — which is generic enough that a reviewer might not specifically check whether the guardrails enforcement mechanism is documented in CODING.md.

**Why**: omd.8 is a new task that introduces an important new capability: automated import-path enforcement. If CODING.md documents only the package layout and build commands but doesn't explain that forbidden-import guardrails exist and how they work, a future agent could inadvertently add code that bypasses the guardrails (e.g., a new test file that imports database/sql for "legitimate test reasons" and trips the enforcement). The guardrails are only useful if agents know they exist.

**Suggested action**: This is adequately covered as-is — omd.8's own acceptance criteria require the CODING.md update, and omd.7 depends on omd.8 being closed. The dependency chain ensures the guardrails documentation lands before acceptance. No change needed, but flagging for awareness.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## MINOR: omd.8 has no dependency on omd.1 — guardrails could theoretically be built before the repo exists

**What**: Task omd.8 (architecture guardrails) has no explicit dependency on any prior task. It blocks omd.6 and omd.7 but doesn't depend on omd.1 (bootstrap) or omd.2 (interfaces). In theory an agent could attempt to create guardrail checks before the repo is bootstrapped or before the package layout exists.

**Why**: In practice this is unlikely to cause problems because the guardrails need Go packages to scan and those won't exist until after bootstrap. But the missing dependency means the task could be picked up as "ready" work prematurely. The dependency chain should reflect that guardrails need the codebase to exist first.

**Suggested action**: Add a dependency: omd.8 depends on omd.1 (bootstrap). This prevents premature pickup and makes the execution order explicit.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [MINOR] Architecture guardrails task has no dependency — could start before package layout exists

**What**: omd.8 "Add architecture guardrails and quality gates" has no DEPENDS ON relationship. It blocks omd.6 and omd.7. But the guardrails check import paths across `internal/` — those packages don't exist until omd.1 (bootstrap) creates them.

**Why**: An agent starting omd.8 before omd.1 would be writing import-path checks against empty directories. The checks need actual code to validate against. This is minor because omd.8 blocks omd.6 which depends on omd.4/omd.5, so in practice the code will exist by the time it matters. But the missing dependency creates confusing scheduling.

**Suggested action**: Add dependency: omd.8 DEPENDS ON omd.1 (bootstrap). The guardrails need the package layout to exist.

title: Add editor and launcher integration tests
description:
## Description
Add integration-oriented coverage for the editing and launcher workflows so the app remains stable as the standalone product reaches feature completeness.

## Instructions
1. Use the shared automated TUI and fixture harness defined for the repo.
2. Add tests for the external editor flow using deterministic temp-file and editor-launch seams.
3. Add tests for create/update/close/comment UI flows.
4. Add tests for launcher context interpolation and launcher invocation behavior via the launcher seam.
5. Add at least one embedded-mode smoke/e2e workflow covering an editing or launcher path.
6. Keep tests deterministic and avoid requiring real interactive editor or terminal sessions.

## Files to Modify
- `internal/launcher/*_test.go` (create or update)
- `internal/mode/details/*_test.go` (create or update)
- `internal/app/*_test.go` (update integration coverage if needed)
- `internal/launcher/testdata/` (create if needed)
- fixture-based editor/launcher e2e helpers if needed

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] External editor flow is covered by automated tests
- [ ] Create/update/close/comment flows are covered by automated tests
- [ ] Launcher interpolation and invocation behavior are covered by automated tests
- [ ] At least one embedded-mode editor/launcher smoke/e2e workflow is automated
- [ ] `go test ./...` passes for editor and launcher coverage
close_reason: Added integration-oriented automated coverage for editor flow, mutation flows, and launcher invocation/interpolation; updated golden/help outputs; go test ./... passes.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## yze.4.5 (editor/launcher tests) does not depend on yze.4.3 (launcher framework) — missing dependency

**What**: `beads-workbench-yze.4.5` (editor and launcher integration tests) depends on yze.4.1 (editor flow), yze.4.2 (CRUD actions), yze.4.4 (built-in launchers), and yze.5 (test harness). It does NOT depend on yze.4.3 (launcher framework). However, yze.4.5's instructions say "Add tests for launcher context interpolation and launcher invocation behavior" — the launcher framework IS what provides context interpolation and invocation behavior.

**Why**: Technically, yze.4.4 depends on yze.4.3, so yze.4.5 transitively depends on yze.4.3 through yze.4.4. The dependency is satisfied indirectly. This is not a blocker — just a missing explicit dependency that could cause confusion if yze.4.4's dependency on yze.4.3 were ever removed.

**Suggested action**: No change strictly necessary — the transitive dependency through yze.4.4 covers it. This is a MINOR structural observation, not a blocker.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [MINOR] yze.4.5 depends on yze.4.4 but not yze.4.3 directly — launcher framework seam may not be available

**What**: yze.4.5 (editor/launcher integration tests) depends on yze.4.1, yze.4.2, yze.4.4, and yze.5. It does NOT directly depend on yze.4.3 (launcher framework). Since yze.4.4 depends on yze.4.3, the transitive dependency exists. However, the launcher process seam is created in yze.4.3, and yze.4.5 needs to use it explicitly.

**Why**: This is not a functional blocker (transitive deps cover it) but it obscures the intent. Someone reading yze.4.5's dependencies won't see that the launcher seam from yze.4.3 is a critical prerequisite. If the dependency graph were ever refactored, removing yze.4.4 would silently break the chain to yze.4.3.

**Suggested action**: Add explicit dependency: yze.4.5 depends on yze.4.3. This makes the seam dependency visible and resilient to future refactoring.

title: Acceptance Review: Editor and launcher flows
description:
## Description
Verify that the standalone app supports the editor-friendly and launcher-focused workflows that define the product direction.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All editor/launcher implementation tasks are closed
- [ ] External editor handoff works for rich issue editing
- [ ] Users can create, update, close, and comment on issues from the app
- [ ] Built-in launchers work with issue-context interpolation
- [ ] `go build ./cmd/bwb` passes
- [ ] `go vet ./...` passes
- [ ] Editor and launcher tests pass
- [ ] At least one embedded-mode editor/launcher smoke/e2e workflow passes
- [ ] Documentation reflects the implemented editor/launcher behavior, config, and testing strategy

## Owner
verifier
close_reason: All criteria verified

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Final feature acceptance has no docs gate — v1 ships with stale docs

**What**: This is the last feature acceptance review before the v1 acceptance (yze.1). It checks editor/launcher code and tests but not whether launcher configuration, editor handoff behavior, or context interpolation rules are documented anywhere a user or agent could find them.

**Why**: Launchers use issue-context interpolation (ID, title, labels, project root). Editor handoff has a specific temp-file flow. These are user-facing behaviors with configuration implications. If they are undocumented, users cannot configure launchers and future agents cannot extend them correctly.

**Suggested action**: Add an acceptance criterion: launcher configuration model and editor handoff flow are documented in `docs/CODING.md` or a dedicated `docs/LAUNCHERS.md` if the surface area warrants it.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [PASS] Editor/launcher acceptance review includes docs gate

**What**: yze.4.6 now includes: "Documentation reflects the implemented editor/launcher behavior, config, and testing strategy." This is the last feature-level acceptance before the v1 product review.

**Why**: Editor handoff and launcher interpolation are user-facing behaviors with configuration. Requiring documentation at this gate prevents the v1 product acceptance from being the first time anyone checks if launchers are documented.

**Suggested action**: None. This is adequate.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [MAJOR] Acceptance review criteria still use human-verification language

**What**: Despite the updates, several acceptance reviews still contain criteria that an agent cannot execute:
- yze.2.5: "Top-level mode switching and selected-issue shell state work"
- yze.3.6: "Search works with text and structured filters and no BQL"
- yze.3.6: "Issue details render official-surface metadata and context"
- yze.4.6: "External editor handoff works for rich issue editing"
- yze.4.6: "Built-in launchers work with issue-context interpolation"
- yze.4.6: "Users can create, update, close, and comment on issues from the app"

**Why**: An agent verifier executing these acceptance reviews will encounter "X works" criteria and have no way to verify them except running `go test ./...` and hoping the tests cover the behavior. The criteria SHOULD reference the automated tests that prove the behavior. The verifier's job is to confirm the tests exist and pass, not to manually verify "works."

**Suggested action**: Rewrite "X works" criteria as "X is verified by passing automated tests" throughout:
- "Top-level mode switching and selected-issue shell state work" → "App-shell tests for mode switching and selection state pass"
- "Search works with text and structured filters" → "Search mode tests covering text search and each structured filter pass"
- "External editor handoff works" → "Editor flow tests using fake editor seam pass"
- "Built-in launchers work with issue-context interpolation" → "Launcher tests verifying command construction with interpolated context pass"
- "Users can create/update/close/comment" → "CRUD action tests using fake gateway pass"

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
Acceptance review verification performed (2026-04-07):

Code inspection showed internal/app/model.go handles key 'e' by calling launcher action 'editor' via launchActionCmd rather than editIssueCmd/services.Editor.EditIssue. Verified by passing tests 'go test ./internal/app -run TestModelLaunchEditorActionUsesLauncherService -v -count=1' and '...TestModelEditIssueActionUsesLauncherServiceFromDetailSelection...' which confirm 'e' launches the launcher service, not the rich editor flow. Verified with 'go test ./internal/launcher/... -run TestServiceLaunchInterpolatesIssueContextAndDelegatesRunner|TestServiceBuiltInDefinitionsForV1Actions -v -count=1'; observed PASS and interpolated command/args/env/workdir for opencode plus built-in actions nvim/opencode/shell-command.

No embedded-mode editor or launcher smoke/e2e test found. Bug filed: beads-workbench-p1q.

Sending yze.4.6 back through verification on the reconciled tree.
