**Why**: This isn't a blocker, but the task says "map each board section to supported gateway query types." An agent might interpret this as requiring a dedicated gateway method per dashboard section and find two of the four missing. The instructions should clarify that ListIssues with filters is the appropriate gateway query type for in-progress and recent views.

**Suggested action**: Add a note to the instructions: "In-progress and recent-work views should map to ListIssues with appropriate filter/sort parameters, not to dedicated gateway methods. Only ready and blocked have dedicated gateway methods because they have blocker-aware semantics beyond simple status filtering."

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## "Assigned to current user" dashboard is in ARCHITECTURE.md but not in any task

**What**: ARCHITECTURE.md line 237 lists "assigned to current user" as a supported built-in queue type. The dashboard definitions task (yze.3.1) lists "ready, in-progress, blocked, and recent work" — no "my work" / assigned-to-me view. The product epic success criteria also list "ready, in-progress, blocked, and recent work views." The architecture doc includes it; the tasks don't.

**Why**: "My work" is one of the most useful dashboard views for a daily driver issue browser. Its omission from the task scope means the architecture doc promises something the implementation won't deliver. Either the architecture doc is aspirational and should be clarified, or this is a genuine gap in the task scope.

**Suggested action**: Add "assigned to current user" as a fifth built-in dashboard view in yze.3.1's scope and acceptance criteria. The gateway can serve this through ListIssues with `--assignee` set to the current user (the bd CLI supports `--assignee`). This is a minor scope addition with high product value.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [MEDIUM] DashboardDefinitionProvider interface not explicitly required in this task

**What**: ARCHITECTURE.md defines a `DashboardDefinitionProvider` interface (lines 213-215) and the separation between dashboard renderer and dashboard definition provider is a core architectural decision. This task says "Implement dashboard definition types and a built-in provider" but does not explicitly require a `DashboardDefinitionProvider` interface — only a built-in provider implementation.

**Why**: Without an explicit interface, the implementer may hardcode the built-in definitions directly into the board mode, bypassing the provider abstraction. This would merge rendering and definition loading, violating the architecture and making file-backed definitions harder to add later. CODING.md rule 4 states: "Dashboard renderer and dashboard provider are separate."

**Suggested action**: Add an acceptance criterion: "A `DashboardDefinitionProvider` interface exists as a separate contract from the board renderer." Add an instruction: "The built-in provider must implement a `DashboardDefinitionProvider` interface, not be inlined into the board rendering code."

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## PASS: Dashboard renderer/provider separation is explicitly required

**What**: Task yze.3.1 now includes a `DashboardDefinitionProvider` interface requirement and the instruction to "Keep dashboard rendering separate from dashboard definition loading." The acceptance criteria require that "Dashboard definitions map to supported gateway query types only" and that "No BQL parser or arbitrary query execution is introduced."

**Why**: ARCHITECTURE.md rule 4 and CODING.md rule 4 both require this separation. The updated task enforces it structurally: the provider interface must exist as a separate contract, and the built-in definitions must map to gateway query types, not arbitrary strings. This prevents the board mode from becoming a BQL execution surface.

**Suggested action**: None. The dashboard separation is adequately protected.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [MINOR] "Assigned-to-current-user" dashboard: implementer needs a hint on how to resolve current user

**What**: The updated acceptance criteria include: "Assigned-to-current-user is either implemented or explicitly documented as deferred with a reason." This is a good pattern — it acknowledges the ARCHITECTURE.md feature without mandating it. However, the task instructions don't mention how to determine the "current user" for filtering. The bd CLI may expose this through BEADS_ACTOR or equivalent.

**Why**: If the implementer decides to include the assigned-to-me view, they need to know how bd identifies the current user. Without this hint, they'll either guess or defer it without attempting discovery.

**Suggested action**: Add a note to instruction #3: "If implementing the assigned-to-current-user view, determine the current user identity from the beads environment (e.g., BEADS_ACTOR or equivalent) and pass it as a filter to ListIssues."

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## yze.3.1 (dashboard definitions) "assigned to current user" view is now handled explicitly

**What**: `beads-workbench-yze.3.1` acceptance criteria now include "Assigned-to-current-user is either implemented or explicitly documented as deferred with a reason." This addresses the prior finding that ARCHITECTURE.md lists assigned-to-me as a queue type but the task didn't mention it.

**Why**: Making the decision explicit — implement or defer with documented rationale — prevents silent scope omission. The verifier has a clear check: either the feature exists, or a documented justification for deferral exists.

**Suggested action**: No change needed. The either/or criterion is an appropriate way to handle a feature that may or may not be feasible depending on `bd` CLI capabilities for assignee filtering.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Verifier PASS: dashboard provider contract, built-in definitions, supported query-type mapping, and assigned-to-current-user behavior were verified. go test ./internal/dashboard -cover, go test ./cmd/bwb -run TestArchitectureGuardrails, go build ./cmd/bwb, go vet ./..., and go test ./... all passed.

title: Acceptance Review: Minimal app shell
description:
## Description
Verify that the minimal Bubble Tea app shell is ready for the browsing experience epic.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All app-shell implementation tasks are closed
- [ ] The root app starts through `cmd/bwb`
- [ ] Shared UI primitives exist and are integrated
- [ ] Top-level mode switching and selected-issue shell state work
- [ ] `go build ./cmd/bwb` passes
- [ ] `go vet ./...` passes
- [ ] App-shell tests pass
- [ ] `docs/CODING.md` reflects the implemented shell architecture and config model
- [ ] `docs/TESTING.md` reflects the implemented app-shell testing approach
- [ ] No orchestration or BQL wiring is present

## Owner
verifier
close_reason: Acceptance review passed

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## App shell acceptance review has no docs check

**What**: This acceptance review checks code, tests, and architecture but not documentation. Phase 2 introduces the Bubble Tea app shell, mode switching, and shared UI primitives — all new architectural surface area. Nothing requires `docs/CODING.md` to be updated with the new packages, key bindings, or mode-switching conventions that agents will need.

**Why**: The app shell is where user-visible interaction patterns are established. Future agents implementing board, search, and detail modes need to understand mode contracts, message routing, and service container usage. If these aren't documented at this gate, Phase 3 agents will make inconsistent assumptions.

**Suggested action**: Add an acceptance criterion: `docs/CODING.md` is updated to reflect any new packages, architectural patterns, or mode contracts introduced by the app shell epic.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [PASS] App shell acceptance review now includes docs gates

**What**: yze.2.5 now requires `docs/CODING.md` to reflect the implemented shell architecture and config model, AND `docs/TESTING.md` to reflect the implemented app-shell testing approach. These are explicit acceptance criteria, not suggestions.

**Why**: The app shell introduces significant new architectural surface area (mode switching, services container, UI primitives, config model). Without docs gates at this checkpoint, Phase 3 agents would be working blind. The updated plan prevents this.

**Suggested action**: None. This is adequate.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## yze.2.5 and yze.3.6 acceptance reviews lack explicit go build and go vet criteria

**What**: `beads-workbench-omd.7` and `beads-workbench-yze.1` both explicitly require `go build ./cmd/bwb` and `go vet ./...`. However, `beads-workbench-yze.2.5` (app shell acceptance) and `beads-workbench-yze.3.6` (browsing acceptance) do not include these as explicit criteria. They mention "tests pass" and "no orchestration or BQL wiring" but not the build/vet quality gates documented in CODING.md.

**Why**: The app shell and browsing phases introduce significant new code. A regression that breaks the build or introduces vet warnings could pass acceptance if the verifier only runs `go test ./...` (which only compiles test-referenced code, not necessarily the full binary). The quality gates should be consistent across all acceptance reviews.

**Suggested action**: Add to `beads-workbench-yze.2.5` and `beads-workbench-yze.3.6` acceptance criteria: "`go build ./cmd/bwb` passes" and "`go vet ./...` passes." These are trivial additions that ensure consistency with omd.7 and yze.1.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Verification follow-up completed: added shared loading primitive under internal/ui/loading, updated internal/app/model.go to use it for board/search/detail loading feedback, added app-shell tests, and confirmed go build ./cmd/bwb, go vet ./..., and go test ./... all pass.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Verifier PASS: all app-shell implementation tasks are closed; cmd/bwb starts; shared UI primitives are integrated; mode switching and selected-issue shell state work; go build ./cmd/bwb, go vet ./..., go test ./..., and architecture guardrail checks all pass; docs/CODING.md and docs/TESTING.md match the implemented shell.

title: Implement shell layout mode switching and gateway-backed selection state
description:
## Description
Implement the visible application shell: layout, top-level mode switching, selected-issue state, loading/error feedback, and shared navigation conventions. This task creates the UI structure that later browsing and editing features will plug into.

## Instructions
1. Implement the shell layout for the main app surface.
2. Add top-level mode switching for the future board and search workflows.
3. Define whether the issue details experience in v1 is a persistent detail pane, a dedicated detail mode, or a hybrid pattern, and implement the shell accordingly.
4. Add selected-issue state and wire board/search selection into the shell’s detail presentation model.
5. Define and implement baseline keybinding/navigation conventions for mode switching, selection, and action entry points.
6. Integrate the shared modal/toast/loading/error primitives from the app-shell epic.
7. Keep the shell independent from legacy orchestration or BQL assumptions.

## Files to Modify
- `internal/app/` (update root app behavior)
- `internal/mode/` (create or update mode controllers/contracts)
- `internal/ui/layout/` (create if needed)
- `internal/ui/` (update shared shell rendering helpers if needed)
- `docs/CODING.md` or in-code architecture comments if documenting the chosen detail presentation model is needed

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The shell renders a stable main layout
- [ ] Users can switch between top-level modes
- [ ] Selected-issue state exists and is visible in the shell
- [ ] The v1 detail presentation model is explicit, implemented consistently, and documented for downstream tasks
- [ ] Baseline keybinding/navigation conventions are implemented and documented in code or docs
- [ ] Loading and error feedback is surfaced through shared UI primitives
close_reason: Implemented shell layout with hybrid detail presentation, mode switching, gateway-backed selection state, keybindings, and shared modal/toast loading-error feedback.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## No task addresses keyboard bindings, help system, or navigation UX conventions

**What**: The app shell and browsing tasks specify modes and layout but no task defines the keyboard interaction model — what keys switch modes, what keys navigate lists, what keys trigger actions, whether there's a help overlay or command palette. For a TUI product, the keybinding model is a core UX decision that affects every mode.

**Why**: Without defined conventions, each mode task will invent its own keybindings. The board might use j/k for navigation while search uses arrow keys. Mode switching might use different keys depending on which agent implemented which task. The product will feel inconsistent. Bubble Tea apps need an explicit keymap strategy.

**Suggested action**: Add keybinding/navigation conventions to the app shell task (yze.2.3) or create a brief sub-task: "Define keybinding conventions for navigation, mode switching, and action triggers. Document in CODING.md or a shared keymap module." This doesn't need to be exhaustive — just consistent foundations (vim-style nav? arrow keys? both? how do you access actions on the selected issue?).

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## MINOR: Detail mode still has ambiguous presentation model — "mode" vs "pane" unresolved

**What**: The app shell task (yze.2.3) instruction 3 says "Define whether the issue details experience in v1 is a persistent detail pane, a dedicated detail mode, or a hybrid pattern." This is good — it asks the implementer to decide. But then the detail task (yze.3.4) says "Implement the standalone detail presentation model chosen in the app-shell epic" without being able to verify what was chosen, since the decision is made during implementation of another task with no documented output format for the decision.

**Why**: The board→detail navigation flow is the product's primary user path. If the app shell agent chooses "pane" but documents it poorly, and the detail agent assumes "mode," the two implementations won't compose. The decision needs to be surfaced in a way both agents can reference — code contracts, CODING.md, or an explicit acceptance criterion that names the chosen model.

**Suggested action**: Add to yze.2.3 acceptance criteria: "The v1 detail presentation model (pane vs. mode vs. hybrid) is documented in code or docs so the detail implementation task (yze.3.4) can reference the decision." This is a documentation handoff, not a design change.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [PASS] Shell layout task now addresses keybinding conventions and detail presentation model

**What**: The updated yze.2.3 includes: (1) explicit requirement to choose the detail presentation model (pane vs mode vs hybrid), (2) baseline keybinding/navigation conventions, (3) acceptance criteria requiring the detail model is "explicit and implemented consistently" and keybindings are "implemented and documented."

**Why**: The first review flagged both the detail-mode ambiguity and the missing keybinding conventions. Both are now explicitly required as decisions in the app shell task.

**Suggested action**: None. These critical UX decisions are now properly scoped.

title: Add app-shell tests for startup and state transitions
description:
## Description
Add focused automated coverage for the minimal app shell so later UI work can build on a stable foundation. Cover startup, mode switching, selection state, feedback behavior, and the chosen automated Bubble Tea testing approach.

## Instructions
1. Use the shared automated TUI test harness defined for the repo.
2. Add tests for app startup and root model initialization.
3. Add tests for top-level mode switching.
4. Add tests for selected-issue state transitions.
5. Add tests for loading/error/toast behavior exposed by the shell.
6. Keep tests deterministic and independent from manual terminal interaction.

## Files to Modify
- `internal/app/*_test.go` (create or update)
- `internal/mode/*_test.go` (create or update)
- `internal/ui/*_test.go` (create or update if needed)
- shared TUI test harness packages if integration is needed

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] App startup behavior is covered by automated tests
- [ ] Mode switching is covered by automated tests
- [ ] Selected-issue state transitions are covered by automated tests
- [ ] Loading/error/toast behavior is covered by automated tests
- [ ] Tests run without manual UI inspection
- [ ] `go test ./...` passes for app-shell coverage
close_reason: Verified automated app-shell coverage added

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## App-shell tests (yze.2.4) mention "mode switching" and "toast behavior" but don't specify how to test them without a terminal

**What**: Task beads-workbench-yze.2.4 says "Add tests for top-level mode switching" and "Add tests for loading/error/toast behavior." These are visual behaviors. The task says "Keep tests deterministic and independent from fragile terminal-only integration where possible" — which is the right instinct but gives no concrete technique.

**Why**: Mode switching and toast behavior are Bubble Tea state transitions that produce View() output. Without specifying the technique (construct model → send key messages → assert on state + View output), an implementer may try to test these by running the binary and scraping output, or skip the visual verification entirely. The phrase "where possible" is a hedge that permits exactly the gap we're trying to avoid.

**Suggested action**: Replace "where possible" with concrete direction:
- Mode switching tests: construct root model, send mode-switch key, assert model state reflects new mode AND View() output matches golden file
- Toast/error tests: inject error from fake gateway, assert toast message appears in View() output
- Loading tests: assert loading indicator appears in View() when gateway returns are pending (use channel-based fake)
- Remove the hedge — these tests MUST be terminal-independent, not "where possible"

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [MINOR] Task still says "for example: teatest, golden output tests, model-level message driving" — punt risk on technique

**What**: The task's instruction 1 presents the TUI testing approach as a choice: "for example: teatest, golden output tests, model-level message driving, or an equivalent fully automated harness." This non-committal language in a task whose entire purpose is to ESTABLISH the standard creates a risk that the implementer picks a minimal approach (e.g., model-level assertions only, no golden files) that satisfies the letter of the task but leaves View() output unverified.

**Why**: The downstream tasks (yze.2.4, yze.3.5, yze.4.5) say things like "Automated board tests pass without manual UI inspection" and "Automated detail tests cover representative rendering states without manual UI inspection." If the harness doesn't include golden file comparison, these criteria can be satisfied with model state assertions alone — which proves the state machine works but NOT that the rendered output is correct. The board could render garbage and the tests would pass.

**Suggested action**: Already raised as a separate comment on this task. Reinforcing here: the technique must include View() output verification (golden files or equivalent), not just model state assertions.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Verifier PASS: app-shell coverage now uses the shared TUI harness plus golden View() verification for startup, mode switching, selected-issue transitions, and loading/error/toast behavior. Verified with go test ./internal/app -count=1 -v and go test ./... -count=1.

title: Implement root app model and services container
description:
## Description
Create the root Bubble Tea app model and simplified services container that will host all Beads Workbench modes. This task must derive the new app shell from the standalone project docs and the new gateway/config boundaries, not from the old Perles app wiring.

## Instructions
1. Use `project-plan/ARCHITECTURE.md` and `project-plan/IMPLEMENTATION.md` as the primary design source for the standalone app shell.
2. Do not inspect, copy, or mirror `/home/hans/dev/github/perles/internal/app/app.go`; that donor file is part of the forbidden legacy starting points for this repo.
3. Create the new root app model under `internal/app/` and wire startup from `cmd/bwb/`.
4. Create a simplified services container that depends on the new gateway, config model, and UI services only.
5. Do not copy old orchestration wiring, BQL executor injection, or SQL-era service assumptions.
6. Make the shell ready to host board, search, and detail workflows in later tasks.

## Files to Modify
- `cmd/bwb/main.go` (create or update)
- `internal/app/` (create root app model and services container)
- `internal/mode/` (create shared mode contracts if needed)
- config package under `internal/` (integrate if needed)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `cmd/bwb` starts the new root app
- [ ] A simplified services container exists under `internal/app/`
- [ ] The root app depends on the new gateway, config model, and UI services only
- [ ] No donor app wiring, orchestration, BQL, or SQL-era imports are copied into the standalone app shell
close_reason: Implemented verified root app shell and minimal services container

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [MEDIUM] Services container needs explicit exclusion list, not just "inspect donor for reference"

**What**: This task's instruction 1 says "Inspect `/home/hans/dev/github/perles/internal/app/app.go` only for high-level structural reference." The donor `app.go` imports 35+ packages including `bql`, `orchestration/controlplane`, `orchestration/session`, `orchestration/v2`, `orchestration/workflow`, `infrastructure/sqlite`, `cachemanager`, `pubsub`, `watcher`, and `registry`. The donor `Model` struct embeds a `controlPlane controlplane.ControlPlane` field. The services container (`mode.Services`) includes `Executor bql.BQLExecutor` as a field.

**Why**: "Inspect for high-level structural reference" is dangerously ambiguous when the donor's structure IS the old architecture. An implementing agent will see the services pattern and naturally replicate its shape, potentially adding fields for subsystems that should not exist in the new architecture. The donor's `mode.Services` struct is the single biggest vector for architectural regression.

**Suggested action**: Replace "Inspect ... for high-level structural reference" with: "The donor `app.go` is listed in IMPLEMENTATION.md as a forbidden starting point. Do NOT replicate its services shape. The new services container must contain only: the BeadsGateway interface, a launcher service, and shared UI state. It must NOT contain a BQL executor, orchestration control plane, cache manager, pub/sub, or watcher." Add acceptance criterion: "The services container does not expose BQL, orchestration, SQL, pub/sub, caching, or watcher services."

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## MAJOR: Services container task still says "inspect donor app.go for reference" — this is the #1 regression vector

**What**: Instruction 1 still says "Inspect `/home/hans/dev/github/perles/internal/app/app.go` only for high-level structural reference." But IMPLEMENTATION.md line 328 lists this exact file as a forbidden starting point: "Important donor paths to avoid as starting points: `/home/hans/dev/github/perles/internal/app/app.go`". The task contradicts the implementation guidance.

**Why**: The donor `app.go` is not a safe reference at any level. Its Model struct embeds `controlPlane controlplane.ControlPlane`. Its services container includes `Executor bql.BQLExecutor`. It imports 35+ packages including orchestration, BQL, SQL infrastructure, cache, pubsub, and watcher subsystems. An implementing agent told to "inspect for high-level structural reference" will see these shapes and unconsciously replicate them. This is not a theoretical risk — it is the exact mechanism by which the old architecture propagates into new code.

**Suggested action**: Remove instruction 1 entirely or replace it with: "Do NOT inspect the donor `app.go` — it is listed as a forbidden starting point in IMPLEMENTATION.md. Build the new services container from the architecture doc's specification: BeadsGateway, config model, launcher service, and shared UI state only." Add acceptance criterion: "The services container does not reference or structurally resemble the donor app.go's service fields."

title: Adapt shared UI primitives from the donor repo
description:
## Description
Create the shared UI primitives needed by the standalone app shell by selectively adapting isolated components from the donor Perles repo. This task should migrate only reusable UI building blocks, not legacy architecture.

## Instructions
1. Inspect these donor paths:
   - `/home/hans/dev/github/perles/internal/ui/shared/modal/`
   - `/home/hans/dev/github/perles/internal/ui/shared/toaster/`
   - `/home/hans/dev/github/perles/internal/ui/styles/`
2. Copy or adapt only isolated UI primitives that fit the new standalone architecture.
3. Place the resulting standalone primitives under `internal/ui/` with package boundaries that match the new repo.
4. Remove any assumptions tied to SQL, BQL, orchestration, or the old Perles service container.
5. Add donor-migration rules for reusable UI primitives and forbidden donor paths to `docs/CODING.md`.
6. Keep the API small and focused on shell feedback, shared styling, and reusable rendering helpers.

## Files to Modify
- `internal/ui/modal/` (create or update)
- `internal/ui/toaster/` (create or update)
- `internal/ui/styles/` (create or update)
- `docs/CODING.md` (update donor migration rules)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Shared modal primitives exist in the new repo
- [ ] Shared toast/notification primitives exist in the new repo
- [ ] Shared style primitives exist in the new repo
- [ ] Migrated code is detached from old Perles app/service assumptions
- [ ] `docs/CODING.md` documents donor migration rules relevant to shared UI code
close_reason: Adapted standalone modal toast and style primitives with donor migration rules

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Donor UI primitives don't actually need Phase 1 gateway acceptance to start

**What**: This task depends on `beads-workbench-omd.7` (Phase 1 gateway acceptance review). But the work is purely about copying/adapting isolated UI rendering primitives — modal, toaster, styles — from the donor Perles repo. These components have zero dependency on the gateway. They're rendering building blocks.

**Why**: This false dependency means the UI primitives task is blocked until the entire gateway foundation is accepted. An agent could start adapting styles and modal components the moment the repository bootstrap (omd.1) is done, since those are purely UI concerns. The gateway acceptance gate artificially serializes work that could overlap.

**Suggested action**: Change the dependency from `beads-workbench-omd.7` (gateway acceptance) to `beads-workbench-omd.1` (repository bootstrap). The bootstrap creates the package layout including `internal/ui/` — that's all this task needs. This enables parallel execution: gateway implementation and UI primitive adaptation can happen simultaneously.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Donor-migration rules exist only in project-plan/ — no living developer doc

**What**: The donor/reference codebase rules (what to reuse, what to avoid, forbidden paths) are documented in `project-plan/IMPLEMENTATION.md` and `project-plan/EXECUTION-PLAN.md`. These are planning docs. There is no equivalent section in `docs/CODING.md` that captures the living rules for the codebase — for example, the "no direct SQL" rule is in `CODING.md` but the donor-reuse boundaries and forbidden-path list are not.

**Why**: Four tasks explicitly reference donor paths (yze.2.1, yze.2.2, yze.3.2, yze.3.4). Each links to `project-plan/` for guidance. But once Phase 1 is complete and donor migration is active, agents need the migration rules in the developer doc they actually read (`docs/CODING.md`), not buried in a planning document. Planning docs describe intent; coding docs describe the rules you follow while coding.

**Suggested action**: When the first donor-migration task lands (yze.2.1 or yze.2.2), add a "Donor Migration Rules" section to `docs/CODING.md` that captures: forbidden donor paths, acceptable donor paths, and the adaptation requirements (strip SQL/BQL/orchestration assumptions). This doesn't need its own beads task — it can be an acceptance criterion on yze.2.2 (which is the first heavy donor-adaptation task).

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [LOW] UI primitives task correctly targets clean donors — positive finding

**What**: The donor paths referenced (modal, toaster, styles) have zero BQL/SQL/orchestration imports. Inspection confirms these are genuinely isolated UI primitives.

**Why**: These are safe transplant targets. The task's scope is architecturally sound.

**Suggested action**: None.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## PASS: yze.2.2 dependency on omd.1 (not omd.7) correctly enables early parallelism

**What**: `beads-workbench-yze.2.2` (UI primitives adaptation) depends on `beads-workbench-omd.1` (repo bootstrap), NOT on `beads-workbench-omd.7` (Phase 1 acceptance). This means UI primitive work can start as soon as the repo layout exists, running in parallel with the entire gateway implementation (omd.2 through omd.7).

**Why**: This was flagged as a false dependency in a prior review and has been correctly fixed. UI primitives (modal, toaster, styles) have zero gateway dependency — they are pure rendering components. Allowing them to run in parallel with gateway work is a significant schedule improvement: approximately 5 tasks' worth of gateway work can proceed alongside UI primitive adaptation.

**Suggested action**: No change needed. This is correctly optimized.

title: Acceptance Review: Beads Workbench v1 standalone product
description:
## Description
Verify that the full Beads Workbench v1 product described in `project-plan/` is implemented, integrated, and ready to treat as the standalone successor to Perles.

## Instructions
1. Verify the Phase 1 foundation epic and all follow-up product epics are closed.
2. Verify the app supports built-in dashboard browsing, search, issue details, create/update/close/comment flows, external editor handoff, and external launchers.
3. Verify the product works against an embedded-mode beads repository using official `bd` surfaces only.
4. Run backend, UI, integration, and end-to-end/smoke tests relevant to the product and confirm they pass.
5. Run the standard product quality gates including `go build ./cmd/bwb` and `go vet ./...`.
6. Verify no direct SQL, BQL, or orchestration subsystem is required by the active product path.
7. Verify `docs/CODING.md`, `docs/TESTING.md`, and `AGENTS.md` accurately reflect the implemented product.
8. If acceptance fails, create follow-up issues instead of reopening completed work.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All child epics and their acceptance review tasks are closed
- [ ] The integrated app delivers the v1 workflows defined in `project-plan/PRODUCT.md`
- [ ] Embedded-mode beads compatibility is verified by automated tests
- [ ] Backend, UI, integration, and end-to-end/smoke tests pass
- [ ] `go build ./cmd/bwb` passes
- [ ] `go vet ./...` passes
- [ ] No direct SQL, BQL, or orchestration dependency is required in the active product path
- [ ] `docs/CODING.md`, `docs/TESTING.md`, and `AGENTS.md` match the implemented product
- [ ] Any acceptance failures are captured as new follow-up issues

## Owner
verifier
close_reason: All criteria verified

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Acceptance review tasks cannot be verified by an agent — criteria are human-subjective

**What**: All four acceptance review tasks (beads-workbench-omd.7, beads-workbench-yze.2.5, beads-workbench-yze.3.6, beads-workbench-yze.4.6) and the product acceptance (beads-workbench-yze.1) use criteria like "External editor handoff works for rich issue editing", "Search works with text and structured filters", "Built-in launchers work with issue-context interpolation", and "Top-level mode switching and selected-issue shell state work". These are human-verification phrases, not agent-verifiable assertions.

**Why**: An agent verifier cannot determine whether "External editor handoff works" without either: (a) running the full TUI interactively and observing it (manual), or (b) running automated tests that prove the specific behavior. The acceptance reviews say "tests pass" as one criterion, but the other criteria describe behavior that can only be verified by running the app. This creates a gap where acceptance reviews become rubber stamps — the agent checks "tests pass" and then assumes the subjective criteria are met because the code exists.

**Suggested action**: Rewrite acceptance criteria to be agent-executable. For every criterion that describes behavior ("X works"), replace it with a testable assertion:
- Instead of "External editor handoff works" → "Integration test exercising temp-file-create → editor-open → parse-result → gateway-update pipeline passes with fake editor"
- Instead of "Search works with text and structured filters" → "Search mode tests cover text search, each structured filter, and combined filters against fake gateway data"
- Instead of "Built-in launchers work" → "Launcher tests verify command construction with interpolated issue context for each built-in action"

The verifier's job should be: run `go test ./...`, check for specific test coverage, and inspect test names — not open the TUI manually.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## v1 acceptance review has no documentation completeness criterion

**What**: The final v1 acceptance (yze.1) checks product workflows, tests, and architecture compliance. It does not verify that project documentation is complete, current, or sufficient for ongoing development. There is no criterion for `docs/CODING.md` accuracy, `docs/TESTING.md` existence, or any user-facing documentation.

**Why**: After v1 acceptance closes, the product is "done" and enters maintenance/extension. If docs are stale or absent at this point, every future session — whether fixing bugs, adding features, or onboarding new agents — starts with archaeology instead of documentation. The cost of fixing docs now is near-zero; the cost later is per-session overhead forever.

**Suggested action**: Add acceptance criteria:
- [ ] `docs/CODING.md` accurately describes the current package layout, build commands, and architectural rules
- [ ] `docs/TESTING.md` exists and covers test harness, fixtures, and running conventions
- [ ] `CLAUDE.md` and `AGENTS.md` reflect the actual project state, not scaffolding placeholders
- [ ] Launcher and editor-handoff behavior is documented

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Product acceptance review doesn't verify embedded-mode specifically enough

**What**: Acceptance criterion says "Embedded-mode beads compatibility is verified" but doesn't specify how. There's no task anywhere in the plan that sets up or tests against an embedded-mode beads repository. All gateway development presumably happens against whatever mode the developer's local beads happens to use. Embedded-mode compatibility is a core product principle (PRODUCT.md principle #2) but there's no task that creates a test fixture or verification step for it.

**Why**: Embedded-mode vs. server-mode beads can have different behaviors around command latency, data freshness, and available features. If the entire development and test cycle happens against server-mode, the product acceptance review becomes the first time anyone tries embedded-mode. Finding problems at final acceptance is the most expensive time to find them.

**Suggested action**: Add a concrete verification step to one of the integration test tasks (omd.6, yze.3.5, or yze.4.5): "Verify gateway operations work against an embedded-mode beads repository." This doesn't need a full test suite — just a documented manual or scripted smoke test that confirms the gateway works when BEADS_DIR points to an embedded-mode project.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Product acceptance review (yze.1) says "Embedded-mode beads compatibility is verified" but no task produces this verification

**What**: The final product acceptance (beads-workbench-yze.1) requires "Embedded-mode beads compatibility is verified" as a top-level criterion. However, searching all 24 tasks in the plan, no implementation or test task produces an embedded-mode compatibility test. The closest is omd.6 (gateway fixture tests), but even that task doesn't mention embedded mode.

**Why**: This criterion will fail at acceptance time because no work was planned to satisfy it. The verifier agent will either: (a) create the embedded-mode test ad-hoc during acceptance review (wrong — reviews should verify, not implement), (b) skip the criterion because "the gateway uses `bd` which supports embedded mode" (assumption, not verification), or (c) fail the review and create a follow-up task (wasted cycle).

**Suggested action**: Add an explicit embedded-mode integration test to one of the existing test tasks (probably omd.6 or yze.3.5) or create a new task. The test should:
- Initialize a temp beads repo in embedded mode
- Run representative gateway read/write operations against it
- Verify the full stack (gateway → bd → embedded beads) works without a Dolt server

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [PASS] v1 product acceptance review now has comprehensive docs verification

**What**: yze.1 now requires: "docs/CODING.md, docs/TESTING.md, and AGENTS.md match the implemented product" as an explicit acceptance criterion. Instructions step 7 says to verify these three docs accurately reflect the implemented product.

**Why**: This is the final gate. Having all three key agent-facing docs verified at v1 acceptance means the product cannot be "done" with stale documentation. The earlier per-phase gates (omd.7, yze.2.5, yze.3.6, yze.4.6) ensure incremental accuracy; this final gate is the backstop.

**Suggested action**: None. This is adequate.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## yze.1 (product acceptance) depends on yze.2.6 (config model) directly — but yze.2.6 has no upstream deps

**What**: The v1 product acceptance review (`beads-workbench-yze.1`) lists `beads-workbench-yze.2.6` (configuration model) as a direct dependency. This is correct — the config model is a deliverable that must be verified. However, yze.2.6 itself has NO dependencies and appears as "ready" immediately. Combined with the yze.2.5 (app shell acceptance) also depending on yze.2.6, this means the config model is both a prerequisite for the app shell acceptance AND has no ordering constraint on when it starts.

**Why**: The config model task can be claimed before the app shell, before the gateway, before anything. Since it also blocks yze.2.5 (app shell acceptance), a prematurely-started config task could become a bottleneck if it's done wrong and needs rework — it would block the entire downstream chain.

**Suggested action**: As noted in a separate comment, add `yze.2.6 depends-on yze.2.1` to ensure the config model is designed after the app container exists. The current state where yze.2.6 blocks yze.2.5 but has no upstream ordering is a structural hazard.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [PASS] Product acceptance review has documentation, quality gates, and embedded-mode criteria

**What**: The updated yze.1 includes: go build/go vet gates, embedded-mode verification via automated tests, docs accuracy for CODING.md/TESTING.md/AGENTS.md, and e2e/smoke test requirements. The dependency chain includes yze.5 (test harness) as a direct dependency.

**Why**: All first-review findings about missing acceptance criteria are addressed. The acceptance is now agent-executable.

**Suggested action**: None. Strong enough for execution.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [MAJOR] v1 acceptance requires "The integrated app delivers the v1 workflows" but no task produces an integrated workflow test

**What**: yze.1 criterion says "The integrated app delivers the v1 workflows defined in project-plan/PRODUCT.md" and "Backend, UI, integration, and end-to-end/smoke tests pass." The embedded-mode smoke tests in yze.3.5 and yze.4.5 cover individual surfaces (browsing smoke, editor/launcher smoke). But no task produces a test that exercises a complete v1 workflow across all surfaces: browse board → select issue → view details → add comment → verify comment appears → open in editor → save changes → verify updates.

**Why**: The first-pass review requested this exact e2e workflow. The updated plan partially addresses it with per-surface smoke tests, but there is still no CROSS-SURFACE end-to-end test. Surface-level smoke tests can all pass while the surfaces don't compose correctly. The v1 acceptance verifier will have no automated way to prove "the integrated app delivers the v1 workflows" beyond "each surface's tests pass individually."

**Suggested action**: Add to yze.5 or create a dedicated task: "At least one cross-surface e2e test exercises a complete v1 workflow: board browse → issue selection → detail view → write action → state refresh." This doesn't need to cover every workflow — one complete loop through the product is sufficient to prove integration. Add a corresponding criterion to yze.1: "At least one cross-surface e2e workflow test passes."

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: yze.4.6 has now passed acceptance and closed. Sending final v1 product acceptance back through verification on the current reconciled tree with build/vet passing, full implementation tasks closed, embedded fixture smoke coverage present, and docs updated to current behavior.
