title: Build minimal Beads Workbench app shell
description:
## Description
Build the minimal Bubble Tea application shell that all later product features will sit on. This epic establishes the root app model, shared UI primitives, mode switching, and gateway-backed shell state without reintroducing legacy orchestration or BQL wiring.

## Goals
- Add a simplified Bubble Tea root app and services container
- Reuse only isolated donor UI primitives from `/home/hans/dev/github/perles` where they fit the new architecture
- Establish top-level mode switching and shell state for board/search/detail workflows
- Add app-shell test coverage for startup and state transitions

## Success Criteria
- [ ] A root Bubble Tea app starts through `cmd/bwb`
- [ ] Shared UI primitives exist for styles, modal/toast feedback, and loading/error presentation
- [ ] The shell supports top-level mode switching and selected-issue state
- [ ] The shell is wired to the new gateway, not direct subprocess calls from screens
- [ ] App-shell tests pass
- [ ] No orchestration or BQL wiring is introduced
close_reason: All app-shell tasks and acceptance review completed

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Configuration loading is not covered by any task

**What**: The architecture doc (ARCHITECTURE.md lines 329-341) specifies v1 configuration areas: editor command / $EDITOR fallback, launcher definitions, basic UI preferences. No task in any epic covers configuration loading, parsing, or storage. The app shell task (yze.2.1) creates a "services container" but doesn't mention config. The launcher framework task (yze.4.3) doesn't mention how launcher definitions are configured. The editor task (yze.4.1) references $EDITOR but doesn't address how the editor command is configured beyond the environment variable.

**Why**: Configuration is cross-cutting. Without a task that establishes the config model, each feature task will invent its own: the editor task will read $EDITOR directly, the launcher task will hardcode definitions, and there'll be no unified config file. This is technically fine for v1 if the product is purely env-var and hardcoded defaults, but the architecture doc implies more. If config is intentionally deferred to hardcoded defaults + env vars, that decision should be explicit.

**Suggested action**: Either (1) add a small config task in the app shell epic that establishes minimal config loading (editor command, basic preferences, optional config file path), or (2) add explicit notes to the editor and launcher tasks saying "v1 uses $EDITOR env var and hardcoded launcher definitions — no config file." Option 2 is simpler and probably sufficient for v1.

title: Deliver Beads Workbench v1 standalone product
description:
## Description
Track delivery of the full Beads Workbench v1 standalone product described in `project-plan/`. This product-level epic covers the backend foundation, the Bubble Tea app shell, the browsing/search/detail experience, the editor/launcher workflows, and the cross-cutting testing/documentation work needed for the first complete standalone release.

## Goals
- Complete the standalone repository and official-beads gateway foundation
- Build a minimal Bubble Tea app shell with shared services and UI primitives
- Deliver built-in dashboards, search, and issue detail browsing without BQL
- Deliver create/update/close/comment flows, external editor handoff, and external launchers
- Establish automated TUI, integration, and end-to-end test harnesses that agents can run without manual UI verification
- Keep project docs (`CODING.md`, `TESTING.md`, `AGENTS.md`) aligned with the implemented product
- Validate the integrated product in an embedded-mode beads repository

## Success Criteria
- [ ] The gateway foundation epic is complete and accepted
- [ ] The app shell epic is complete and accepted
- [ ] The browsing experience epic is complete and accepted
- [ ] The editor and launcher epic is complete and accepted
- [ ] Cross-cutting automated TUI/e2e test harness work is complete
- [ ] Built-in dashboards cover ready, in-progress, blocked, and recent work
- [ ] Search supports text and structured filters without BQL
- [ ] Issue details show metadata, content, comments, and dependency context from official surfaces
- [ ] Users can create, update, close, and comment on issues from the product
- [ ] External editor handoff works for rich issue editing
- [ ] External launchers work with issue-context interpolation
- [ ] Backend, UI, integration, and end-to-end tests pass
- [ ] Project docs reflect the implemented product and testing strategy
- [ ] No direct SQL, BQL, or orchestration subsystem is required in the active product path

## Source Documents
- `project-plan/PRODUCT.md`
- `project-plan/ARCHITECTURE.md`
- `project-plan/IMPLEMENTATION.md`
- `project-plan/EXECUTION-PLAN.md`
close_reason: all steps complete

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## No Bubble Tea testing strategy is defined anywhere in the plan

**What**: The entire plan mentions "tests" repeatedly but never once specifies *how* Bubble Tea UI components will be tested programmatically. There is no mention of `teatest`, golden file testing, `tea.Model` unit testing via `Update`/`View` calls, or any other concrete TUI testing approach. The donor repo (`perles`) has 320+ uses of `teatest.RequireEqualOutput` with golden files — this proven pattern is completely absent from the new plan.

**Why**: This is the single biggest testability gap. An agent cannot verify that a board renders correctly, that mode switching works visually, that search results display properly, or that detail views show the right content — unless there is a concrete testing mechanism. Without specifying the testing approach, implementation tasks will either skip UI tests entirely (leaving verification to manual eyeballing) or each task will invent its own ad-hoc approach. Either outcome defeats the goal of agent-verifiable product quality.

**Suggested action**: Add a cross-cutting task (or expand the existing test tasks) that explicitly establishes:
1. `teatest` golden file testing as the standard for all View() output verification
2. `tea.Model` unit testing via Init/Update/View for state machine behavior
3. A `testdata/` golden file convention matching the donor repo's proven pattern
4. A helper package (e.g. `internal/testutil/`) for building test models with fake gateway data

This should be a prerequisite for `beads-workbench-yze.2.4` (app-shell tests) and `beads-workbench-yze.3.5` (browsing tests), not something each test task discovers independently.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## No fake/mock gateway is planned as shared test infrastructure

**What**: Every test task (beads-workbench-omd.6, beads-workbench-yze.2.4, beads-workbench-yze.3.5, beads-workbench-yze.4.5) needs to exercise UI or integration behavior against gateway data, but no task creates a shared fake/mock `BeadsGateway` implementation. The command runner gets test seams (omd.3), but the higher-level gateway interface — which every mode controller and every UI test will depend on — has no planned fake.

**Why**: Without a shared fake gateway, every test task will either: (a) build its own ad-hoc mock, creating inconsistency; (b) depend on a real `bd` CLI and a real beads repo, making tests non-deterministic and requiring environment setup an agent may not have; or (c) skip gateway-dependent tests entirely. This is a structural gap that makes the entire UI testing story fragile.

**Suggested action**: Add an explicit deliverable to `beads-workbench-omd.2` (or a new task in the foundation epic) that creates a `FakeBeadsGateway` implementing the full `BeadsGateway` interface with configurable return values and error injection. This becomes shared test infrastructure for all later epics.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## No embedded-mode beads test fixture is planned

**What**: The product acceptance review (beads-workbench-yze.1) requires "Embedded-mode beads compatibility is verified" but no task in the entire plan creates an embedded-mode beads test repository, explains how to bootstrap one for testing, or provides fixture data that represents a realistic beads workspace. The v1 product principle is "embedded-mode compatibility first."

**Why**: An agent verifying acceptance will need a real or realistic beads repo to run the gateway against. Without a planned fixture, the agent either creates one ad-hoc (unreliable), uses the current repo's own beads data (circular and non-deterministic), or skips the check (defeating the purpose). This is especially critical because the gateway shells out to `bd` — integration tests MUST have a beads repo to operate against.

**Suggested action**: Add a task (likely in the foundation epic or as a cross-cutting test infrastructure task) that creates a reproducible embedded-mode beads test fixture. This should include:
- A script or `TestMain` setup that initializes a temp beads repo with known issues, statuses, types, labels, comments, and dependencies
- Enough data to exercise all gateway read operations (list, ready, blocked, show)
- Enough data to exercise write operations (create, update, close, comment)
- Clear teardown so tests are isolated

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## No documentation tasks anywhere in the v1 plan

**What**: The entire 30-issue plan contains zero tasks dedicated to creating or updating project documentation (`docs/TESTING.md`, `docs/CHANGE-WORKFLOW.md`, updating `docs/CODING.md` as code lands, populating `CLAUDE.md`). Documentation is not mentioned in any task scope, any acceptance criteria, or any acceptance review.

**Why**: This project will be built by AI agents working across sessions. Agents depend on `AGENTS.md` and `docs/` as their primary onboarding surface. If no task ever requires updating docs, the docs will be stale by the end of Phase 1 and dangerously wrong by v1. The "planned layout" in `CODING.md` will describe directories that don't exist alongside directories that do. New agents will make wrong assumptions. Rework follows.

**Suggested action**: Add a documentation concern to the plan. At minimum:
1. Add an acceptance criterion to `beads-workbench-omd.1` (bootstrap) requiring `docs/CODING.md` to be updated from "planned layout" to actual layout, and `CLAUDE.md` to be populated with real build/test commands and architecture overview (its current content is placeholder scaffolding).
2. Add a docs-update acceptance criterion to each acceptance review task (omd.7, yze.2.5, yze.3.6, yze.4.6) requiring that `docs/CODING.md` reflects the current code and any new docs needed for the phase are present.
3. Create `docs/TESTING.md` as part of the first test task (omd.6) — this is the right moment to document the test harness, fixtures approach, and running commands.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## docs/CHANGE-WORKFLOW.md is not needed for v1 — intentional absence

**What**: The standard project docs model includes `docs/CHANGE-WORKFLOW.md` for release/change process documentation. This project currently has no release flow, no CI pipeline, no PR workflow documented or planned in the beads plan. The question is whether this doc should be planned.

**Why**: Beads Workbench is a private single-developer project using `bd` for issue tracking and direct-push git workflow. There is no multi-contributor PR process, no release tagging scheme, and no CI/CD pipeline in the plan. Creating a change workflow doc now would be documenting a process that doesn't exist.

**Suggested action**: Intentionally skip `docs/CHANGE-WORKFLOW.md` for v1. If a release flow or CI pipeline is added later, create the doc at that time. No beads change needed — this is a deliberate non-gap.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## No end-to-end workflow test exists in the plan — agent cannot verify the product works as a whole

**What**: The plan has unit-ish tests per layer (gateway tests in omd.6, app-shell tests in yze.2.4, browsing tests in yze.3.5, editor/launcher tests in yze.4.5) but there is no task that tests a complete user workflow end-to-end. For example: "start app → load board → select issue → view details → add comment → verify comment appears" or "start app → search by status → select result → open in editor → save changes → verify update applied."

**Why**: Layer-by-layer tests can all pass while the integrated product fails. The gateway can correctly decode JSON, the UI can correctly render data, and the mode controller can correctly transition state — but the wiring between them can still be broken. The product acceptance review (yze.1) says "The integrated app delivers the v1 workflows" but provides no automated way to prove this. An agent will be left with "all unit tests pass, I guess it works?"

**Suggested action**: Add a dedicated e2e/smoke test task in the top-level epic that:
1. Initializes a temp embedded-mode beads repo with fixture data
2. Constructs the full app model (real gateway, real `bd`, fixture repo)
3. Exercises at least these workflows programmatically via tea.Model Update sequences:
   - Board browse → select issue → verify detail state
   - Search → filter → verify result set
   - Create issue → verify it appears in list
   - Add comment → verify it appears in detail
4. Can run as `go test ./... -tags=e2e` or similar
5. Does NOT require a human to open the terminal

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Product epic success criteria reference "integration tests" but no task defines what integration means

**What**: The product epic says "Backend, UI, and integration tests pass." Three test tasks exist: omd.6 (gateway tests), yze.2.4 (app-shell tests), yze.3.5 (browsing tests), yze.4.5 (editor/launcher tests). But none of them define what "integration test" means in this context. Are these tests that run against a real bd CLI with a real beads database? Are they tests with mocked gateway calls? The term "integration" is used inconsistently — omd.6 says "use fixtures or test seams," while yze.3.5 says "integration-oriented tests" and yze.4.5 says "integration tests."

**Why**: If "integration test" means "uses a real beads database," those tests need CI infrastructure and a test beads project. If it means "tests multiple packages together with mocked boundaries," that's a different thing entirely. Agents will interpret this differently, leading to inconsistent test strategies across epics.

**Suggested action**: Define the testing vocabulary in one place (CODING.md or the product epic description): "Unit tests mock the gateway. Integration tests exercise multiple packages together with test seams. End-to-end tests (if any) run against a real bd CLI." Then update the test task acceptance criteria to use the defined terms consistently.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## STRENGTH: Separate test tasks per epic ensure test coverage is not optional

**What**: Every epic has a dedicated test task (omd.6 for gateway, yze.2.4 for app shell, yze.3.5 for browsing, yze.4.5 for editor/launcher) that blocks the acceptance review. This structural decision means an agent cannot close an epic without passing through the test task gate.

**Why**: Many plans treat testing as a "stretch goal" or fold it into implementation tasks where it can be quietly skipped. Having explicit blocking test tasks with acceptance reviews that depend on them is a strong structural guarantee. The weakness is in specificity (noted in other comments), not in structure.

**Suggested action**: Keep this pattern. When strengthening the test tasks per other review comments, preserve the blocking dependency chain: implementation → test task → acceptance review.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Donor migration guidance is appropriate and well-bounded

**What**: Every task that references the donor repo (`/home/hans/dev/github/perles`) gives specific absolute paths to inspect, explicit forbidden paths, and clear instructions to "copy or adapt only" isolated pieces. The forbidden-donor list (orchestration, bql, infrastructure, old app.go) is consistent across all tasks. The "inspect for patterns, don't blindly copy" tone is maintained throughout.

**Why**: This is positive confirmation. The donor guidance is the area most likely to cause architecture drift (copying old SQL/BQL assumptions into the new codebase), and the plan handles it well. Each task that touches donor code has both an allow-list and a deny-list.

**Suggested action**: No change needed. The donor migration guidance is sound.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Dependency chain is sound and executable in realistic order

**What**: The dependency graph across all four epics follows a clean linear spine with appropriate parallelism: omd.1 → omd.2 → omd.3 → [omd.4 || omd.5] → omd.6 → omd.7 → [yze.2.1 || yze.2.2] → yze.2.3 → yze.2.4 → yze.2.5 → [yze.3.1 || yze.3.3 || yze.3.4] → yze.3.2 || yze.3.5 → yze.3.6 → [yze.4.1 || yze.4.2 || yze.4.3] → yze.4.4 → yze.4.5 → yze.4.6 → yze.1. No circular dependencies. The acceptance reviews correctly depend on all their epic's implementation tasks. Cross-epic dependencies flow through acceptance reviews as gates.

**Why**: Positive confirmation. The dependency structure is the backbone of execution soundness. This graph would allow 2-3 agents to work in parallel at several points without stepping on each other.

**Suggested action**: No change needed to the dependency structure (though the false dependency on yze.2.2 noted separately should be fixed to improve parallelism further).

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## STRENGTH: Dependency chain prevents skipping test phases

**What**: The dependency chain is well-structured: implementation tasks → test tasks → acceptance review → next epic. For example, yze.3.2/3.3/3.4 (implementation) → yze.3.5 (tests) → yze.3.6 (acceptance) → yze.4.x (editor/launcher). This means an agent cannot proceed to the editor/launcher epic without the browsing tests passing acceptance.

**Why**: This is exactly the right structure for agent-driven execution. The agent cannot skip testing because the next epic's tasks depend on the acceptance review. Combined with the gateway seam architecture, this creates a strong pipeline where each phase is verifiable before the next begins.

**Suggested action**: No change needed to the dependency structure. The improvements needed are all in the *content* of the test and acceptance tasks, not their ordering.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [MEDIUM] No task covers configuration model — v1 config scope undefined

**What**: ARCHITECTURE.md (lines 329-340) and PRODUCT.md both describe v1 configuration areas: editor command / $EDITOR fallback, launcher definitions, basic UI preferences. No task in the plan addresses configuration loading, config file format, or config discovery. The editor task (beads-workbench-yze.4.1) mentions `$EDITOR` but not a config model. The launcher task (beads-workbench-yze.4.3) mentions issue-context interpolation but not how launcher definitions are configured.

**Why**: Without a configuration task, each feature will invent its own config approach: the editor flow will read `$EDITOR` directly, launchers will hardcode definitions, and UI preferences will be compile-time constants. This creates inconsistency and makes it harder to add a unified config later. More critically, launcher definitions need to be user-configurable to be useful — built-in launchers for `nvim`/`opencode`/`tmux` won't cover every user's workflow.

**Suggested action**: Add a task in the app shell epic or as a cross-cutting task: "Implement minimal v1 configuration model" covering: config file discovery, editor command resolution, launcher definition loading, and basic UI preferences. This doesn't need to be complex — even a simple TOML/YAML file with a documented schema would prevent ad-hoc config sprawl.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [LOW] Extension points correctly deferred — positive finding

**What**: The EXECUTION-PLAN.md recommends a Phase 5 epic for extension points: file-backed dashboard definitions, optional caching, federated read mode, small local filter language. The current beads plan correctly stops at Phase 4 (editor/launcher) and does not include Phase 5 tasks.

**Why**: This is architecturally correct. Including extension point tasks in v1 would create pressure to implement them prematurely. The plan's omission of Phase 5 is a strength, not a gap.

**Suggested action**: None. This is a positive finding. The plan correctly defers extension points.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [HIGH] No go vet / build gate in any acceptance review task

**What**: Every acceptance review task (beads-workbench-omd.7, beads-workbench-yze.2.5, beads-workbench-yze.3.6, beads-workbench-yze.4.6, beads-workbench-yze.1) mentions "tests pass" but none require `go vet ./...` or `go build ./cmd/bwb` as explicit acceptance steps. CODING.md defines both as standard quality gates.

**Why**: `go test ./...` does not catch all vet violations, and a passing test suite doesn't guarantee the binary builds cleanly. An acceptance reviewer could approve a phase where `go vet` reports issues or the binary entrypoint has compilation errors outside test scope. These are the project's own documented quality gates in CODING.md.

**Suggested action**: Add to every acceptance review task's criteria: "`go build ./cmd/bwb` succeeds" and "`go vet ./...` reports no issues." These are already in CODING.md — the acceptance reviews should enforce them.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [HIGH] Plan has no automated architectural enforcement — each task bans SQL/BQL but nothing prevents imports

**What**: Every task says "do not introduce SQL/BQL/orchestration." But there is no task for a build-time or CI-time check that would catch a forbidden import. If an implementer adds `database/sql` or copies donor code that transitively imports `bql`, there is no automated gate. The only protection is human review in acceptance tasks.

**Why**: Implementation agents working on later tasks (board mode, search mode, detail mode) will be adapting heavily BQL-coupled donor code. The donor's board.go imports bql directly, the donor's details.go imports bql directly, and every mode controller imports bql through the services container. A single uncaught transitive import reintroduces the forbidden dependency. Human-only enforcement is insufficient when the donor code is this coupled.

**Suggested action**: Add a task (or add to an existing test task) that creates a build-time architecture test: a Go test that inspects import paths across `internal/` and fails if any package imports `database/sql`, any `bql` package, or any `orchestration` package. This is cheap to implement and catches regression automatically. Example approach: `go list -json ./internal/... | jq` or a Go test using `go/build` to inspect imports. Place this in the gateway tests task (beads-workbench-omd.6) or as a new cross-cutting task.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Summary: Plan needs a cross-cutting "Test Infrastructure" task to close the biggest gaps

**What**: The review identified a consistent pattern across all epics: the architecture enables testability, the dependency chains enforce test ordering, but the individual test tasks lack the specificity and shared infrastructure to actually deliver agent-verifiable coverage. The gaps cluster around:
1. No TUI testing technique specified (teatest/golden files)
2. No shared fake gateway
3. No embedded-mode fixture repo
4. No subprocess seams for editor/launcher
5. No e2e workflow tests
6. Acceptance criteria use human-verification language

**Why**: Each gap on its own is addressable. Together, they create a plan where every test task will underdeliver because the test infrastructure doesn't exist yet when the test tasks start. The implementing agent will spend test-task time building infrastructure instead of writing coverage.

**Suggested action**: Add a single cross-cutting task (in the foundation epic or as a new test-infrastructure task early in yze.2) that delivers:
- `internal/testutil/fakegateway.go` — configurable `FakeBeadsGateway`
- `internal/testutil/fixtures.go` — representative issue/dashboard/comment fixture data builders
- `internal/testutil/teatest.go` — helpers for golden file test setup (if needed beyond stock teatest)
- A documented convention: "all UI tests use teatest golden files; all mode tests use FakeBeadsGateway; all subprocess tests use injectable interfaces"
- An embedded-mode beads fixture bootstrap script for integration tests

This task should block all test tasks (yze.2.4, yze.3.5, yze.4.5).

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## PASS: Cross-epic gating through acceptance reviews is well-structured

**What**: The dependency flow between epics is: omd.7 (Phase 1 acceptance) → yze.2.1 (app shell start); yze.2.5 (app shell acceptance) → [yze.3.1, yze.3.3, yze.3.4] (browsing start); yze.3.6 (browsing acceptance) → [yze.4.1, yze.4.2, yze.4.3] (editor/launcher start); yze.4.6 (editor/launcher acceptance) → yze.1 (product acceptance). Every epic-to-epic transition flows through an acceptance review gate.

**Why**: This means no later epic can begin until the prior epic passes formal verification. An agent cannot start building the board renderer until the app shell is accepted. Cannot start the editor flow until browsing is accepted. This is the strongest structural protection in the plan against half-baked foundations.

**Suggested action**: No change needed. This pattern is correct and should be preserved.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## PASS: Donor references are now safe and specific

**What**: Every task that references the donor repo uses absolute paths to specific files, gives explicit forbidden-path lists, and maintains the "inspect for rendering patterns only, do not copy architecture" framing. The forbidden-donor list (orchestration, bql, infrastructure, old app.go) is consistent across all tasks. Safe donor targets (modal, toaster, styles, external_editor.go, issue_markdown.go) have been verified to have zero BQL/SQL/orchestration imports.

**Why**: Donor migration is the highest-risk vector for architecture regression. The plan handles it correctly: each task has an allow-list and a deny-list, the high-risk donors (board.go, details.go) are explicitly flagged as "rendering reference only" with warnings about their BQL coupling, and the low-risk donors (UI primitives, editor helpers) are correctly identified as clean transplant targets.

**Suggested action**: None. Donor reference safety is now adequate across the plan.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## PASS: Test tasks block acceptance reviews — testing is not optional

**What**: Every acceptance review depends on its epic's test task: omd.7 depends on omd.6; yze.2.5 depends on yze.2.4; yze.3.6 depends on yze.3.5; yze.4.6 depends on yze.4.5. Additionally, all UI test tasks (yze.2.4, yze.3.5, yze.4.5) depend on yze.5 (shared test harness). This creates a double gate: test infrastructure must exist before test tasks run, and test tasks must pass before acceptance reviews close.

**Why**: This is the correct structure for agent-driven development where testing cannot be skipped. The dependency chain makes test coverage a hard prerequisite for forward progress, not a "nice to have."

**Suggested action**: No change needed. This two-layer test gating pattern (harness → tests → acceptance) is sound.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## PASS: Acceptance reviews include documentation verification criteria

**What**: All acceptance review tasks (omd.7, yze.2.5, yze.3.6, yze.4.6, yze.1) now include documentation criteria: omd.7 requires `docs/CODING.md` and `docs/TESTING.md` to match the foundation; yze.2.5 requires docs to reflect shell architecture and config model; yze.3.6 requires docs to reflect browsing architecture; yze.4.6 requires documentation of editor/launcher behavior; yze.1 requires all docs to match the implemented product. This addresses the prior review finding about missing documentation gates.

**Why**: Documentation checks at acceptance gates ensure docs stay current as each phase lands. Agents in later phases will have accurate onboarding docs. This was a gap identified in the previous review and has been properly addressed.

**Suggested action**: No change needed. The documentation gates are now in place across all acceptance reviews.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [MINOR] No task requires updating AGENTS.md during implementation — only v1 acceptance checks it

**What**: AGENTS.md is the top-level agent onboarding surface. It currently contains: project identity, a pointer to docs/CODING.md, session completion protocol, and beads integration. During implementation, new conventions will emerge (e.g., test commands, mode patterns, config conventions) that agents need. However, no implementation task or mid-phase acceptance review includes an AGENTS.md update requirement. Only the final v1 acceptance (yze.1) verifies AGENTS.md matches the implemented product.

**Why**: AGENTS.md is less likely to drift than CODING.md because it's structural rather than content-heavy — it mainly points to other docs. The real agent onboarding content lives in docs/CODING.md and docs/TESTING.md, both of which now have per-phase gates. However, if AGENTS.md needs to evolve (e.g., adding a pointer to docs/TESTING.md, adding a "Testing" section parallel to the "Coding" section), that update won't happen until v1 acceptance.

**Suggested action**: This is borderline acceptable. AGENTS.md is thin by design and delegates to docs/. If the plan keeps AGENTS.md as a pointer document (which it currently is), the v1 backstop is sufficient. Consider adding "AGENTS.md references docs/TESTING.md" as a criterion to omd.7 or yze.2.5 — the first acceptance review where TESTING.md exists. This is minor because the downstream docs are well-gated.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [PASS] docs/CHANGE-WORKFLOW.md correctly absent — previous review finding confirmed

**What**: The prior review noted that docs/CHANGE-WORKFLOW.md is not needed for v1 and its absence is intentional. The updated plan does not add it, which is correct. There is no multi-contributor PR process, no release tagging scheme, and no CI/CD pipeline that would warrant this document.

**Why**: Confirming this is still a deliberate non-gap in the updated plan. No action needed.

**Suggested action**: None. Intentional absence is correct.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [PASS] Plan structure is sound and execution-ready at the macro level

**What**: The updated plan addresses the major structural gaps from the first review round. The plan now has: (1) a cross-cutting TUI/e2e test harness task (yze.5) that blocks all UI test tasks, (2) an architecture guardrails task (omd.8) that enforces forbidden imports at build time, (3) a configuration model task (yze.2.6) that prevents ad-hoc config sprawl, (4) acceptance reviews with explicit `go build`/`go vet`/docs gates, and (5) documentation requirements threaded through acceptance reviews. The dependency graph is clean: foundation → app shell → browsing → editor/launcher, with cross-cutting test infrastructure available early.

**Why**: The first review's blockers — no testing strategy, no fake gateway, no embedded-mode fixtures, no architecture enforcement, no docs gates — have all been addressed by new tasks or updated acceptance criteria. The plan now has 33 issues organized across 5 epics with a coherent execution spine.

**Suggested action**: None for the macro structure. The remaining findings below are all MINOR-level refinements, not structural blockers.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Follow-up review summary: Plan is now execution-ready with minor remaining gaps

**What**: This is a follow-up architecture review after the plan was updated with guardrails task (omd.8), configuration task (yze.2.6), TUI test harness task (yze.5), updated donor instructions, tighter acceptance criteria, and explicit BQL-transplant warnings. The five focus areas were re-evaluated.

**Why**: The prior review identified structural gaps. This review verifies whether the updates closed them.

**Findings**:

### PASS areas (8):
- Gateway-first architecture: Protected by omd.8 automated guardrails + per-task forbidden imports + acceptance gates. Defense in depth.
- Donor references: Specific absolute paths, allow/deny lists, "rendering reference only" framing for BQL-coupled donors. Adequate.
- Board transplant safety: BQL-coupled board code explicitly prohibited. Rendering-only reference. Adequate.
- Detail transplant safety: BQL-coupled detail code explicitly prohibited. Rendering-only reference. Adequate.
- Search routing: No-shell-out criterion + gateway-only access rule. Adequate.
- Launcher thinness: Fire-and-forget boundary, no coordination/polling/lifecycle. Adequate.
- Dashboard renderer/provider separation: Interface required, gateway-query-only definitions. Adequate.
- Config model: New task (yze.2.6) prevents ad-hoc config sprawl. Adequate.
- TUI test harness: New task (yze.5) centralizes test infrastructure before UI tests. Adequate.

### MAJOR (1):
- **yze.2.1**: Services container still says "inspect donor app.go for reference" — this file is listed as forbidden in IMPLEMENTATION.md. The instruction contradicts the plan's own guidance and is the single most likely vector for reintroducing orchestration/BQL service fields. Fix: remove or invert the instruction.

### MINOR (5):
- **omd.8**: No dependency on omd.1 — guardrails task could be picked up before repo exists. Fix: add dependency.
- **omd.4**: Acceptance criteria don't name SearchIssues or catalog methods specifically despite instructions requiring them. Fix: add specific checkboxes.
- **yze.5**: No dependency — could be picked up before gateway interface exists. Fix: add dependency on omd.7.
- **yze.2.6**: No dependency — config task could run before app model exists. Fix: add dependency on yze.2.1.
- **yze.2.3**: Detail pane-vs-mode decision has no documentation handoff point. Fix: add criterion requiring the decision to be documented.

### Overall assessment:
No remaining BLOCKERs. One MAJOR that should be fixed before implementation begins (yze.2.1 donor reference). Five MINORs that are dependency hygiene and criteria specificity — worth fixing but not blocking. The plan is execution-ready once the MAJOR is resolved.

**Suggested action**: Fix the yze.2.1 donor reference instruction. Address the dependency gaps in omd.8, yze.5, and yze.2.6. Then begin Phase 1 implementation.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [PASS] docs/CODING.md is likely to stay accurate under current acceptance gates

**What**: The updated plan creates a multi-layered defense for docs/CODING.md accuracy:
1. omd.8 requires updating CODING.md with guardrails and quality gates documentation
2. omd.7 verifies CODING.md matches the implemented foundation architecture
3. yze.2.5 verifies CODING.md reflects the shell architecture and config model
4. yze.2.6 requires documenting config model in CODING.md
5. yze.3.6 verifies CODING.md reflects browsing architecture
6. yze.4.6 verifies documentation reflects editor/launcher behavior
7. yze.1 verifies CODING.md matches the final implemented product

This is four verification gates plus three implementation tasks that directly update CODING.md. The document cannot fall more than one phase behind before an acceptance review catches it.

**Why**: CODING.md is the primary agent reference for build commands, package layout, and architectural rules. Having it verified at every phase gate means drift is caught within one epic's worth of work. This is the right granularity — per-task verification would be excessive, per-product verification would be too late.

**Suggested action**: None. The gate structure is sound.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [PASS] Plan adequately requires docs creation/update as implementation progresses — summary assessment

**What**: Reviewing the full updated plan for documentation readiness across all 33 issues, the docs lifecycle is now:

**Creation points:**
- docs/TESTING.md: created in omd.6 (gateway test docs) and/or yze.5 (full testing strategy)
- docs/CODING.md: already exists; updated in omd.8 (guardrails), yze.2.6 (config model)

**Verification gates (4 phase gates + 1 product gate):**
- omd.7: CODING.md matches foundation, TESTING.md documents test strategy
- yze.2.5: CODING.md reflects shell architecture + config, TESTING.md reflects app-shell testing
- yze.3.6: CODING.md + TESTING.md reflect browsing
- yze.4.6: Documentation reflects editor/launcher behavior + config + testing
- yze.1: CODING.md + TESTING.md + AGENTS.md match implemented product

**Why**: This is a substantial improvement over the original plan (which had zero documentation tasks or gates). Every acceptance review now has at least one docs-verification criterion. The two most important docs (CODING.md and TESTING.md) are checked at every phase boundary. AGENTS.md is checked at v1 acceptance as a backstop.

**Suggested action**: The remaining minor gaps (omd.1 not owning its own CODING.md update, TESTING.md creation race between omd.6 and yze.5, AGENTS.md update timing) are individually commented. None are blockers. The overall docs readiness is adequate for execution.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Execution-readiness verdict: READY — begin execution with minor dependency fixes

**What**: The updated plan is execution-ready. All BLOCKER and MAJOR findings from the first review round have been addressed through new tasks (omd.8 guardrails, yze.2.6 config, yze.5 test harness), updated acceptance criteria, and strengthened instructions. The remaining findings are 4 MINOR items — all dependency scheduling refinements, not content gaps.

**Why**: The plan now has:

**Remaining MINOR items** (fix before or during first sprint):

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter

**What**: The overall dependency graph is sound for sequential phased execution with parallelism within each phase. The critical path is: omd.1 → omd.2 → omd.3 → omd.4/omd.5 → omd.6 → omd.7 → yze.2.1 → yze.2.3 → yze.2.4 → yze.2.5 → yze.3.x → yze.3.5 → yze.3.6 → yze.4.x → yze.4.5 → yze.4.6 → yze.1. This is ready for phased agent execution.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [PASS] Fake seams are now correctly distributed: created during implementation, standardized in harness, consumed by test tasks

**What**: The updated plan follows a consistent pattern for testability seams:
- Command runner seam: created in omd.3, used by omd.4/omd.5 tests
- Editor opener seam: created in yze.4.1, standardized fake in yze.5, tested in yze.4.5
- Launcher process seam: created in yze.4.3, standardized fake in yze.5, tested in yze.4.5
- Fake gateway: created in yze.5, consumed by yze.2.4/yze.3.5/yze.4.5

This "implement seam → standardize fake → consume in tests" pattern is coherent and the first-pass review's recommendation is now partially implemented.

**Why**: Each external dependency (bd CLI, $EDITOR, launcher tools) has a planned seam that makes it testable without the real tool. This is the foundation of agent-verifiable execution.

**Suggested action**: No structural change needed. The remaining gap (error injection in fakes) is noted separately.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Second-Pass Review Summary: Testability and Agent-Verifiable Execution Readiness

### Overall Assessment

The updated plan is substantially stronger than the first pass. The addition of yze.5 (TUI/e2e harness), omd.8 (architecture guardrails), yze.2.6 (config model), inline test requirements in implementation tasks, embedded-mode smoke requirements, and docs gates in acceptance reviews collectively address the major structural gaps from the first review. The plan is now architecturally testable — the seams exist, the fakes are planned, the dependency ordering prevents test-skipping.

The remaining gaps are specification-level, not structural.

### Findings by Severity

**BLOCKER**: None.

**MAJOR (4)**:
1. `yze.5`: No dependency on omd.7 — can start before gateway interface exists, making FakeBeadsGateway impossible to implement correctly
2. `yze.5`: TUI test technique still presented as a menu of options instead of a decision — risks inadequate View() output verification downstream
3. `yze.4.6` (and other acceptance reviews): Six acceptance criteria still use "X works" human-verification language instead of "X is verified by automated tests"
4. `yze.1`: No cross-surface e2e workflow test exists anywhere — per-surface smoke tests can all pass while the integrated product is broken
5. `yze.5`: FakeBeadsGateway scope says "deterministic" but doesn't require error injection capability — downstream error-path UI tests impossible without it
6. `yze.3.5`: No task requires testing the board→detail cross-mode navigation flow — the primary user workflow

**MINOR (5)**:
1. `yze.2.6`: Config task has no dependencies — can start before app model exists
2. `omd.6`: Still overlaps with omd.3/4/5 test scope — should be narrowed to cross-cutting concerns
3. `yze.3.5`: Browsing test task covers three UI surfaces in one task — risk of under-coverage on later surfaces
4. `yze.4.5`: Missing explicit dependency on yze.4.3 (launcher framework seam)
5. `yze.3.5`/`yze.3.2`/`yze.3.4`/`yze.4.2`: Error-path UI rendering has no test requirement anywhere except search empty states

**PASS (9)**:
1. `yze.5`: TUI test harness task addresses the primary first-pass gap — correct scope and blocking position
2. `omd.8`: Architecture guardrails close the automated enforcement gap
3. `yze.2.6`: Config model closes ad-hoc config sprawl gap
4. `omd.7` (all acceptance reviews): Now include docs gates and go build/go vet quality commands
5. `yze.3.5`/`yze.4.5`: Embedded-mode smoke requirements now in browsing and editor test tasks
6. `yze.3.2`/`yze.3.3`/`yze.3.4`/`yze.4.1`/`yze.4.2`/`yze.4.3`/`yze.4.4`: Implementation tasks now require inline tests with testability seams
7. `yze.4.1`: Editor flow requires replaceable seam — agent-testable
8. `yze.4.3`: Launcher framework requires replaceable process seam — agent-testable
9. `yze.4.4`: Built-in launcher scope reduced to testable minimum
10. Overall dependency chain is sound
11. Fake seam distribution follows correct implement→standardize→consume pattern
12. docs/TESTING.md lifecycle is planned correctly

### Remaining Workflows Requiring Manual UI Verification

After the MAJOR findings are addressed, the only remaining area that partially resists pure automation is **visual rendering correctness beyond golden file matching** — if a golden file is wrong (approved with bad output), no automated test catches it. This is inherent to golden file testing and acceptable for v1. All functional behavior (state transitions, data correctness, error handling, command construction) can be fully agent-verified.

title: Acceptance Review: Phase 1 gateway foundation
description:
## Description
Verify that the Phase 1 repository foundation and backend seam are ready for the minimal app-shell phase. This task serves as the blocking acceptance review for the epic and should be completed by verification after all implementation tasks are closed.

## Instructions
1. Verify all Phase 1 implementation tasks under the epic are closed.
2. Confirm the repository contains a working `bwb` entrypoint.
3. Confirm the active gateway path depends on official beads surfaces only.
4. Confirm no direct SQL, BQL, or orchestration dependency is required by the foundation.
5. Run the standard quality gates for the foundation, including `go build ./cmd/bwb`, `go vet ./...`, and the project test suite relevant to the foundation.
6. Verify `docs/CODING.md` reflects the actual foundation architecture and quality gates.
7. Verify `docs/TESTING.md` exists and documents the gateway/test-harness expectations introduced by Phase 1.
8. If acceptance fails, create follow-up bugs or tasks instead of reopening completed work.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All implementation tasks in the epic are closed
- [ ] `bwb` entrypoint exists in the repository
- [ ] The active code path depends on official beads surfaces only
- [ ] No direct SQL, BQL, or orchestration dependency is required for the gateway foundation
- [ ] `go build ./cmd/bwb` passes
- [ ] `go vet ./...` passes
- [ ] Tests for the gateway foundation pass
- [ ] `docs/CODING.md` matches the implemented foundation architecture
- [ ] `docs/TESTING.md` documents the implemented foundation test strategy
- [ ] Any acceptance failures are captured as new follow-up issues

## Owner
verifier
close_reason: All criteria verified

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Acceptance review does not check documentation state

**What**: This acceptance review verifies code, tests, and architecture compliance but has no criterion checking that project docs are current. After Phase 1 closes, `docs/CODING.md` may still say "planned layout," `CLAUDE.md` may still have placeholder sections, and no testing doc may exist despite a full test suite being present.

**Why**: This is the gate between "foundation" and "UI work." Every agent working on Phase 2+ will read the docs first. If docs are stale or missing at this gate, the problem compounds across all subsequent phases. Acceptance reviews are the natural checkpoint for documentation health.

**Suggested action**: Add acceptance criteria:
- [ ] `docs/CODING.md` accurately reflects the current package layout and build commands
- [ ] `CLAUDE.md` placeholder sections are populated with real project information
- [ ] `docs/TESTING.md` exists and documents the test harness and fixture approach
- [ ] No "planned" or "will be created" language remains in docs for things that now exist

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Acceptance reviews say "If acceptance fails, create follow-up issues" — but don't say what AUTOMATED checks to run

**What**: All acceptance review tasks (omd.7, yze.2.5, yze.3.6, yze.4.6, yze.1) include "If acceptance fails, create follow-up bugs or tasks." This is good process. However, the reviews' actual check is vague: they say "Verify X works" and "Run tests." An agent verifier needs a concrete checklist of automated commands to execute.

**Why**: An agent running an acceptance review should be able to: (1) run `go test ./...`, (2) run `go build ./cmd/bwb`, (3) run `go vet ./...`, (4) check specific test names/counts for coverage assertions, (5) check that no forbidden imports exist (SQL, BQL). Without specifying these commands, the agent verifier will run `go test` and call it done, even if coverage is inadequate.

**Suggested action**: Add to each acceptance review task an "Automated verification commands" section:
```
go build ./cmd/bwb
go test ./...
go vet ./...
# Check no forbidden imports
grep -r "database/sql" internal/ && exit 1 || true
# Check test count minimums (specific to each epic)
go test -v ./internal/gateway/beads/... 2>&1 | grep -c "^--- PASS" # expect >= N
```

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [PASS] Phase 1 acceptance review now enforces docs alignment

**What**: omd.7 now includes criteria for `docs/CODING.md` matching the implemented foundation architecture AND `docs/TESTING.md` documenting the implemented test strategy. Instructions 6 and 7 explicitly require verifying these docs exist and are current.

**Why**: This was a blocker-level gap in the original plan. The updated plan closes it. An agent running the Phase 1 acceptance review now has concrete, verifiable docs gates that prevent stale documentation from passing through to Phase 2.

**Suggested action**: None. This is adequate.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter

The first-pass findings about missing build gates and documentation checkpoints have been addressed.

**Why**: These are agent-executable checks. An agent verifier can run the build/vet commands and inspect doc accuracy without subjective judgment. This closes the gap where acceptance reviews were rubber stamps.

**Suggested action**: No change needed. The acceptance reviews now contain the right automated gates.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## PASS: Acceptance reviews include quality gate commands (go build, go vet)

**What**: Acceptance review tasks omd.7 and yze.1 now explicitly require `go build ./cmd/bwb` and `go vet ./...` as acceptance criteria. The omd.8 task (architecture guardrails) also establishes these as standard quality gates with documentation in `docs/CODING.md`. This addresses the prior review finding about missing build/vet gates.

**Why**: Having build and vet as explicit acceptance criteria means the verifier agent has concrete automated commands to run, not just subjective "it works" assessments. Combined with omd.8's guardrail enforcement, this creates a strong automated quality baseline.

**Suggested action**: No change needed. Verify that yze.2.5 and yze.3.6 also include `go build` and `go vet` checks — currently they don't explicitly list them, relying on the "tests pass" criterion. Consider adding explicit build/vet criteria to all intermediate acceptance reviews for consistency.

**What**: The updated omd.7 includes: `go build ./cmd/bwb` passes, `go vet ./...` passes, `docs/CODING.md` matches the implemented foundation, and `docs/TESTING.md` documents the test strategy. This addresses the first review's finding about missing quality gates and documentation checks in acceptance reviews.

**Why**: The acceptance review is now agent-executable. A verifier agent can run the build/vet commands, check test output, and verify doc accuracy — all without manual UI inspection.

**Suggested action**: None. This is now strong enough.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
Acceptance review performed:

title: Verify gateway behavior with tests and fixture coverage
description:
## Description
Make the gateway foundation safe enough for UI work to begin by adding focused automated coverage around command building, decoding, failure behavior, and any remaining cross-cutting gateway verification not already required by the implementation tasks.

## Instructions
1. Add focused automated tests for command construction across representative read and write flows.
2. Add decoding tests for representative payloads returned by official `bd` commands.
3. Add failure-case tests for process failures, invalid output, missing fields, and command stderr handling.
4. Use fixtures or test seams where helpful so coverage is deterministic and not tied to fragile shell state.
5. Define the initial test vocabulary for this repo (`unit`, `integration`, `end-to-end`/`smoke`) so later UI work uses consistent terminology.
6. Document any known limitations in the official command surface that affect the gateway design.
7. Create or update `docs/TESTING.md` with the initial gateway test strategy, commands, and test vocabulary.
8. Ensure tests run with standard Go tooling.

## Files to Modify
- `internal/gateway/beads/*_test.go` (create/update)
- `internal/gateway/beads/testdata/` (create fixtures if needed)
- Repo docs or code comments for known gateway limitations (update where appropriate)
- `docs/TESTING.md` (create or update)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Tests cover representative command building for read and write flows
- [ ] Tests cover representative decoding cases
- [ ] Tests cover representative failure scenarios and error normalization
- [ ] The repo’s initial unit/integration/end-to-end test vocabulary is defined
- [ ] Any known command-surface limitations are documented
- [ ] Gateway-related test harness/fixture guidance is documented in `docs/TESTING.md`
- [ ] `go test ./...` passes for the gateway foundation
- [ ] No new SQL, BQL, or orchestration dependency is introduced by the test strategy
close_reason: Added gateway fixture coverage and Phase 1 testing documentation

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Missing docs/TESTING.md — test strategy lives nowhere persistent

**What**: This task creates the first real test infrastructure (fixtures, seams, failure-case coverage) and acceptance criterion 4 says "Any known command-surface limitations are documented." But there is no mention of creating `docs/TESTING.md` or documenting the test harness, fixture approach, or running conventions anywhere agents can find them later.

**Why**: After this task, the project will have a non-trivial test setup: fixture files in `testdata/`, command runner test seams, JSON decoding tests. Three more test tasks follow (yze.2.4, yze.3.5, yze.4.5), each adding different test flavors. Without a central testing doc, every future agent will reverse-engineer the test approach from code. Worse, they may introduce inconsistent patterns because nothing documents the intended approach.

**Suggested action**: Add an acceptance criterion: "A `docs/TESTING.md` exists documenting the test harness design, fixture conventions, how to run tests, and the intended approach for gateway vs. UI vs. integration tests." This is the natural moment — the person writing the first tests knows the design choices.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Task 6 (test verification) substantially overlaps with Tasks 3, 4, and 5 test scope

**What**: Tasks 3 (command runner), 4 (read ops), and 5 (write ops) all include `*_test.go` in their "Files to Modify" and have test-related acceptance criteria. Task 6 then adds another pass of "test command building, test JSON decoding, test failure cases, test representative read and write flows." The scope of task 6 is almost entirely a re-statement of the test expectations already distributed across tasks 3-5.

**Why**: An agent implementing task 4 will add tests for read operations (its acceptance criteria require it). Then an agent implementing task 6 will re-examine the same code and either (a) find the tests already exist and have nothing meaningful to add, or (b) add redundant tests that duplicate coverage. Task 6's unique contribution — "document known limitations" — is a single deliverable buried in a full test-task wrapper.

**Suggested action**: Simplify task 6 to focus on what tasks 3-5 explicitly don't cover: (1) cross-cutting failure mode tests (e.g., bd binary not found, permission errors, network timeouts), (2) documenting known command-surface limitations, and (3) a verification pass that confirms test coverage from tasks 3-5 meets the epic's acceptance bar. Alternatively, remove task 6 entirely and distribute its unique items (limitation docs, cross-cutting failure tests) into tasks 3-5. This reduces the epic from 7 tasks to 6 without losing coverage.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Gateway test task (omd.6) is a good idea but comes too late — tests should be required in implementation tasks

**What**: Task beads-workbench-omd.6 ("Verify gateway behavior with tests and fixture coverage") is a standalone test task that runs after omd.4 (reads) and omd.5 (writes). However, omd.4 and omd.5 each say tests should exist ("create/update tests" in Files to Modify) but their acceptance criteria don't require passing tests. The test burden is deferred to omd.6.

**Why**: Deferring all test coverage to a single task after implementation creates a risky pattern: the implementing agent closes omd.4 and omd.5 with "tests exist" (files were created) but the tests may be incomplete, trivial, or wrong. Then omd.6 becomes "verify and fill gaps" which is actually "write most of the tests." This is a common planning antipattern where test tasks become afterthoughts. In practice, omd.6 will likely just run `go test ./...` and confirm the tests from omd.4/omd.5 pass.

**Suggested action**: Strengthen omd.4 and omd.5 acceptance criteria to include:
- "Tests for command construction, JSON decoding, and failure handling for each implemented operation pass"
- Then reframe omd.6 as focused on cross-cutting concerns: fixture data, edge cases, known-limitation documentation, and integration-level gateway tests that exercise the real `bd` CLI against a fixture repo

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [MINOR] docs/TESTING.md creation timing has a potential race between omd.6 and yze.5

**What**: Task omd.6 says "Create or update docs/TESTING.md if gateway-specific harness/fixture usage needs to be documented there." Task yze.5 says "Create docs/TESTING.md" as a primary deliverable. The dependency graph shows omd.6 does NOT depend on yze.5, and yze.5 does NOT depend on omd.6. They are independent in the dependency chain. omd.7 (Phase 1 acceptance) depends on omd.6 and requires "docs/TESTING.md documents the implemented foundation test strategy." But yze.5 blocks yze.2.4, yze.3.5, and yze.4.5 — not omd.6 or omd.7.

**Why**: This creates an ambiguous ownership question: does omd.6 create the initial TESTING.md (since omd.7 verifies it exists), or does yze.5 create it? If an agent executes omd.6 before yze.5, they will create a minimal TESTING.md to satisfy omd.7's acceptance gate. Then yze.5 will either need to merge into or overwrite it. Conversely, if yze.5 runs first, omd.6's "create or update" is fine. The execution order is unspecified.

**Suggested action**: Clarify one of two approaches: (a) Make omd.6 the initial creator of docs/TESTING.md for gateway-specific content, and yze.5 the task that expands it with TUI testing strategy. This is the most natural flow since omd is earlier in the logical execution order. Or (b) add a dependency: omd.6 depends on yze.5, ensuring the TESTING.md framework exists before gateway test docs are added. Option (a) is simpler — just clarify in yze.5 that it should "create or expand" docs/TESTING.md rather than "create" (implying from scratch).

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [MINOR] omd.6 still overlaps substantially with omd.3/omd.4/omd.5 test scope

**What**: The first-pass review noted that omd.6 (gateway test verification) overlaps with the test deliverables already required by omd.3, omd.4, and omd.5. The updated plan strengthened inline test requirements in those tasks but did not narrow omd.6's scope. omd.6 still says "Add focused automated tests for command construction across representative read and write flows" and "Add decoding tests for representative payloads" — work that omd.3/omd.4/omd.5 already require. Meanwhile, omd.6 now depends on omd.8 (architecture guardrails), which is new and correct.

**Why**: The overlap creates ambiguity about where the "real" test coverage lives. If omd.4 writes read-operation tests (as its criteria require), then omd.6 either finds those tests already exist (making it a verification-only pass) or adds redundant tests. The task should be reframed to focus on what the implementation tasks explicitly DON'T cover.

**Suggested action**: Narrow omd.6's scope to: (1) cross-cutting verification that `go test ./...` passes for the entire gateway, (2) architecture guardrail tests are running and passing, (3) cross-cutting failure modes not covered by individual tasks (bd binary missing, permission errors), (4) known-limitation documentation, (5) docs/TESTING.md creation. Remove the redundant "command construction" and "decoding" scope that omd.3/omd.4/omd.5 already own.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## PASS: omd.6 depends on omd.8 — guardrails exist before test verification

**What**: `beads-workbench-omd.6` (gateway tests) depends on `beads-workbench-omd.8` (architecture guardrails). This means the automated import-checking guardrails will be in place before the test verification task runs.

**Why**: This is a strong ordering choice. The test verification task can then include the guardrail checks as part of its verification — confirming that no forbidden imports snuck through during omd.3/omd.4/omd.5 implementation. The guardrails become part of the test suite, not a separate manual step.

**Suggested action**: No change needed. This is correctly structured. The guardrail task should be made to depend on omd.1 (noted separately) but its position as a blocker for omd.6 is correct.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [MINOR] omd.6 still overlaps with omd.3/4/5 test scope but has enough unique deliverables

**What**: omd.6 (gateway test verification) still includes instructions that overlap with omd.3, omd.4, and omd.5 test requirements. However, the updated task now focuses on "cross-cutting gateway verification not already required by the implementation tasks" and has unique deliverables: docs/TESTING.md creation, known-limitations documentation, and dependency on omd.8 (guardrails).

**Why**: This is MINOR because: (1) the "cross-cutting" framing differentiates it enough, (2) docs/TESTING.md is a concrete unique output, and (3) a reasonable implementer will focus on gaps rather than duplicating prior work.

**Suggested action**: Acceptable as-is. No change needed for execution readiness.

title: Implement gateway write operations through official beads commands
description:
## Description
Complete the first end-to-end backend seam by implementing the core write operations through official `bd` commands. This makes the new gateway useful for real issue mutation flows before the UI is built.

## Instructions
1. Implement gateway support for create issue, update issue, close issue, and add comment operations.
2. Use the shared command runner and normalized error model.
3. Use this command baseline:
   - `bd create`
   - `bd update`
   - `bd close`
   - `bd comments add`
4. Map gateway input models to official command arguments without introducing unofficial backend access.
5. Keep error handling consistent with the read-path behavior.
6. Do not add SQL, BQL, or orchestration dependencies.

## Files to Modify
- `internal/gateway/beads/` (implement write operations)
- `internal/domain/` (adjust mutation models only if needed)
- `*_test.go` under gateway packages (create/update tests)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Create issue is available through the gateway interface
- [ ] Update issue is available through the gateway interface
- [ ] Close issue is available through the gateway interface
- [ ] Add comment is available through the gateway interface
- [ ] Write-operation failures surface through the same normalized gateway error model used by reads
- [ ] No unofficial backend access is introduced
close_reason: Implemented and verified official CLI-backed write gateway operations

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Verifier found Phase 1 write-path gaps now tracked in beads-workbench-5d4 and beads-workbench-hbr. omd.5 remains in progress until those defects are fixed and re-verified.

title: Implement gateway read operations through official beads commands
description:
## Description
Deliver the first useful read path for the future UI by implementing typed read operations on top of official `bd` commands only. This establishes the production data-loading seam without SQL or BQL.

## Instructions
1. Implement `ListIssues`, `ReadyIssues`, `BlockedIssues`, `ShowIssue`, and `SearchIssues` on the CLI-backed gateway.
2. Implement catalog reads needed by the UI and write flows, including status, type, and label catalogs.
3. Use the shared command runner and decoding helpers from the prior task.
4. Map JSON output from official commands into the domain models defined for Beads Workbench.
5. Use the official command surface available in this environment, such as `bd list --json`, `bd ready --json`, `bd blocked --json`, `bd show --json`, and any official catalog/search commands exposed by `bd`.
6. Normalize command and decode failures into the gateway error model.
7. Do not introduce any direct SQL reads, schema inspection, or BQL execution.

## Files to Modify
- `internal/gateway/beads/` (implement read operations)
- `internal/domain/` (adjust models only if needed)
- `*_test.go` under gateway packages (create/update tests)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `ListIssues` returns typed issue summaries
- [ ] `ReadyIssues` returns typed ready-work models
- [ ] `BlockedIssues` returns typed blocked-work models
- [ ] `ShowIssue` returns a typed issue detail model
- [ ] `SearchIssues` returns typed search results through the gateway
- [ ] Status, type, and label catalogs are available through the gateway
- [ ] Official `bd` output is mapped without SQL or BQL dependencies
- [ ] Failures surface through the normalized gateway error model
close_reason: Implemented and verified official CLI-backed read and catalog gateway operations

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Read operations scope is missing catalog queries and search

**What**: This task implements ListIssues, ReadyIssues, BlockedIssues, and ShowIssue. It does not implement StatusCatalog, TypeCatalog, LabelCatalog, or SearchIssues. The architecture doc specifies catalog methods on the gateway. The bd CLI has `bd statuses --json`, `bd types --json`, `bd label list-all`, and `bd search --json` — all are official surfaces. The browsing epic (yze.3.3) requires structured filters for status/type/priority/assignee/labels, and the search mode needs a gateway-backed search operation.

**Why**: Without catalog queries in Phase 1, the search mode task (yze.3.3) will need to either add gateway methods itself (scope creep on a UI task) or hardcode values (fragile, breaks for projects with custom statuses/types). Without a SearchIssues gateway method, the search mode will have to choose between shelling out directly (violating architecture) or abusing ListIssues with filters (which may not match `bd search` semantics for text queries).

**Suggested action**: Add StatusCatalog, TypeCatalog, LabelCatalog, and SearchIssues to this task's scope and command baseline. The commands are: `bd statuses --json`, `bd types --json`, `bd label list-all --json`, `bd search <query> --json`. This keeps all gateway read operations in one place and avoids retroactive Phase 1 changes.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [MEDIUM] StatusCatalog and TypeCatalog read operations not included in gateway reads task

**What**: This task implements `ListIssues`, `ReadyIssues`, `BlockedIssues`, and `ShowIssue`, but does not mention `StatusCatalog` or `TypeCatalog`. These are read operations defined in ARCHITECTURE.md's gateway interface (lines 101-102) and are needed before the UI can render create/update forms.

**Why**: Status and type catalogs are metadata reads that support the write flows in beads-workbench-yze.4.2 (create/update/close). If they are not implemented in the gateway reads task, they will be missing when the UI tasks need them, causing either a gap in the write UI or ad-hoc implementation outside the gateway.

**Suggested action**: Add `StatusCatalog` and `TypeCatalog` to the read operations scope, or create a small follow-up task in the gateway epic for catalog operations. The command baseline should include whatever `bd` command surfaces these (likely `bd` metadata commands or parsing from list output).

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## MINOR: Gateway read ops (omd.4) includes catalog and search but acceptance criteria only name 4 specific methods

**What**: The instructions say "Implement catalog reads needed by the UI and write flows, including status, type, and label catalogs" (instruction 2) and "Implement `ListIssues`, `ReadyIssues`, `BlockedIssues`, `ShowIssue`, and `SearchIssues`" (instruction 1). But the acceptance criteria only name ListIssues, ReadyIssues, BlockedIssues, and ShowIssue as specific checkboxes. SearchIssues and catalog reads are covered by the vaguer criterion "Search and catalog reads needed by the UI are available through the gateway."

**Why**: Acceptance criteria drive verification. An agent verifier checking this task will confirm the 4 named methods exist and pass the vague criterion with minimal effort. SearchIssues and the catalog methods deserve their own acceptance checkboxes to match the instruction specificity. Otherwise the instructions promise more than the criteria enforce.

**Suggested action**: Add specific acceptance criteria: "SearchIssues returns typed search results", "StatusCatalog returns available status options", "TypeCatalog returns available type options", "LabelCatalog returns available label options" (or whatever the actual catalog surface is).

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## omd.4 and omd.5 now include catalog reads and search — scope gap is closed

**What**: `beads-workbench-omd.4` now includes "Implement catalog reads needed by the UI and write flows, including status, type, and label catalogs" in its instructions and "Search and catalog reads needed by the UI are available through the gateway" in its acceptance criteria. `beads-workbench-omd.2` includes "catalog/query methods needed by the UI and write flows, including status, type, and label catalogs" in its instructions. This addresses the prior review finding about missing catalog methods.

**Why**: The search and catalog operations being part of the gateway foundation means the browsing and CRUD tasks (yze.3.3, yze.4.2) will have the gateway methods they need without scope creep or architecture violations. This is a clean resolution of the cross-epic dependency gap.

**Suggested action**: No change needed. The catalog and search gaps identified in the prior review are now addressed in the task scope.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Verifier found Phase 1 read-path gaps now tracked in beads-workbench-5d4, beads-workbench-dr3, and beads-workbench-epz. omd.4 remains in progress until those defects are fixed and re-verified.

title: Implement shared bd command runner and CLI decoding helpers
description:
## Description
Create one reusable command execution layer for all future beads gateway operations. This keeps subprocess logic, environment propagation, JSON decoding, and error normalization out of feature code and makes the CLI-backed gateway testable.

## Instructions
1. Implement a shared `bd` command runner under `internal/gateway/beads/` or a closely related package.
2. Support working-directory selection and environment propagation for beads commands.
3. Add helpers for executing JSON-returning commands and decoding structured output.
4. Normalize subprocess failures, stderr content, exit-code failures, and decode failures into the gateway error model.
5. Add test seams so command execution can be exercised without fragile shell-dependent end-to-end tests.
6. Keep this layer generic enough for all planned read and write gateway methods.

## Files to Modify
- `internal/gateway/beads/` (create runner and decoding helpers)
- `internal/domain/` (update if shared result/error models need support)
- `*_test.go` under gateway packages (create tests)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] One shared command execution layer exists for `bd` interactions
- [ ] Working directory and environment propagation are supported
- [ ] JSON command execution and decoding helpers exist
- [ ] Process, stderr, exit-code, and decode failures map into normalized gateway errors
- [ ] Tests can exercise command behavior without fragile shell-only coupling
close_reason: Implemented shared bd command runner with workdir/env support, JSON decode helpers, normalized gateway errors, and seam-driven unit tests for execution behavior.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## STRENGTH: Command runner test seams (omd.3) are explicitly required

**What**: Task beads-workbench-omd.3 explicitly requires "Build test seams for command execution" and "Tests can exercise command behavior without fragile shell-only coupling." This is one of the few places where the plan correctly requires testability as part of the implementation — not as an afterthought in a separate test task.

**Why**: The command runner is the lowest-level boundary between the app and the external `bd` CLI. Having test seams here means all gateway-level tests can avoid subprocess execution, which is exactly right for agent-automated testing. This is the model that should be replicated for the editor opener and launcher executor.

**Suggested action**: Use omd.3 as the template when strengthening testability requirements for yze.4.1 (editor flow) and yze.4.3 (launcher framework). The pattern is: "implementation task requires test seam; test task exercises it."

title: Define core domain and gateway interfaces
description:
## Description
Define the core code contracts that the rest of Beads Workbench will build on. This task creates source-specific domain models, query/mutation inputs, normalized gateway errors, and the `BeadsGateway` interface without leaking SQL-era assumptions into the new architecture.

## Instructions
1. Create domain models for issue summaries, issue details, blocked issue views, search results, and any supporting metadata required by the gateway.
2. Define query input models for list, ready, blocked, show, and search flows.
3. Define mutation input models for create, update, close, and comment flows.
4. Define normalized error types suitable for future TUI presentation.
5. Define a source-specific `BeadsGateway` interface under `internal/gateway/beads/`.
6. Include catalog/query methods needed by the UI and write flows, including status, type, and label catalogs.
7. Keep the interface bound to a single beads source/project. Do not design federation into the interface itself.
8. Do not introduce `database/sql`, BQL contracts, or orchestration dependencies.

## Files to Modify
- `internal/domain/` (create models)
- `internal/gateway/beads/` (create interface and error contracts)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Source-specific issue summary/detail/search models exist
- [ ] Query models exist for list, ready, blocked, show, and search flows
- [ ] Mutation models exist for create, update, close, and comment flows
- [ ] Normalized gateway error types exist for UI use
- [ ] `BeadsGateway` interface exists and is source-specific
- [ ] The interface includes catalog methods for status, type, and labels needed by the UI
- [ ] No SQL-specific, BQL-specific, or orchestration-specific types leak into the contracts
close_reason: Implemented domain models and source-specific gateway contracts

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## Gateway interface is missing StatusCatalog, TypeCatalog, and LabelCatalog methods

**What**: The architecture doc (ARCHITECTURE.md lines 101-103) specifies `StatusCatalog` and `TypeCatalog` on the `BeadsGateway` interface. The task description for omd.2 says to define the gateway interface, but its scope lists only list/ready/blocked/show for reads and create/update/close/comment for writes. It does not mention catalog queries. The bd CLI confirms `bd statuses --json`, `bd types --json`, and `bd label list-all` all exist and return structured data. These are needed by the search UI (yze.3.3 requires structured filters for status, type, priority, assignee, labels) and by the create/update actions (yze.4.2 needs to populate dropdowns).

**Why**: If the gateway interface is defined without catalog methods, the search and create/update tasks will either (a) shell out to bd directly from the UI, violating the core architectural rule, or (b) require retroactive gateway changes that re-open closed Phase 1 work. This is a gap between the architecture spec and the task instructions.

**Suggested action**: Add explicit scope items to this task: define `StatusCatalog`, `TypeCatalog`, and `LabelCatalog` (or equivalent) query types and methods on the `BeadsGateway` interface. Also add corresponding implementation items to omd.4 (read operations) to implement these against `bd statuses --json`, `bd types --json`, and `bd label list-all --json`.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [MEDIUM] StatusCatalog and TypeCatalog methods missing from gateway interface scope

**What**: The ARCHITECTURE.md defines the `BeadsGateway` interface shape with `StatusCatalog(ctx) ([]StatusOption, error)` and `TypeCatalog(ctx) ([]TypeOption, error)` (lines 101-102). These are needed for create/update UI controls (dropdowns for status, type selection). This task's instructions list query/mutation models but do not mention catalog models or catalog methods.

**Why**: If the gateway interface is defined without catalog methods, the create/update UI tasks (beads-workbench-yze.4.2) will either (a) hardcode status/type values, which breaks across projects, or (b) add catalog methods later as a bolt-on, creating interface churn after the gateway is "accepted." The architecture explicitly calls these out.

**Suggested action**: Add to instructions: "Define catalog query methods for status and type options as part of the gateway interface, per ARCHITECTURE.md lines 101-102." Add corresponding acceptance criterion: "Catalog methods for status and type options exist on the gateway interface."

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [PASS] Catalog methods are now addressed in both interface and read operations

**What**: The first review flagged missing StatusCatalog/TypeCatalog/LabelCatalog methods. The updated omd.2 now includes instruction #6: "Include catalog/query methods needed by the UI and write flows, including status, type, and label catalogs" and acceptance criterion #6: "The interface includes catalog methods for status, type, and labels needed by the UI." omd.4 also includes instruction #2: "Implement catalog reads needed by the UI and write flows, including status, type, and label catalogs."

**Why**: This closes the cross-epic dependency gap where search (yze.3.3) and create/update (yze.4.2) would have needed catalog data that didn't exist in the gateway.

**Suggested action**: None. This gap is closed.

title: Bootstrap Beads Workbench repository foundation
description:
## Description
Set up the new standalone repository so implementation proceeds in `beads-workbench` instead of continuing inside the donor `perles` repo. This creates the minimal executable Go foundation and package skeleton required for all later work.

## Instructions
1. Before creating code, review the donor/reference guidance in `project-plan/EXECUTION-PLAN.md` and `project-plan/IMPLEMENTATION.md` so the new repo uses `/home/hans/dev/github/perles` only as a selective donor, not as an architecture template.
2. Create `go.mod` with module path `github.com/hk9890/beads-workbench`.
3. Create a minimal binary entrypoint under `cmd/bwb/` that builds successfully.
4. Create the initial package layout described by `project-plan/IMPLEMENTATION.md`:
   - `internal/app/`
   - `internal/domain/`
   - `internal/gateway/beads/`
   - `internal/launcher/`
   - `internal/dashboard/`
   - `internal/mode/`
   - `internal/ui/`
5. Ensure the copied planning docs remain in `project-plan/` and are referenced from the repo docs where useful.
6. Update `docs/CODING.md` so the initial package-layout section reflects the actual bootstrapped layout rather than a future/planned state.
7. Add any minimal README or provenance scaffolding needed for the standalone repo identity.
8. Do not copy SQL, BQL, or orchestration code into the new active path.
