Treat `/home/hans/dev/github/perles/internal/orchestration/`, `/home/hans/dev/github/perles/internal/bql/`, `/home/hans/dev/github/perles/internal/beads/infrastructure/`, and `/home/hans/dev/github/perles/internal/app/app.go` as forbidden starting points.

## Files to Modify
- `go.mod` (create)
- `cmd/bwb/` (create)
- `internal/app/` (create)
- `internal/domain/` (create)
- `internal/gateway/beads/` (create)
- `internal/launcher/` (create)
- `internal/dashboard/` (create)
- `internal/mode/` (create)
- `internal/ui/` (create)
- `docs/CODING.md` (update)
- `README.md` (create or update, if needed)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Donor/reference guidance in `project-plan/` has been reviewed before bootstrapping
- [ ] `go.mod` exists with module path `github.com/hk9890/beads-workbench`
- [ ] `go build ./cmd/bwb` succeeds
- [ ] `cmd/bwb/` exists with a minimal entrypoint
- [ ] The planned package layout exists in `internal/`
- [ ] `docs/CODING.md` reflects the actual bootstrapped package layout
- [ ] `project-plan/` remains present in the repo
- [ ] No SQL, BQL, or orchestration package is added to the active foundation path
close_reason: Implemented repository bootstrap foundation

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## docs/CODING.md will be immediately stale after bootstrap

**What**: `docs/CODING.md` line 20 says "Planned layout — directories will be created during implementation" and lists the intended package structure. This task creates the actual directories. After this task closes, the doc will still say "planned" when the layout is real. Meanwhile, `CLAUDE.md` lines 53-69 contain placeholder sections ("_Add your build and test commands here_", "_Add a brief overview of your project architecture_") that should be populated during bootstrap.

**Why**: `AGENTS.md` line 9 sends every future agent to `docs/CODING.md` as the primary coding reference. If the doc says "planned" when the layout exists, or if `CLAUDE.md` says "add your commands here" when `go build ./cmd/bwb` is the correct command, the first session after bootstrap starts with wrong context. This is the cheapest moment to fix both — the person doing bootstrap knows exactly what landed.

**Suggested action**: Add two acceptance criteria:
- `docs/CODING.md` "Planned layout" section is updated to reflect the actual created layout (remove "Planned layout — directories will be created during implementation" language)
- `CLAUDE.md` placeholder sections are populated with real build/test commands, architecture overview, and coding conventions that match what was bootstrapped

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [LOW] Bootstrap task has strong forbidden-path list — positive finding

**What**: The task explicitly lists four forbidden donor starting points by absolute path: orchestration, bql, beads/infrastructure, and app/app.go. The acceptance criteria include "No SQL, BQL, or orchestration package is added to the active foundation path."

**Why**: This is a well-constructed guardrail. Naming the specific forbidden paths removes ambiguity for the implementing agent.

**Suggested action**: None. This is a strong protection.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [MINOR] docs/CODING.md update is not in omd.1 acceptance criteria — still relies on omd.7 backstop only

**What**: The prior review comment on omd.1 specifically recommended adding acceptance criteria requiring docs/CODING.md to be updated from "planned layout" to actual layout during the bootstrap task. The updated omd.1 acceptance criteria still do not include a docs update requirement. The docs gate only appears at omd.7 (acceptance review).

**Why**: This is a minor gap, not a blocker, because omd.7 now catches it. But the optimal time to update "Planned layout — directories will be created during implementation" is during omd.1 when the person doing the bootstrap knows exactly what was created. Deferring to omd.7 means either the acceptance reviewer does it (wrong — reviewers should verify, not implement) or the reviewer creates a follow-up task for a 30-second docs fix that should have been done inline.

**Suggested action**: Add to omd.1 acceptance criteria: "docs/CODING.md 'Planned layout' language is updated to reflect actually-created directories." This is low-severity because omd.7 is the backstop, but it would be cleaner to fix it at the source.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [MINOR] No implementation task explicitly updates CODING.md package layout — only acceptance reviews verify it

**What**: Multiple acceptance reviews verify that docs/CODING.md reflects the current architecture. But looking at the implementation tasks themselves, only omd.8 and yze.2.6 explicitly list "docs/CODING.md (update)" in their Files to Modify. The tasks that actually create new packages — omd.1 (bootstrap), omd.2 (domain/gateway interfaces), yze.2.1 (app model), yze.3.x (mode controllers) — do not include docs/CODING.md in their scope.

**Why**: This means the actual CODING.md update work falls to acceptance reviewers, who are supposed to verify, not implement. The reviewer at omd.7 will find CODING.md says "planned layout" and have to either update it themselves or create a follow-up task. This isn't catastrophic because the acceptance reviews catch it, but it creates unnecessary rework cycles.

**Suggested action**: This is a low-severity pattern issue. The acceptance review backstop works. But for clean execution, consider adding "Update docs/CODING.md package layout section to reflect created packages" to omd.1's scope. This is the single most impactful place to fix it since omd.1 creates the entire initial package tree.

title: Bootstrap Beads Workbench foundation and official beads gateway
description:
## Description
Create the new repository foundation for Beads Workbench and implement the first production-ready backend seam that talks only to official beads surfaces. This epic establishes the standalone codebase, the source-specific Beads Gateway boundary, a CLI-backed gateway implementation, and the test coverage needed before UI work begins.

## Goals
- Bootstrap the standalone Go module and binary entrypoint for `bwb`
- Establish the source-specific `BeadsGateway` seam for all issue reads and writes
- Implement a shared `bd` command runner and JSON decoding layer
- Implement core read and write gateway operations through official `bd` commands only
- Add focused automated tests for command construction, decoding, and failure handling

## Success Criteria
- [ ] The repository has the baseline module, binary entrypoint, and copied design docs
- [ ] A source-specific `BeadsGateway` interface exists in the new repo
- [ ] A CLI-backed gateway implementation performs core reads and writes through `bd`
- [ ] Gateway errors are normalized into UI-usable categories
- [ ] Tests cover command construction, output decoding, and failure behavior
- [ ] No direct SQL, BQL, or orchestration dependency exists in the active foundation path
- [ ] All child implementation tasks are closed
- [ ] Acceptance review task is closed

## Source Documents
- `project-plan/PRODUCT.md`
- `project-plan/ARCHITECTURE.md`
- `project-plan/IMPLEMENTATION.md`
- `project-plan/EXECUTION-PLAN.md`
close_reason: Foundation epic accepted and all child work completed

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Plan coverage audit: current beads plan covers only Phase 1 (repository bootstrap + BeadsGateway foundation). It does NOT yet cover the full v1 product described in project-plan/. Missing planned follow-up epics include: (1) minimal Bubble Tea app shell, (2) browsing experience with built-in dashboards/search/details, (3) editor and launcher flows, and (4) broader UI/integration acceptance coverage beyond gateway tests. Full product execution should not be considered fully planned until those follow-up epics/tasks are created.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## STRENGTH: Gateway seam architecture is inherently testable

**What**: The plan's core architectural decision — a `BeadsGateway` interface as the single data boundary between UI and beads — is excellent for testability. Every mode controller and every UI component can be tested against a fake gateway without needing `bd` or a beads repo. The command runner test seams (omd.3) add another clean boundary below the gateway.

**Why**: This two-layer testability boundary (fake gateway for UI tests, fake command runner for gateway tests) means the plan already has the right seams in the architecture. The problem is not architectural — it's that the test tasks don't leverage these seams explicitly. The architecture enables agent-verifiable testing; the test tasks just need to specify it.

**Suggested action**: No architectural change needed. This is the plan's strongest testability feature. The fix is to ensure all test tasks explicitly reference and depend on these seams.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## STRENGTH: Architecture docs clearly separate renderer from provider, gateway from UI — all testable seams

**What**: The architecture doc and plan consistently separate: dashboard renderer from dashboard definition provider, gateway interface from CLI implementation, launcher interface from subprocess execution, editor interface from editor binary. These are all clean testable seams.

**Why**: Each separation point is a testing boundary. You can test the dashboard renderer with fake definitions. You can test the CLI adapter with a fake command runner. You can test the launcher framework with a command recorder. You can test the editor flow with a fake editor opener. The architecture was designed (intentionally or not) to support dependency injection at every major boundary.

**Suggested action**: The plan should explicitly call out these seams as the testing strategy in a cross-cutting "testing approach" section or task. Right now the architecture enables testability but the test tasks don't leverage it explicitly.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## [LOW] Gateway epic dependency chain is correctly strict — positive finding

**What**: The dependency chain within the gateway epic is well-structured: bootstrap → interfaces → runner → reads/writes (parallel) → tests → acceptance review. Each task blocks the next appropriately, and the acceptance review depends on all implementation tasks.

**Why**: This prevents an implementer from jumping ahead to read/write operations before the interface and runner foundations exist. The parallel structure of reads and writes after the runner is also correct — they share the runner but don't depend on each other.

**Suggested action**: None. This is a strong structural protection.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
## PASS: Phase 1 internal dependency chain is correctly strict and properly sequenced

**What**: Within the gateway epic, the dependency chain is: omd.1 → omd.2 → omd.3 → [omd.4 ‖ omd.5] → omd.6 → omd.7. Each step correctly requires the prior step's deliverables: bootstrap before interfaces, interfaces before runner, runner before read/write ops (parallel), ops before tests, tests before acceptance. The omd.8 (guardrails) → omd.6 dependency is also correct — guardrails should exist before the test verification pass runs.

**Why**: This is the backbone of the plan. An agent cannot skip to gateway implementation without the interface, cannot test without the implementation, cannot accept without tests. The parallel branch for reads and writes after the runner is good — they share the runner but don't depend on each other.

**Suggested action**: No change needed. This is a strong structural protection. The only gap is omd.8's missing dependency on omd.1 (noted separately).

title: dependency-only detail view shows duplicate Structure section
description:
Discovered during verification of beads-workbench-2l4 in the built binary against the embedded fixture repo. Expected: when an issue has blocked/blocks/related refs but no parent-group structure, the Dependencies pane shows Blocked by / Blocks / Related rows only, with selection moving across those rows. Actual: after browser-item population falls back to dependency refs, the detail renderer also shows a Structure section containing the same refs even though no parent-group context exists.

## Root Cause
`dependencyGroups()` appends a Structure section unconditionally from `browserItems`. After the model populates `browserItems` from dependency fallback rather than parent-group context, those same refs appear twice.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] When BrowserItems are populated from dependency refs because no parent-group exists, the Structure section does not appear in Dependencies.
- [ ] When real parent-group structure exists, the Structure section still appears as intended.
- [ ] Real-app review confirms dependency-only detail view no longer duplicates dependency rows.
close_reason: Fixed dependency fallback rendering to suppress Structure group unless parent-group context exists; added regression tests for fallback and parent-group paths; focused detail/app tests pass.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Root cause confirmed at details.go:270-271. What: dependencyGroups() unconditionally appends a Structure section from browserItems. After 2l4 populated browserItems from BlockedBy+Blocks+Related refs when no parent-group exists, these same refs now appear in both their typed sections (Blocked by/Blocks/Related) AND the Structure section. Why: Structure is meant for parent-group browsing context (parent + siblings), not a flat dump of dependency refs. When browserItems come from dependencies, Structure just duplicates them. Suggested action: suppress the Structure group when browserItems were populated from dependencies rather than from a parent-group. The simplest approach: add a flag (e.g., BrowserFromParentGroup bool) to the detail state, and only render Structure when that flag is true.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Missing acceptance criterion. What: no criterion says Structure must not render when browserItems come from dependency fallback. Why: a tasker could suppress duplicate rows in some other way (deduplication) rather than fixing the actual semantic problem — Structure has no meaning without parent-group context. Suggested action: add criterion: 'When BrowserItems are populated from dependency refs (no parent-group), the Structure section must not appear in the Dependencies pane.'

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Ordering relative to 74r/6eq. What: yxjx touches dependencyGroups() in details.go which affects rendered row count in the Dependencies pane. 74r changes pane width. Why: fixing yxjx removes the entire Structure section for dependency-only issues, reducing pane content. Width tuning in 74r should account for the final content, not the inflated duplicate. Suggested action: fix yxjx before 74r. yxjx and 6sk are independent and can run in parallel.

title: Related links render under Blocked by/Blocks instead of Related
description:
Discovered during verification of beads-workbench-59z while augmenting the embedded fixture with a `bd dep relate` link. Expected: a relates-to link created with `bd dep relate <id1> <id2>` appears in detail-mode under the Related group. Actual: `bd show --json` returns the link with dependency_type `relates-to`, but the gateway/detail pipeline only treats dependency_type `related` as related work, so the app renders the linked issue under Blocked by/Blocks and leaves Related empty.

## Root Cause
Gateway normalization only recognizes `related` and misses `relates-to`, even though `bd dep relate` emits `relates-to`.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Gateway normalization classifies both `related` and `relates-to` dependency types as related work.
- [ ] Unit tests cover both dependency type strings.
- [ ] Real-app detail view shows `bd dep relate` links under Related rather than Blocked by/Blocks.
close_reason: Fixed dependency-type normalization for related/relates-to and added unit coverage for both variants in gateway detail mapping.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review: Root cause confirmed — single-line fix. What: read_gateway.go:543 checks dependencyType == "related" but bd dep relate produces dependency_type "relates-to". The condition needs to also match "relates-to". Why: this is a data contract mismatch between bd CLI output and gateway parsing. Every relates-to link falls through to blockedBy (line 557). Suggested action: change line 543 to 'if dependencyType == "related" || dependencyType == "relates-to"'. Add a test case with dependency_type "relates-to" in read_gateway_test.go.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review: Bug is well-scoped and actionable. One minor wording improvement. What: the repro steps say 'run bd create --id bwf-6 ... and bd dep relate bwf-2 bwf-6' but the acceptance criteria don't explicitly state the gateway must handle both 'related' and 'relates-to' dependency types. Why: a tasker might fix only 'relates-to' and break the existing 'related' path, or vice versa. Suggested action: add acceptance criterion: 'dependencyReferencesFromPayload classifies both dependency_type "related" and "relates-to" as related work, verified by unit tests for each.'

title: Content pane scroll continues or feels wrong after repeated down-key scrolling
description:
Discovered from user review after epic beads-workbench-90w was closed. Expected: content scrolling in detail mode should move predictably with each key press and stop immediately when key input stops. Actual: user reports that when content exceeds the page and they press Down, scrolling feels slow and appears to continue after they stop pressing.

## Investigation First
Code inspection suggests detail scrolling is currently discrete integer-offset scrolling with no animation, so this may be terminal key-repeat event queuing rather than a renderer bug. Verify that hypothesis before changing code.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Reproduce with controlled single-tap vs key-hold behavior and determine whether extra motion is caused by queued repeat key events, render lag, or a detail-mode state bug.
- [ ] If single discrete taps already behave correctly, capture the finding and decide whether this should be documented or handled differently.
- [ ] If a real detail-mode bug exists, file or convert this issue into a concrete implementation fix with exact reproduction and expected behavior.
close_reason: Verified: single-tap scroll is correct; apparent continued motion comes from queued repeated Down key events, not a detail-mode bug

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review — scroll architecture analysis: The scroll implementation is purely integer-offset based (applyScrollAction at model.go:370-386) with move=1 per key press. There is NO animation, momentum, or async scroll. The 'continues after key stops' symptom is almost certainly terminal key-repeat buffering: held keys generate a burst of KeyMsg events that queue in Bubble Tea's event loop, and the UI processes them after the key is released. This is not a bug in this codebase — it's standard terminal key-repeat behavior. What: the reported symptom is likely terminal key-repeat event queuing, not a scroll state bug. The 'slow' feel is because move=1 scrolls one line per keypress, which at terminal repeat rates feels sluggish for long content. Why: if the real problem is key-repeat perception, the fix is either (a) debouncing scroll events or (b) increasing scroll step size, NOT fixing scroll state logic. Suggested action: (1) verify by testing with a single discrete key tap — if one tap = one line and it stops, it's key-repeat. (2) If confirmed, downgrade to P2 and consider increasing scroll-down step to 2-3 lines, or adding j/k acceleration on held keys. Do NOT redesign the scroll system for a non-bug.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Set review — investigation-first framing is correct, likely a non-bug. What: the scroll code (applyScrollAction, move=1) is purely discrete with no animation or momentum. The prior reviewer's analysis is accurate — this is almost certainly terminal key-repeat buffering. The acceptance criteria correctly require reproduction before any code change. Why: this should not block any other work in the set. If investigation confirms key-repeat, the only reasonable action is either (a) close as wontfix/not-a-bug, or (b) optionally increase scroll step to 2-3 lines for faster traversal. Suggested action: deprioritize to last in execution order. Have the implementer do a single-tap test; if single tap = exactly one line of scroll, close this issue with a finding comment. Do not redesign scroll.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Verifier: reproduced with embedded fixture + PTY capture. Single Down produced one discrete content-step; rapid repeated Down produced matching discrete queued steps that rendered shortly after the last key. No async momentum or detail-mode state bug found. Artifact: /tmp/bwb-verify-pxs-t8fTvk

title: Metadata pane should support up/down field selection and enter-based editing beyond status
description:
Discovered from user review after epic beads-workbench-90w was closed. Expected: the Metadata pane should support actionable field navigation with up/down and Enter for a constrained set of editable fields in detail mode. Actual: current behavior only meaningfully supports status and does not expose a true field-by-field navigation/editing model. This is follow-up product work rather than a regression bug.

## Scope Constraint
Limit this follow-up to a small v1 metadata interaction surface:
- editable fields: `Status` and `Priority` only
- non-editable informational rows remain read-only

## Interaction Model
- Up/down moves selection between editable metadata fields only.
- Enter on `Status` opens the existing status dialog.
- Enter on `Priority` cycles through priorities in place (`P0 -> P1 -> P2 -> P3 -> P4 -> P0`).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Metadata field navigation is explicitly limited to Status and Priority for this follow-up.
- [ ] Up/down navigates between Status and Priority only.
- [ ] Enter on Status triggers the existing status edit flow.
- [ ] Enter on Priority cycles priority through P0..P4 and wraps back to P0.
- [ ] Non-editable informational rows remain readable but are not treated as actionable fields.
close_reason: Implemented v1 metadata interaction model limited to Status/Priority; added navigation/edit behavior and focused tests.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review: This is a feature request, not a bug — current behavior is as designed (status-only editing). Reclassify as type:task or type:enhancement. Hidden complexity: adding field selection requires (1) defining which metadata fields are editable vs read-only, (2) an edit interaction model per field type (priority is numeric, owner is text/selector, labels are multi-value), (3) rendering selected-field highlighting in metadata pane. What: the description says 'up/down field selection and Enter-based editing' but doesn't specify which fields or what the edit UX looks like for non-status fields. Why: without scoping which fields are editable and how, this will balloon into an open-ended design task. Suggested action: constrain to status + priority only as v1, with explicit edit interaction for each (status=dialog, priority=cycle or dialog). Defer labels/owner editing.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Set review — scope constraint is good but edit UX still undefined. What: the description says 'enter triggers the correct edit action' and scope constraint says 'status and priority only', but it doesn't specify what the edit interaction IS for priority. Status already has a dialog — what does priority editing look like? Cycle through P0-P5? A dialog? Inline text entry? Why: without specifying the interaction model, this will stall during implementation. The field navigation part (up/down between status and priority) is trivial; the edit UX is where the real work lives. Suggested action: specify 'priority edit = cycle P0→P1→P2→P3→P4→P5→P0 on Enter' or similar concrete interaction. Also: the existing metadataField struct already has an editable bool — add editable:true to Priority in metadataGroups and add MetadataFieldPriority constant. This is the only new feature in the set; defer it to last.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Verifier rerun: PASS. Focused tests passed; live embedded-fixture run confirmed metadata selection only toggles Status/Priority, Enter on Status opens Update Status modal, and Enter on Priority updates bwf-2 from P0 to P1. Follow-up bug filed for capture harness timeout on open modal: beads-workbench-nh66.

title: Add shell focus and refresh tick orchestration
description:
## Description
Enable shell-owned focus awareness and periodic refresh triggers because `internal/app` owns cross-mode reload decisions.

## Instructions
1. Update `cmd/bwb/main.go` to enable Bubble Tea focus reporting for the main program.
2. Add app-level message/state handling in `internal/app/model.go` for focus gain/loss and periodic auto-refresh ticks.
3. Route focus/tick events through a shell-owned refresh-active-surface path that can target detail, board, or search.
4. Skip auto-refresh when help/action modals are open or the target view is already loading.
5. Define the refresh cadence as a named constant that defaults to about 60 seconds, and make focus-triggered refresh degrade safely when the terminal does not report focus events.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Program startup enables focus reporting
- [ ] Shell handles focus regain and blur without crashing or spamming reloads
- [ ] Periodic tick can request refresh of only the active surface
- [ ] Duplicate reload commands are avoided while the target surface is already loading
- [ ] Tick interval is defined as a named constant and defaults to about 60 seconds
- [ ] If focus events are unavailable in the terminal, the app still behaves correctly and scheduled refresh remains the fallback freshness mechanism

## Files to Modify
- `cmd/bwb/main.go`
- `internal/app/model.go`
- `internal/app/model_test.go`

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Missing acceptance criterion: configurable or constant-defined tick interval. What: instructions say 'periodic auto-refresh ticks' but no AC specifies the ~60s target or where this constant lives. Why: without this, the implementer may hardcode a magic number or pick an arbitrary interval with no test coverage of the cadence. Suggested action: add AC — 'Tick interval is defined as a named constant (not a magic number) and defaults to ~60s.'

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Good: instruction 4 explicitly handles skip-while-loading and modal-open suppression. This prevents the most common auto-refresh footgun.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Confirmed: current main.go creates the program with only tea.WithAltScreen(). Adding tea.WithFocusReporting() is the correct enablement path in Bubble Tea. No conflict with existing options.

title: Verify redesigned detail screen with updated tests and real-app runtime review
description:
## Description
Update automated verification for the redesigned detail screen and run built-binary runtime verification against the embedded fixture so the final screen is checked in the real app, not only through unit/golden tests.

## Instructions
1. Update and/or add detail renderer tests and goldens to cover the new layout structure:
   - explicit height-aware rendering
   - full-height Dependencies box
   - dominant center Content pane
   - comments integrated into the center pane as a smaller lower subsection
   - right metadata rail with vertical divider and Quick actions
   - shared compact dependency row rendering
   - markdown-rendered details and comments.
2. Run the relevant automated test packages for the touched detail/app UI surfaces.
3. Build the real binary and verify the detail screen against the embedded fixture repo using the documented runtime verification workflow. The verification must include opening a seeded issue detail screen and checking that:
   - the screen uses the available height well
   - the center content area is dominant
   - comments are visually smaller and do not consume disproportionate vertical space
   - the implemented pane behavior matches the accepted 3-pane interaction model.
4. If runtime review reveals polish gaps, file follow-up beads work instead of silently ignoring them.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Updated automated tests/goldens cover the redesigned dense full-height detail layout.
- [ ] Relevant test commands pass.
- [ ] Built-binary runtime verification is performed against the embedded fixture repo.
- [ ] The real detail screen is manually/visually checked after opening a seeded issue.
- [ ] Runtime review explicitly confirms strong vertical space usage.
- [ ] Runtime review confirms comments remain visually secondary within the center pane.
- [ ] Any newly discovered polish defects are captured as follow-up beads issues.
close_reason: Verified: all criteria met

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Verifier rerun: BLOCKED. Focused tests pass (internal/ui/details, internal/mode/details, internal/app, internal/ui/search) and built-binary fixture review confirms the 3-pane model, but runtime captures at 120x34/140x34/160x34 show the Content pane still leaves a large lower blank region so vertical space is not used strongly and comments are not anchored as a lower subsection. Follow-up: beads-workbench-b14. Next: rebalance content-pane vertical allocation, then rerun runtime capture.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Verifier rerun: PASS. Focused detail/app tests pass, and built-binary fixture captures at 120x34/140x34/160x34 show the Content pane now uses spare height well: comments anchor as a lower secondary subsection, center content remains dominant, and the accepted 3-pane model still holds. Artifacts: /tmp/bwb-verify-90w2-rerun-XWcbyH and /tmp/bwb-verify-markdown-hUIRhb/detail-120x34-markdown-comment-hotkey3.json

title: Acceptance Review: Redesign issue detail screen layout and styling
description:
## Description
Run final acceptance review for the redesigned issue detail screen epic. Confirm the implementation matches the approved layout direction, the polished rendering goals, and the required runtime verification depth before closing the epic.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Implementation task is closed.
- [ ] Verification task is closed.
- [ ] Dependencies use the shared dashboard-style border/row visual language.
- [ ] Comments use markdown-aware styling and look polished in the real app.
- [ ] Right metadata rail is grouped, separated by a vertical divider, and includes Quick actions.
- [ ] A real built-binary detail screen was opened and visually checked against the fixture repo.
- [ ] Any follow-up polish gaps discovered during review are tracked as new beads issues.
close_reason: All criteria verified

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Acceptance review: PASS. Tasks .1 and .2 are closed; focused detail/app tests pass; built-binary fixture captures at 120x34/140x34/160x34 plus markdown-comment runtime review confirm shared dependency styling, markdown-aware polished comments, grouped metadata rail with vertical separator, and no new polish gaps. Artifacts: /tmp/bwb-verify-90w2-rerun-XWcbyH and /tmp/bwb-verify-markdown-hUIRhb/detail-120x34-markdown-comment-hotkey3.json

title: Implement redesigned issue detail layout and shared dependency/comment rendering
description:
## Description
Implement the redesigned issue detail layout described in the parent epic. Rework the main detail renderer so the screen uses a boxed Dependencies pane on the left, a dominant boxed center Content pane, and a grouped metadata/actions rail on the right separated by a vertical divider.

## Instructions
1. Add explicit height handling to the detail renderer state/path so vertical allocation is implementable and testable.
2. Refactor `internal/ui/details/details.go` layout logic so the primary detail view renders:
   - left: a `Dependencies` section box that expands to use the available column height
   - center: one `Content` pane using most of the center height, with issue details/body first and a smaller comments subsection rendered near the bottom of that pane
   - right: grouped metadata plus quick actions with no nested boxes, separated from the center column by a visible vertical rule.
3. Reuse the dashboard section border treatment for the left and center boxes by using the same `styles.FormSection` rounded-border component rather than inventing a new box style.
4. Render dependency issue rows with the same compact visual language as dashboard issue rows. Reuse shared issue-row rendering where practical (`internal/ui/shared/issuerow`) so type/priority/status/id styling stays aligned with the dashboard.
5. Ensure the same structured issue relationship rendering approach is usable for other issue-detail side-panel / structure-data surfaces instead of creating a one-off dependency-only renderer.
6. Replace the current plain-text comment-body rendering with markdown-aware rendering using a reusable shared markdown render component, and use that same component for both the main issue details/body text and comment text.
7. Keep the top summary compact and readable; avoid duplicate standalone ID lines competing with the title.
8. Make the layout actively consume available vertical space instead of producing a sparse upper-half-only composition.
9. Preserve the accepted interaction model: 3 focus panes (`Dependencies`, `Content`, `Metadata`), center-pane scrolling covers both issue details and comments, dependency-row direct interaction is out of scope unless essentially free.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Detail renderer accepts explicit height input.
- [ ] Dependencies render in a bordered section that visually matches dashboard section borders.
- [ ] Dependency references use shared compact issue-row styling rather than raw `id · title` strings.
- [ ] The same relationship-rendering component/path can support dependency/structure panels elsewhere in detail mode.
- [ ] The center Content pane occupies most of the center-column height and includes comments as a smaller lower subsection.
- [ ] Comments and issue details both use the same reusable markdown-render component/path.
- [ ] Right-side metadata is rendered as grouped text with a vertical separator and includes Quick actions.
- [ ] Top-of-detail summary no longer repeats the issue ID as a separate low-value line.
- [ ] The final layout looks dense and intentional rather than vertically under-filled.
- [ ] The implemented interactions match the explicit 3-pane focus/overflow rules from the epic.

## Files to Modify
- `internal/ui/details/details.go`
- `internal/ui/details/metadata.go`
- `internal/ui/shared/issuerow/row.go` (only if shared rendering helpers need a small extension)
- `internal/ui/shared/markdown/*` (or equivalent shared markdown component surface)
- `internal/app/*` and/or detail-mode state wiring if height/focus plumbing requires it
- any directly-related detail renderer support files/tests required by the redesign
close_reason: Implemented 3-pane dense detail layout with explicit height/scroll plumbing, section/issuerow/markdown reuse, and updated focused tests+goldens.

title: Redesign issue detail screen layout and styling
description:
## Description
Redesign the issue detail screen so it reads as a structured operator workspace instead of a flat metadata dump. The new layout should keep Dependencies on the left in a boxed section, place the main issue details in the center, and use a simple grouped metadata/action rail on the right separated by a vertical rule.

## Decisions Captured From Discussion
- Left side stays dedicated to dependency information.
- Rename the left section to `Dependencies`.
- The dependencies section must use the same bordered section treatment as the dashboard (`styles.FormSection` / same rounded border styling).
- The border/boxed surface should be implemented as one reusable component approach rather than ad-hoc one-off box rendering.
- Dependency issues inside that section should render with the same compact issue-row language used in the dashboard, and should reuse the dashboard/shared issue rendering component where possible.
- The same dependency rendering approach should also be used anywhere else in issue detail that shows structured issue relationship data / side-panel issue structure data.
- Center column is a single content component/pane for this pass.
- Within that center pane, issue details/body take the dominant vertical share and comments are rendered as a smaller lower subsection; comments are NOT a separate focus pane in this pass.
- Comments should render with markdown-aware coloring/styling via a reusable markdown-render component.
- The same markdown-render component should be used for both issue details/body text and comment text.
- Right side should not use separate boxes; it should be a grouped metadata rail separated by a vertical line.
- Add a `Quick actions` group in the right rail.
- The layout must use the full available screen height instead of leaving large dead areas.
- Dependencies should use effectively the full available vertical span on the left side.
- The main issue details area should use effectively the full available vertical span in the center.
- Comments should be visually bounded to a smaller portion of vertical space (roughly ~20% of the usable height, not half the screen).
- Height must be an explicit renderer input so vertical allocation is implementable and testable.
- UX/interaction details must be explicit enough that the reviewer and implementer do not need to guess navigation or focus behavior.
- After implementation, manually open the real app and inspect the detail screen to ensure it actually looks good and uses space well.

## Interaction / Overflow Rules
- Detail mode remains a 3-pane model for this pass: `Dependencies` | `Content` | `Metadata`.
- Comments belong to the `Content` pane and do not receive separate pane focus.
- Content-pane scrolling covers the full center pane as one scrollable region (issue details first, comments below).
- Dependencies pane scrolls independently if dependency content exceeds visible height.
- Metadata rail scrolls independently only if needed; otherwise it remains statically visible.
- Dependency row rendering is visual in this pass; direct dependency-row navigation/open behavior is out of scope unless it falls out naturally with minimal extra work.
- Responsive fallbacks must remain usable: narrower layouts may collapse to simpler arrangements, but the wide/detail target layout should honor the dense full-height design.

## Updated Dense Layout Direction
```text
Beads Workbench                                                          Board   Search   Detail

Blocked bug for fixture
BUG P0 blocked bwf-2   @bob   #blocking #fixture

╭─ Dependencies ─────────────────────────────╮  ╭─ Content ──────────────────────────────────────────╮  │ Workflow
│ Blocked by                                 │  │ Used to validate blocked/ready and dependency     │  │ Status     blocked
│   BUG P0 blk bwf-1 Seed fixture root task  │  │ reads.                                            │  │ Type       bug
│                                            │  │                                                    │  │ Priority   P0
│ Blocks                                     │  │ Additional body/details/notes continue here and   │  │
│   (none)                                   │  │ consume most of the center height.                │  │ People
│                                            │  │                                                    │  │ Assignee   bob
│ Related                                    │  │                                                    │  │ Creator    hans.k…
│   (none)                                   │  │                                                    │  │
│                                            │  │                                                    │  │ Labels
│                                            │  │                                                    │  │ • blocking
│                                            │  │                                                    │  │ • fixture
│                                            │  │──────────────── Comments (2) ──────────────────── │  │
│                                            │  │ Hans Kohlreiter · 2026-04-18 16:13               │  │ Time
│                                            │  │   Needs dependency resolution                    │  │ Created    2026-04-18 16:13
│                                            │  │ Hans Kohlreiter · 2026-04-18 16:13               │  │ Updated    2026-04-18 16:13
│                                            │  │   Second deterministic comment                    │  │
│                                            │  ╰────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯  │ Quick actions
│                                            │                                                          │ [e] Edit
│                                            │                                                          │ [c] Comment
│                                            │                                                          │ [r] Refresh
╰────────────────────────────────────────────╯                                                          │ [q] Back
```

## UX / Interaction Requirements
- Dependencies groups (`Blocked by`, `Blocks`, `Related`) must remain readable and clearly grouped.
- The right rail must communicate available actions clearly enough that operators understand what can be done from detail mode.
- The new composition must not make comments dominate the screen when description/dependency context is the primary working surface.
- Focus, scrolling, and panel emphasis behavior are intentionally defined above and should not be reinvented during implementation.
- If dependency-row interaction is later desired, capture it as follow-up work instead of silently expanding scope here.

## Technical Reuse Requirements
- Reuse the shared bordered section component already used by the dashboard.
- Reuse the shared compact issue renderer used by the dashboard for dependency issue rows where possible.
- Reuse/extend the shared markdown-render component for both issue details and comments.
- Avoid duplicating near-identical rendering logic for issue relationship groups or markdown text blocks when an existing shared component can be extended.
- Prefer extending the current detail layout machinery rather than rewriting it from scratch.

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Detail renderer accepts explicit height so vertical allocation is deterministic and testable.
- [ ] Detail layout uses a left Dependencies box, center Content box, and a right grouped metadata rail separated by a vertical divider.
- [ ] Dependencies rendering reuses the dashboard section border style and the shared compact issue-row visual language.
- [ ] The same structured issue rendering approach is usable for dependency/relationship panels elsewhere in detail mode.
- [ ] A reusable markdown-render component is used for both issue details and comment text.
- [ ] Comments render with markdown-aware styling/colors instead of plain wrapped text.
- [ ] The right rail includes grouped metadata plus a Quick actions group without separate boxes.
- [ ] The layout uses the full available terminal height without leaving large empty dead zones.
- [ ] Comments are visually constrained to a smaller vertical share than the main details and dependencies regions.
- [ ] UX/interaction details are explicit enough for implementation and verification.
- [ ] Automated UI coverage is updated for the new layout.
- [ ] Built-binary runtime verification is performed against the embedded fixture repo, including opening an issue detail screen and visually confirming the layout looks good and uses space well.
close_reason: Acceptance review verified; redesigned detail screen layout and runtime behavior meet epic criteria

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: UX gap: No height parameter in State/Render. What: The current Render function receives State.Width but no Height. The epic requires 'full available terminal height' and '~20% for comments', but the renderer has no way to know the terminal height. Why: Without height, the layout cannot split vertical space between Dependencies, Issue details, and Comments — the core layout promise of this epic is unimplementable. Suggested action: Add Height to details.State and thread it through the column renderers. Task .1 instructions should explicitly call this out as step 0.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: UX gap: No scrolling behavior defined. What: The epic says Dependencies and Issue details use 'full available vertical span' but says nothing about what happens when content overflows the box. Does the description scroll? Do dependencies scroll independently? Is there a viewport per pane? Why: Bubble Tea full-height boxes that overflow need explicit viewport management. Without this, the implementer will either clip content silently or invent scroll behavior that may conflict with the existing FocusPane keyboard model. Suggested action: Add explicit overflow/scroll rules to the epic for each of the three boxed areas (Dependencies, Issue details, Comments). At minimum: 'content that overflows the box is scrollable within its pane when focused.'

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: UX gap: Keyboard navigation between panes is undefined. What: The existing code has FocusPane with Content/Browser/Metadata, but the new layout adds a Comments pane and renames Browser to Dependencies. The epic does not specify how the user moves focus between the four regions (Dependencies, Issue details, Comments, Metadata rail), nor what keys do it. Why: Without this, the implementer will guess the focus model, and the verifier cannot test it. The current 3-pane focus model needs to become a 4-pane model (or Comments shares focus with Content). Suggested action: Explicitly define the focus cycle order and the key(s) that move between panes. Decide whether Comments is a separate focus target or a sub-region of Content.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: UX gap: Dependency row interaction undefined. What: The ASCII mockup shows dependency rows but doesn't say whether they're navigable/selectable. The epic mentions 'if dependency navigation is supported' — this conditional must be resolved before implementation. Why: If yes, the dependency list needs cursor state, selection rendering, and an 'open dependency' action. If no, they're static text and much simpler. This is a binary design decision that changes implementation scope significantly. Suggested action: Decide now: are dependency rows navigable in this epic, or is that follow-up work? If navigable, add acceptance criteria for cursor movement and the action to open a dependency.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Technical reuse: FormSection is ready, but needs Height threading. What: styles.FormSection already supports Height for fixed-height boxes — good, direct reuse works. However, the current detail renderer never passes Height because it doesn't know terminal height (see height comment). Why: The reuse path is sound but blocked on the same Height gap. No new component needed for bordered sections. Suggested action: Task .1 should say 'reuse styles.FormSection with Height parameter' not just 'reuse the dashboard section border treatment.'

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Technical reuse: issuerow.RenderReferenceCompact is the right component for dependency rows. What: The shared/issuerow package already has RenderReferenceCompact that takes domain.IssueReference — exactly the type used by detail.BlockedBy/Blocks/Related. Why: Task .1 says 'reuse shared issue-row rendering where practical' but the current renderReferences() in details.go builds its own 'id · title' strings instead. The path is clear: replace renderReferences with calls to issuerow.RenderReferenceCompact. Suggested action: Task .1 instruction 3 should explicitly say 'replace renderReferences() with issuerow.RenderReferenceCompact calls' rather than the vague 'reuse where practical.'

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Technical reuse: markdown renderer is already shared and used for descriptions. What: The details.go already uses shared/markdown for description rendering (renderMarkdownMultiline calls readOnlyMarkdown.RenderReadOnly). However, renderComments() on line 471 calls renderMultiline() — the plain text path — not renderMarkdownMultiline(). Why: The fix is trivial: change renderComments to call renderMarkdownMultiline instead of renderMultiline. No new component needed. Suggested action: Task .1 should say 'change renderComments to use renderMarkdownMultiline' — this is a one-line change, not a 'introduce or normalize' effort.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Complexity concern: Three-column → three-column refactor is less work than the epic implies. What: The existing renderThreeColumn already does left/content/metadata with the same conceptual layout (browser rail, content, metadata). The redesign is essentially: (1) move dependencies from content into the left rail, (2) add Comments as a sub-section of center, (3) wrap sections in FormSection boxes, (4) use issuerow for deps. Why: The epic reads like a from-scratch redesign, but it's really a restructuring of the existing three-column renderer. Framing it as a rewrite risks the implementer throwing away working layout math. Suggested action: Task .1 should say 'refactor renderThreeColumn' not 'rework the main detail renderer.' Preserve the existing width-splitting logic and column-merging approach.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Simplification: The vertical divider for the right rail is trivial — don't over-specify. What: The epic and mockup show a vertical line separating the metadata rail. This is just a borderVertical character in the column merge loop — the same approach already used by FormSection. Why: Task .1 mentions 'a visible vertical rule' as if it's a distinct rendering concern. It's one character per line in the merge loop. Suggested action: Don't create a separate component or abstraction for the vertical divider. A comment in task .1 saying 'add borderVertical in the merge loop' is sufficient.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Scope concern: Two-column and single-column responsive fallback undefined. What: The existing code has three rendering paths: single-column, two-column, three-column based on width breakpoints. The epic only defines the three-column layout. What happens below InspectorThreeColumnMinWidth? Does the two-column layout also change? Does single-column? Why: If only the wide layout changes, the implementer needs to know that explicitly. If all three change, the scope is 3x what's described. Suggested action: Epic should state whether two-column and single-column layouts are in-scope or explicitly out-of-scope for this work.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Accepted reviewer follow-up: comments remain part of the center/content pane for this pass; implement height-aware dense layout, explicit overflow behavior, and a 3-pane focus model. Dependency rows reuse shared issue rendering visually but direct dependency-row navigation is out of scope for this pass unless trivially free.

title: Minor doc clarity improvements from project-doc review
description:
Batch of MINOR findings from project-doc review that can be addressed together:

1. key-bindings.md: search-mode backspace/ctrl+u entries are not flagged as hardcoded/non-configurable, unlike the modal y/n note on line 83. Add a similar note to the search section or the Notes section for consistency. Evidence: internal/mode/search/model.go handles these directly (lines 99, 341), not via the configurable keybinding system.

2. README.md docs index (lines 19-24) omits RUNTIME_UI_VERIFICATION.md while both AGENTS.md (line 19) and OVERVIEW.md (line 46) reference it. README.md already lists the user-guide, so this is an inconsistency, not a scoping choice. Add a line for it.

3. OVERVIEW.md and CODING.md package maps use 'detail' in descriptive text (e.g., 'board/search/detail feature models') while the actual directory is internal/mode/details (plural). Not a broken path reference but could confuse someone grepping for the package.
close_reason: Verified the README and key-bindings items were already satisfied in-tree and fixed the remaining CODING.md details/details wording mismatch.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Current repo state note before execution: README.md already includes docs/RUNTIME_UI_VERIFICATION.md and docs/user-guide/key-bindings.md already marks backspace/ctrl+u as built-in/non-configurable. Remaining actionable item is the CODING.md wording mismatch (internal/mode/details vs 'detail' in descriptive text).

title: CODING.md has three copies of the quality gate command block internally
description: Within CODING.md itself, the go build/go vet/go test command set appears three times: 'Build and Test' section (lines 12-16), 'Recommended local verification before handoff' section (lines 23-28), and 'Quality Gates' section (lines 330-336). This internal duplication makes the file longer and creates a drift risk if any one copy is updated without the others. Suggested fix: keep one authoritative 'Quality Gates' section. The 'Build and Test' section can stay as a quick-reference if desired but should be labeled as a shorthand. The 'Recommended local verification' block is a superset (includes architecture guardrails first) — consolidate it into the Quality Gates section and reference from the Build and Test shorthand.
close_reason: Consolidated CODING.md to a single authoritative quality-gates sequence and removed the duplicate internal command blocks.

title: CHANGE-WORKFLOW.md quality gates section violates topic boundary with CODING.md
description: CHANGE-WORKFLOW.md 'Quality gates for code changes' (lines 24-33) duplicates the exact same go build/go vet/go test block owned by CODING.md, plus adds the RUNTIME_UI_VERIFICATION.md pointer. This violates A3 topic boundaries — CHANGE-WORKFLOW.md owns the change landing workflow, not the definition of quality gates. Evidence: CHANGE-WORKFLOW.md line 16 already correctly says 'run the quality gates from docs/CODING.md' in the local change loop. The standalone section below then contradicts this by re-defining them. Suggested fix: remove the 'Quality gates for code changes' section (lines 24-33) from CHANGE-WORKFLOW.md entirely. The local change loop (line 16) already routes correctly to CODING.md. If the RUNTIME_UI_VERIFICATION pointer is needed, add it as a bullet in the local change loop step 3 (which line 17 already does).
close_reason: CHANGE-WORKFLOW.md now routes quality-gate execution to CODING.md only and no longer redefines that command block.

title: Deduplicate fast deterministic loop command across TESTING.md and RUNTIME_UI_VERIFICATION.md
description: The exact same long go test command (with 4 test name patterns and 3 package paths) appears identically in TESTING.md line 57 and RUNTIME_UI_VERIFICATION.md line 10. If test names change, both files must be updated or they drift. TESTING.md section 'Fast deterministic UI verification loop' and RUNTIME_UI_VERIFICATION.md section '1) Fast deterministic automated loop' are the locations. Suggested fix: RUNTIME_UI_VERIFICATION.md is the operator runbook and should own the concrete command. TESTING.md should reference it with 'See docs/RUNTIME_UI_VERIFICATION.md for the fast deterministic scenario command' instead of duplicating it. TESTING.md already has the pointer on line 62 — remove the duplicated command block above it.
close_reason: Removed the duplicated fast deterministic scenario command from TESTING.md and kept the runbook as the command owner.

title: Deduplicate quality gate commands across canonical docs
description: CHANGE-WORKFLOW.md section 'Quality gates for code changes' (lines 24-31) fully duplicates the go build/go vet/go test command block from CODING.md. TESTING.md (lines 40-44) also repeats it. RELEASING.md (lines 31-37) repeats it again. CODING.md itself has the same block three times (Build and Test, Recommended local verification, Quality Gates). This violates topic boundary rule A3: CODING.md owns quality gates, other docs should reference it. OVERVIEW.md does this correctly already by not repeating commands. Suggested fix: keep the authoritative quality gate block in CODING.md once. In CHANGE-WORKFLOW.md, TESTING.md, and RELEASING.md, replace duplicated blocks with a one-liner like 'Run the quality gates from docs/CODING.md' (which RELEASING.md already does on line 31 — then contradicts by repeating the block). Within CODING.md, consolidate the three copies into one authoritative section.
close_reason: Made CODING.md the sole quality-gates command authority and replaced duplicate blocks in TESTING.md and RELEASING.md with references.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Consolidation note: this task, beads-workbench-zlw, and beads-workbench-705 are all facets of the same quality-gate duplication problem. They can be addressed in a single change: (1) consolidate CODING.md internal copies, (2) make CHANGE-WORKFLOW.md reference CODING.md instead of repeating, (3) make TESTING.md and RELEASING.md reference instead of repeating. Suggested approach: fix beads-workbench-705 first (CODING.md internal), then beads-workbench-zlw and this one together.

title: Make the right metadata panel support direct field actions
description:
## Description
Turn the right metadata column into an actionable panel for lightweight edits instead of a mostly passive information rail.

## Resolved Scope
- Status editing is the required first workflow.
- Priority editing is out of scope for the first pass.
- Pressing Enter on the status field opens a dialog-based status editing flow. Reuse an existing dialog pattern if one already fits; otherwise create a dedicated status dialog.

## Instructions
1. Implement status as the first editable metadata field in the right panel.
2. Use an existing dialog pattern if the codebase already has a suitable one for this style of field editing; otherwise create a dedicated status dialog.
3. Keep priority editing out of scope for this first pass.
4. Integrate the dialog with the panel/focus model produced by the layout and browser tasks.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Status is the first editable metadata field
- [ ] Pressing Enter on status opens a dialog-based status editing workflow
- [ ] Existing dialog patterns are reused when appropriate, otherwise a dedicated status dialog is added
- [ ] Priority remains out of scope for the first pass
close_reason: Implemented actionable metadata status editing: Enter on status opens status dialog, wired to update issue status with tests for dialog open and interaction wiring; priority edits remain out of scope.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Missing dependency on .1 and .2. What: Task .3 has no dependency on .1 or .2, but the metadata panel editing interaction will need to operate within the panel layout those tasks produce. The right metadata panel's sizing, focus model, and key handling all depend on the shared layout contract (.1) and the panel interaction model (.2). Why: If .3 is implemented before .1/.2, the metadata editing UI will be built against the current ad-hoc layout and will need to be reworked when the panel model lands. Suggested action: Add beads-workbench-9dn.1 and beads-workbench-9dn.2 as dependencies for .3.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: User already resolved the primary open question. What: The open questions ask 'Which fields are editable in v1' and 'Does Enter open an inline picker, a compact modal, or a command-style chooser?' The user explicitly stated: 'status editing definitely desired first; priority may be a later extension, but status is the minimum useful workflow.' The interaction style (inline picker vs modal) is still open. Why: Half of this discussion task's blocking questions are already answered, making it partially executable. The remaining interaction-style question is genuinely open and should not block defining the scope. Suggested action: Update instructions to reflect that v1 scope is status-only, note that the interaction-style question (inline picker vs modal vs command chooser) is the sole remaining design decision, and unblock the task for implementation once the .1/.2 layout contract is landed.

title: Related links missing in detail rail with real bd show output
description: Acceptance verification found that the shipped left related-issues rail does not surface actual related links from the real bd CLI. In a seeded fixture repo, running 'bd link bwf-2 bwf-3 --type related' followed by 'bd show bwf-2 --json' produced no top-level 'related' array; instead the related link appeared inside 'dependencies' with dependency_type='related'. The current gateway show parser only reads primary['related'], so IssueDetail.Related stays empty and the detail rail/inline related section cannot render real related links.
acceptance_criteria:
- Gateway show parsing populates IssueDetail.Related from the actual bd CLI output shape used for related links
- Built binary runtime verification shows a Related group/item after creating a real related link with bd link --type related
- Add regression coverage against the observed real bd show JSON shape so the parser cannot silently drop related links again
close_reason: Fixed show parser to map related links from dependencies entries with dependency_type=related; added regression tests and embedded-fixture runtime verification.

title: Refresh architecture docs for the simplified package boundaries
description:
## Description
After the code refactor, the docs must match the resulting ownership model and package responsibilities.

## Instructions
1. Update `docs/CODING.md` package layout and core architectural rules to match the final app, mode, gateway, and dashboard boundaries.
2. Update `project-plan/ARCHITECTURE.md` to capture the simplified UI ownership model and the typed CLI gateway mapping strategy.
3. Refresh any README notes that would otherwise describe outdated package behavior.
4. Update `docs/TESTING.md` if the refactor changes test harness patterns, fake usage, scenario coverage expectations, or golden-file refresh workflow.
5. Keep the docs concise and consistent with the architecture guardrails.

## Files to Modify
- `docs/CODING.md` — refresh package layout and rules
- `project-plan/ARCHITECTURE.md` — refresh architecture narrative
- `README.md` — update only if package behavior references become stale
- `docs/TESTING.md` — update only if test harness conventions change during the refactor

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Documentation describes the actual package boundaries after the refactor
- [ ] No stale references remain to the removed generic controller approach or generic map based gateway decoding
- [ ] `docs/TESTING.md` is updated if implementation tasks changed test harness expectations or golden-file workflow
- [ ] Documentation remains aligned with current guardrails and quality gates
close_reason: Updated CODING and ARCHITECTURE docs for app/mode ownership, event-driven selection sync, typed gateway decoding, and dashboard definition validation; README/TESTING verified current.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review finding — docs task is straightforward but has a hidden risk: it depends on all four implementation tasks, so it is on the critical path for epic closure. If any implementation task is delayed or reworked, wz9.5 and wz9.6 both block. This is the correct dependency structure — just noting that if the plan slips, docs are the first thing that will tempt someone to skip. Why: docs skipped after refactor = immediately stale architecture docs = worse than no docs. Suggested action: none, the dependency chain is correct. Just keep discipline.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review finding — TESTING.md not listed. What: docs/CODING.md references docs/TESTING.md for testing strategy. If wz9.1 and wz9.2 change how tests work (new fake patterns, new scenario tests, changed golden file conventions), TESTING.md may need updates too. Why: incomplete docs refresh defeats the purpose. Suggested action: add docs/TESTING.md to the files-to-modify list with a conditional note: 'update if test harness patterns change during implementation tasks.'

title: Lock down dashboard provider contract for multiple providers
description:
## Description
A second dashboard provider is expected soon, so the dashboard boundary should be explicit, validated, and narrow before that work lands.

## Instructions
1. Add a standalone validation entry point in `internal/dashboard` (for example `ValidateDefinitions`) that validates provider output before the board uses it. Do not put validation logic on `Provider` and do not bury it inside `internal/mode/board`.
2. Define and enforce the minimum invalid states the board must reject: provider returns zero definitions; dashboard definition has empty ID or Title; dashboard has zero sections; section has empty ID or Title; section uses an unsupported `QueryType`.
3. While tightening `internal/dashboard/definition.go`, make the query contract explicit enough that invalid or unsupported query configurations are detectable without inventing new machinery. If exact payload-shape validation is not representable with the current struct shape, document and enforce the narrower supported rule instead of guessing.
4. Update `internal/mode/board` to validate definitions before use and to fail locally and predictably on invalid provider output, without adding plugin machinery or arbitrary query execution.
5. Add tests for empty, invalid, and valid multi-dashboard provider outputs.
6. Keep built-in definitions simple and aligned only to currently supported gateway query types.

## Files to Modify
- `internal/dashboard/definition.go` — clarify contract and validation entry points
- `internal/dashboard/*.go` — add standalone validation helpers and tests as needed
- `internal/dashboard/builtin_provider_test.go` — extend built-in provider coverage
- `internal/mode/board/model.go` — validate provider output before use
- `internal/mode/board/model_test.go` — add board-side contract coverage

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `internal/dashboard` exposes a standalone validated contract for dashboard definitions
- [ ] Board loading fails predictably for zero-dashboard, empty-id/title, zero-section, and unsupported-query-type cases
- [ ] Built-in provider behavior remains covered and unchanged for valid definitions
- [ ] No plugin registry, BQL surface, or arbitrary query execution is added
close_reason: Implemented standalone dashboard definition validation, enforced board-side contract checks, and added invalid/valid provider output coverage.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review finding — 'validation' scope is vague without concrete invalid states. What: instructions say 'add validation or contract helpers defining minimum valid dashboard/section shape' and 'add tests for empty, invalid, and valid multi-dashboard provider outputs'. But the task does not enumerate what invalid means. Why: the tasker will have to invent the invalid cases. Looking at definition.go, the concrete failure modes are: (1) Definition with empty ID or Title, (2) Section with empty ID/Title, (3) Section with Query.Type that does not match any populated query payload, (4) Section with unsupported QueryType, (5) Definition with zero sections, (6) Provider returning zero definitions. Some of these already fail implicitly in runSectionQuery (line 388 in board/model.go: 'unsupported dashboard query type'). Suggested action: enumerate the invalid states in the instructions so the tasker builds the right validation and the right tests.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review finding — missing dependency on wz9.3 may create a consistency gap. What: wz9.4 does not depend on wz9.3, but the dashboard Query struct (definition.go line 37-45) maps directly to gateway query types. If wz9.3 changes the gateway query types or adds new ones (e.g., search-based sections), the dashboard contract should reflect that. Why: in practice this is probably fine because wz9.3 only changes internal decoding, not the BeadsGateway interface (acceptance criterion: 'BeadsGateway interface remains unchanged'). But if wz9.3 accidentally surfaces new query capabilities, wz9.4 would need to know. Suggested action: no hard dependency needed — the BeadsGateway interface stability guarantee in wz9.3 makes this safe. Just noting for awareness.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review question — where does validation execute? What: the instructions say to add validation helpers and have board/model.go consume validated definitions. But it is not clear whether validation should happen (A) inside the Provider itself (Validate on return), (B) in a separate Validate() function called by the board before use, or (C) in the board loader when it receives definitions. Why: this affects error reporting and the contract boundary. If the provider validates, a file-backed provider must also validate — good, but couples Provider to validation rules. If the board validates, each consumer re-validates — redundant. Option B is cleanest: provider returns, board calls Validate(defs) before use. Suggested action: specify in instructions that validation should be a standalone function in internal/dashboard (not on Provider, not in board) so both built-in and future file-backed providers get the same gate.

title: Missing duration in compact detail metadata rail
description: Verification found that the refined metadata rail renders creator, closed timestamp, and close reason, but never renders duration for closed issues. The epic decisions and target layout explicitly require Duration in the time metadata group when created and closed timestamps are available.
acceptance_criteria:
- Closed issue detail renders a Duration line in the metadata rail when both created and closed timestamps are present
- Duration is omitted when either timestamp is unavailable
- Detail tests/goldens cover the rendered duration output
close_reason: Implemented duration rendering in metadata rail with test and golden coverage for closed issues in compact/detail views.

title: Redesign metadata rail as grouped 34-column inspector
description:
## Description
Redesign the detail metadata rail as a compact grouped inspector instead of a loose flat key/value list. The goal is denser scanability and better visual hierarchy while staying within Beads Workbench styling.

## Decisions
- Rail width is fixed at 34 columns
- Divider lines separate metadata groups
- Labels render in their own section, one per line
- Close metadata appears only when available
- Counts remain in the right rail; navigable related issue lists stay out of scope for this task

## Instructions
1. Refactor metadata rendering to use grouped sections with divider lines.
2. Keep core fields near the top: Type, Priority, Status.
3. Add ownership, time, close metadata, labels, and counts sections when populated.
4. Render labels one per line with wrapping or truncation that respects the 34-column rail.
5. Preserve the existing two-column detail layout and single-column fallback.
6. Update tests and goldens to lock the new density and grouping behavior.

## Files to Modify
- internal/ui/details/details.go
- internal/ui/details/metadata.go
- internal/ui/details/details_test.go
- internal/ui/details/metadata_test.go
- internal/ui/details/testdata/*
- internal/ui/search/testdata/*

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Right rail uses a fixed 34-column width
- [ ] Divider lines separate metadata groups
- [ ] Labels render as a dedicated one-per-line section
- [ ] Creator and close metadata render when available
- [ ] Two-column and single-column layouts still pass tests
close_reason: Implemented grouped 34-column metadata rail with divider-separated sections, dedicated label lines, conditional creator/close metadata, and updated detail/search tests plus goldens.

title: Acceptance Review: Refine compact detail metadata rail
description:
## Description
Verify the compact metadata rail refinement before the epic is closed.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Right rail is visibly denser and grouped compared with the first flat rail
- [ ] Fixed 34-column rail is used on wide layouts
- [ ] Labels render as a dedicated section
- [ ] Creator and close metadata render when available
- [ ] Narrow layout fallback still works
close_reason: All criteria verified

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Verifier: FAIL. Grouped 34-column rail, labels section, creator/close metadata, and narrow fallback all verify, but duration is still missing from the metadata rail for closed issues. Evidence: internal/ui/details/metadata.go, internal/ui/details/testdata/full.golden, runtime capture at 120x30 and 100x30. Filed blocker: beads-workbench-an8.4.

title: Refine compact detail metadata rail
description:
## Description
Refine the Beads Workbench detail metadata rail so it feels denser, more structured, and more informative. This follow-up replaces the first flat key/value rail with a compact fixed-width inspector closer to the donor Perles feel without copying it verbatim.

## Decisions
- Right rail uses a fixed width of 34 columns on wide layouts
- Labels render as a dedicated one-per-line section, not a single inline comma-separated value
- Close metadata should be shown when available: closed timestamp, duration, and close reason
- Creator metadata should also be surfaced when available

## Target Layout
- Core section: Type, Priority, Status
- Ownership section: Assignee, Creator
- Time section: Created, Updated, Closed, Duration
- Close section: Close Reason
- Labels section: one label per line
- Counts section: Comments, Blocked by, Blocks, Related

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Metadata rail uses a fixed 34-column layout
- [ ] Rail is grouped with divider lines between sections
- [ ] Labels render as a dedicated section
- [ ] Close metadata and creator render when available
- [ ] Narrow layouts still degrade cleanly
close_reason: all steps complete

title: Extend issue detail model with creator and close metadata
description:
## Description
Extend the issue detail read model and gateway decoding so the metadata rail can display creator, closed timestamp, duration inputs, and close reason when `bd show --json` provides them.

## Decisions
- `owner` should surface as creator metadata, not as the assignee fallback when both fields exist
- Close metadata should be available to the UI when present
- Duration can be computed from created and closed timestamps in the UI if both are available

## Instructions
1. Extend the domain issue detail model with creator and close metadata fields needed by the detail rail.
2. Decode those fields from `bd show --json` without regressing existing assignee behavior.
3. Add tests proving creator and close metadata decode correctly when present and stay empty when absent.
4. Keep list/search summary behavior stable unless additional fields are explicitly needed there.

## Files to Modify
- internal/domain/issues.go
- internal/gateway/beads/read_gateway.go
- internal/gateway/beads/read_gateway_test.go

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Domain model exposes creator and close metadata needed by the detail rail
- [ ] Gateway decoding fills creator and close metadata when available
- [ ] Assignee precedence remains correct
- [ ] Tests cover present and absent metadata cases
close_reason: Implemented issue detail creator/close metadata decoding and added present/absent coverage tests while preserving assignee precedence behavior.

title: Refine detail metadata rail density and field coverage
description:
## Description
The first pass of the right metadata rail is functionally correct, but the current UX is too loose and text-heavy compared with the donor Perles detail view. The next iteration should make the rail denser, more structured, and more informative without copying Perles verbatim.

## Discussion Outcome
- Use a fixed metadata rail width of 34 columns
- Render labels as a dedicated one-per-line section
- Add support for creator, closed timestamp, duration, and close reason
- Keep dependency information as compact counts in the right rail
- Use grouped metadata sections with divider lines instead of one flat field list

## Agreed Target Sections
- Core: Type, Priority, Status
- Ownership: Assignee, Creator
- Time: Created, Updated, Closed, Duration
- Close: Close Reason
- Labels: one label per line
- Counts: Comments, Blocked by, Blocks, Related

## Follow-up Tracking
Implementation follow-up is now tracked in the new epic:
- Refine compact detail metadata rail

## Acceptance Criteria
- [x] Proposed rail layout is denser and grouped, not a flat field list
- [x] Proposed field set distinguishes always-visible and optional metadata
- [x] Required gateway or domain follow-up work is identified
- [x] Follow-up implementation tasks can be created without ambiguity
close_reason: Discussion resolved: fixed 34-column grouped metadata rail approved; labels move to one-per-line section; creator and close metadata will be added in follow-up epic beads-workbench-an8.

title: Acceptance Review: Structured issue inspector right rail
description:
## Description
Verify the right rail inspector outcome before the epic is closed.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Metadata is presented in a dedicated right rail on wide terminals
- [ ] Main content remains readable and scrollable
- [ ] Narrow terminal fallback works
- [ ] Left related issues navigation is still out of scope
close_reason: All criteria verified

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter

Artifacts: /tmp/beads-workbench-xxd3-verify-svu9yiic/artifacts. Next: fix assignee decoding and rerun acceptance.

title: Structured issue inspector right rail
description:
## Description
Replace the current linear issue detail presentation with a structured inspector that keeps rich content in the main pane and issue metadata in a dedicated right rail. This epic is about viewing only. The left related issues rail is out of scope here and tracked separately.

## Goals
- Improve scanability of issue metadata
- Keep main content focused on description and notes
- Preserve narrow terminal fallback

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Wide terminals show a structured right metadata rail
- [ ] Right rail covers status, type, priority, assignee, labels, and timestamps
- [ ] Narrow terminals fall back cleanly
