A shared toSearchResultPage(summaries, total) helper would collapse both.

title: Tracker hygiene: beads-workbench-b9u is missing reproduction steps
description:
## Description
`bd lint` reports that `beads-workbench-b9u` is missing the required `## Steps to Reproduce` section, so the ticket is not fully execution-ready by repository tracker conventions.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Run `bd lint` from the repository root.
2. Observe the warning for `beads-workbench-b9u`.

## Why
The bug itself is structurally relevant to this epic, so its ticket should be complete and reproducible for future verification or follow-up work.

## Scope
- Add a concise `## Steps to Reproduce` section to `beads-workbench-b9u` that captures the current board/search rendering mismatch.
acceptance_criteria:
- `bd lint` no longer reports a missing `## Steps to Reproduce` section for `beads-workbench-b9u`.
- The added steps are concrete enough for a tasker or verifier to reproduce the mismatch without guessing.
close_reason: Added concrete reproduction steps to beads-workbench-b9u

title: drainCmd infinite loop risk with self-referencing BatchMsg
description:
In internal/testing/ui/scenarios.go, drainCmd (lines 84-108) recursively flattens BatchMsg commands. If a Cmd returns a BatchMsg that contains itself (or creates a cycle), this loops forever with no guard. In production Bubble Tea, the runtime handles this with its own scheduler. In the test harness, you're manually draining commands synchronously.

This is unlikely to trigger in practice because the project's commands are well-behaved, but the harness has no depth/iteration limit. A single misbehaving Init() or Update() return during development could hang the test suite silently.

Suggested action: add a max-iteration guard (e.g., 1000 messages) with a clear panic message. This is cheap insurance and makes debugging easier when it does happen.
close_reason: Noted — low risk, tracked as P4 suggestion

title: Config: syncEditorLauncher deep-copies all definitions just to update one command field
description: In load.go:276-292, syncEditorLauncher creates a full deep copy of every launcher definition (cloning Args, Env slices) just to set the editor launcher's Command field. This runs on every config load. The merge() function already copies definitions. syncEditorLauncher could mutate in place after merge has created the working copy, avoiding redundant allocations. Alternatively, the editor-launcher sync logic could be folded into merge() itself since they always run together (line 214 immediately after editor override). Suggested action: move editor-command sync into merge() and operate on the already-copied slice. File: internal/config/load.go:276-292, 214
close_reason: Minor optimization — defer

title: scenarios_controller.go duplicates applyCmd logic — extract generic helper
description:
scenarios_controller.go (applyControllerCmd, lines 28-41) is a near-copy of scenarios.go (applyCmd, lines 69-82). The only difference is the receiver type: tea.Model vs mode.Controller. Both share the same drainCmd. This is a textbook case for a Go generic function:

func applyCommands[T interface{ Update(tea.Msg) (T, tea.Cmd) }](model T, cmd tea.Cmd) T

This eliminates the duplication and makes it impossible for the two to drift. If generics are not desired (Go 1.18+ is required), at minimum add a comment linking the two implementations to prevent them from diverging independently. The Controller variant has no test file (scenarios_controller_test.go does not exist), which increases the drift risk.
close_reason: Covered by beads-workbench-qyz (test helpers)

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Note: the generic approach requires the Controller interface to return (Controller, tea.Cmd) from Update, which it does. However, Go generics can't directly express 'T where T.Update returns (T, tea.Cmd)' due to the self-referential constraint limitation. The practical fix may be a shared function that takes an update func and drainCmd, rather than true generics. Either way, linking the two implementations with a comment is the minimum action.

title: Fakes self-tests are tautological — they test the test double, not behavior
description:
The 4 fake test files (beads_gateway_test.go, launcher_test.go, editor_test.go, process_runner_test.go) test that the fakes return what you configure them to return. This is tautological: you set a field, call a method, and assert the field came back. These tests verify the implementation of trivial struct access, not meaningful behavior. The compile-time interface assertions (var _ Interface = (*Fake)(nil)) already guarantee the fakes satisfy their contracts — that's the valuable check. The rest is noise that must be maintained when interfaces change.

Consider: if these tests were deleted, what bug could slip through? None — any interface mismatch is caught by the compile-time assertion, and any behavioral regression in the fake would surface in the 70+ production tests that use them. The fakes are too simple to have bugs.

Suggested action: delete the 4 fake test files. The compile-time interface assertions are sufficient. If a fake becomes complex enough to need its own tests (e.g., stateful behavior, conditional logic), add tests at that point.
close_reason: Noted — tautological but low cost, keeping for now

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Counterpoint: TESTING.md (line 12) explicitly lists internal/testing/fakes/*_test.go as unit test examples. Deleting them would require updating the testing strategy doc. The tests do serve as executable documentation of the fake API. This is P4/backlog — the cost of keeping them is low (they're small and never fail spuriously), the cleanup value is marginal.

title: Config: keybinding context strings should use typed constants instead of bare strings
description: In keybindings.go:12-17, context identifiers (ShellContext, BoardContext, etc.) are untyped string constants. They're used as map keys in KeyBindings struct (lines 71-77), in ResolveKeyBindings, Match(), Keys(), Primary(), and throughout load.go. Using bare strings means typos like Match('shel', ...) compile and silently return false. Suggested action: define type BindingContext string and use it for the context constants and as the parameter type for Match/Keys/Primary/DisplayPrimary/etc. This catches typos at compile time without changing runtime behavior. File: internal/config/keybindings.go:12-17
close_reason: Covered by beads-workbench-dsd (Go conventions — optional)

title: Config: DisplayKeyName calls canonicalKeyName twice — once externally, once in the switch
description: In keybindings.go:390-397, DisplayKeyName calls canonicalKeyName(key) in the switch condition and then again in the default return. This is a minor inefficiency but more importantly obscures intent — the function should canonicalize once, then format. Suggested action: assign canon := canonicalKeyName(key) once and use it in both the switch and default return. File: internal/config/keybindings.go:390-397
close_reason: Minor — double call is cheap, defer

title: Config: MergeKeyBindings has repetitive per-context merge blocks — extract helper
description: In keybindings.go:173-204, MergeKeyBindings repeats the same 3-line pattern (nil check + range + cloneStringSlice) five times, once per context (Shell, Board, Search, Detail, Modal). This is a maintenance burden — adding a new context requires copying the block. The same pattern appears in sanitizeOverride (load.go:128-148). Suggested action: extract a helper like mergeBindingMap(base, override map[string][]string) map[string][]string and call it per context. Files: internal/config/keybindings.go:173-204, internal/config/load.go:128-148
close_reason: Covered by beads-workbench-0jc (DRY violations)

title: Editor service_test.go: missing test for ShowIssue gateway failure path
description:
service_test.go tests three error paths: opener failure, no-changes skip, and successful update. But it does not test what happens when the initial `gateway.ShowIssue` call fails (service.go:61-63). This is the first thing EditIssue does and a likely real-world failure (network issue, invalid issue ID, etc.).

The test infrastructure already supports this — `FakeBeadsGateway.SetError(MethodShowIssue, err)` exists and is unused in these tests.

Suggested fix: Add one test that sets a ShowIssue error and verifies EditIssue propagates it without writing temp files or calling the opener.

File: internal/launcher/editor/service_test.go.
close_reason: Covered by beads-workbench-9uk (test improvements)

title: Config: allowedActionsForContext rebuilds maps on every call — should be package-level vars
description: In keybindings.go:399-474, allowedActionsForContext allocates a new map every time it is called. It is called from: (1) package-level var initialization (allowedMappingKeys, allowedSequenceMappingKeys), (2) sanitizeOverride, (3) buildContextBindings — so it runs multiple times per config load. The maps are static and never mutated. Currently this wastes allocations and makes the code longer than necessary. The same function body is also a 70+ line switch that repeats the exact constant slices already used in DefaultKeyBindings(). Suggested action: make allowedActionsForContext return from pre-built package-level map[string]map[string]struct{} instead of rebuilding every call, or derive from DefaultKeyBindings() to stay DRY. File: internal/config/keybindings.go:399-474
close_reason: Minor optimization — defer

title: Editor opener_test.go: missing test coverage for ProcessOpener.Open and editorCommand fallback chain
description:
opener_test.go only tests the splitEditorCommand helper (2 cases). There is zero test coverage for:

1. The `editorCommand()` fallback chain: explicit command → $EDITOR env → 'vi' default. This is a documented config behavior (CODING.md lines 97-99) with no verification.
2. The `ProcessOpener.Open` method itself — no test verifies it wires command+args+path correctly or handles exec errors.

The splitEditorCommand tests are fine but they're testing the least interesting part. The fallback chain is where config-related bugs would actually hide.

Suggested fix: Add tests for the fallback chain using t.Setenv to control $EDITOR, and a test that exercises Open with a trivial command (e.g., 'true' or '/bin/echo') to verify the path argument is appended correctly. The Opener interface already exists as a seam for the service layer — the opener itself is the boundary that should have its own coverage.

File: internal/launcher/editor/opener_test.go.
close_reason: Covered by beads-workbench-9uk (test improvements)

title: Config: canonicalKeyName has inconsistent handling of whitespace-only input
description: In keybindings.go:369-388, canonicalKeyName checks for literal space (key == ' ') on line 371 before checking trimmed empty on line 374. If key is multiple spaces ('  '), it falls through to trimmed empty, returns '' — but then isValidKeyName('') returns false. A single space becomes 'space' and is valid. This edge case is unlikely in practice since YAML parsing would normalize whitespace, but the function's contract is confusing: it special-cases one whitespace character but not others. The trimmed variable is set from TrimSpace(key) but the space check uses the untrimmed key — mixing representations in the same function. Suggested action: document that the function expects pre-trimmed input or normalize consistently. Minor, but the mixed logic is a maintenance trap. File: internal/config/keybindings.go:369-388
close_reason: Noted — YAML normalizes input, edge case only

title: Gateway: commandRunner() nil-guard in writes.go is redundant with NewGateway constructor
description: writes.go lines 18-24: commandRunner() checks g == nil || g.runner == nil and returns a GatewayError. But NewCLIGateway (read_gateway.go line 30-36) already ensures runner is never nil by defaulting to NewCommandRunner(RunnerConfig{}). The only way commandRunner() nil-guard triggers is if someone constructs a Gateway{} literal bypassing the constructor — which is an internal package so callers are controlled. Meanwhile, the read methods (ListIssues, ShowIssue, etc.) call g.runner directly without this nil-guard, so the protection is inconsistent. Suggested action: either add the same nil-guard everywhere (read and write paths) or remove it from writes.go since the constructor prevents it. The inconsistency is worse than either choice.
close_reason: Covered by beads-workbench-2t4 (dead code removal)

title: Gateway: write methods don't validate empty issueID unlike ShowIssue
description: writes.go: UpdateIssue (line 72), CloseIssue (line 119), and AddComment (line 140) all accept issueID string but don't validate it's non-empty. ShowIssue (read_gateway.go line 142) correctly validates with ErrorCodeValidationFailed. The write methods will pass an empty string to bd CLI and rely on the CLI to reject it, which produces a less informative error (ErrorCodeCommandFailed instead of ErrorCodeValidationFailed). Suggested action: add the same TrimSpace+validation check from ShowIssue to UpdateIssue, CloseIssue, and AddComment.
close_reason: Covered by beads-workbench-dsd (Go conventions)

title: Gateway: GatewayError.Unwrap returns concrete error, preventing errors.Is chain through Cause
description: errors.go line 44: GatewayError.Unwrap returns e.Cause directly. This is correct for errors.Is/errors.As unwrapping. However, GatewayError is a value type (not pointer receiver), which means errors.As will fail if callers check for *GatewayError instead of GatewayError. The runner_test.go assertGatewayErrorCode helper uses errors.As(err, &gatewayErr) with a value receiver which works, but this is a subtle Go gotcha that could bite future callers. Also, Error() drops the Cause message entirely — if Cause is set, the user-facing error loses the underlying detail. Suggested action: (1) consider including Cause in Error() output (e.g. 'operation: message: cause'), (2) add a comment on GatewayError documenting the value-receiver Unwrap behavior.
close_reason: Covered by beads-workbench-rit (error cause message)

title: Domain: catalog types StatusOption, TypeOption, LabelOption are structurally identical — consider unifying
description: catalog.go: StatusOption, TypeOption, and LabelOption are all {Name string, Description string}. Three identical structs for the same shape. This adds zero type safety because the fields are both plain strings — there is no compiler help from having separate types. The gateway StatusCatalog/TypeCatalog/LabelCatalog methods and their consumers would be simpler with a single CatalogOption type. Counter-argument: if these types are expected to diverge (e.g. TypeOption gains an Icon field), separate types are forward-looking. But today they are dead weight. Suggested action: evaluate whether these are expected to diverge. If not, unify into a single CatalogOption type.
close_reason: Covered by beads-workbench-dsd (Go conventions — optional)
